I must continue betting for value to get paid by hands like Ace-King, Ace-Jack, or Ace-Ten, and to charge any remaining club draws. A bet of 1000 into a 1900 pot (roughly 53% pot) is a strong value bet that leaves Elon with a manageable stack to potentially shove or call on the river if the board doesn't get any scarier.

Facing a button turn bet of 1000 into 2900 (~34% pot) OOP on AcQc9sTh board holding TPTK (Ah pairing Ac) + nut heart flush draw (10 clean outs) after check-call flop line. Flash's aggressive history (frequent c-bets/raises that polarize post-call) includes value (AK/AQ/sets/two-pair/straights) and bluffs/missed draws (suited connectors like JTs, KJs, flush draws turning into semibluffs). My hand crushes his bluffs/draws (~70%+ equity), has ~35% vs nuts (sets/straights), overall strong equity (~45-55% vs polarized range) with blocker to AA/AK. Pot odds excellent (3.9:1 direct + implied vs bluffs). Stack 2775 (~27BB eff) supports call leaving ~18BB for river flexibility: hero-call shoves/draws/value, fold to river overbets if bricked without improving. Raising to 2000 commits ~73% stack OOP vs likely shove from better Ax/monster/draws while folding out his equity-poor bluffs we dominate; check-raise less balanced after prior passive line. Calling exploits his aggression, controls pot, realizes equity, and balances range with previous check-call. Plan: call, evaluate river (bet/flush/river shove improved hands, check-call showdown value, pot control vs aggression). Long-term: profits from villain's high bluff freq per history.

River OOP heads-up pot control with pair of Aces and weak 8 kicker (~18BB effective stack) on scary AcQc9sThTc board (club flush complete, paired Tens, straight possibilities like KJ/J8). Check-call flop/turn line caps my range to Ax (AJ+, AT/AQ/AK/AA blocked somewhat), stubborn draws. Flash's button aggression (c-bet flop, turn bet after check ~1/3 pot) vs history of frequent betting gets value from thin Ax/overpairs/draws and bluffs (missed SC like J8/KJ flush draws, floats, semi-bluffs). Shoving 1775 (~45% pot) polarizes too much: gets called by value (club flushes with KQ/Ax clubs, trips Txx, two-pair AQ/QT/A9/TT sets, str KJ/J8) we lose to (~30% equity), folds weaker Ax/bluffs we beat (~70%+ equity). EV negative as called disproportionately by better. Thin bet risks free showdown or raises. Checking induces bets from his wide polarized range (high bluff freq per history: e.g., Hand 25/49/57 bets into checks, folds to resistance), excellent pot odds if bets 1000-1500 (3.5-4:1+ implied vs bluffs), hero-fold overbets/shoves (stack commitment < pot). Beats bluffs/missed draws/thin Ax AJ-AT, loses nuts but realizes equity/exploits long-term aggression balancing prior passive line. Plan: check, call 25-40% pot bets with blocker-heavy TPTK, fold >50% pot.

Flash has improved to a powerhouse hand: Queens full of Tens (QQQTT). On this board, a full house is exceptionally strong, only losing to pocket Aces (unlikely since Elon did not 3-bet pre-flop) or pocket Tens (quads). While the board is coordinated with potential straights and flushes, Flash's full house beats all of them. Elon has checked the river, and with only 1,775 chips remaining in his stack compared to a 3,900 chip pot, he is highly likely to call an all-in bet with any Ace, a straight, or a flush. This is a mandatory value bet to extract the remainder of Elon's stack.

River facing all-in call of 1775 (~31% pot) into 5675 OOP with TPTK (Ah pairing Ac, weak 8 kicker) and Ah blocker to AA/AK/AQ on scary AcQc9sThTc board (club flush complete, paired Tens for possible sets/trips QT/AT/TT). Flash's aggressive button range (wide preflop open, 3-barrel line: flop c-bet 500/1400, turn 1000/2900, river 1775/5675) polarizes to value (club flushes KcJc+/AcXc, two-pair AQ/A10/Q10, trips TT/A10/QT, sets AA/QQ/99 unlikely from range) or bluffs (missed draws like spade flush J8s/KJs, heart draws KhJh/QJh, overpairs AJ+/KJ floating). History shows Flash frequently c-bets/turn bets into checks and barrels for value/thin value/bluffs (e.g., Hands 25,49,57,18 aggressive lines folding to resistance but pushing weak). Prior check-check-call-call line caps me to medium Ax/stubborn draws but blocks top of villain value; ~45-55% equity vs polarized range (crushes bluffs ~80%, ~30% vs nuts), pot odds 3.2:1 direct superior to break-even (~26%). Short stack (18BB pre-call, all-in now) committed postflop calls; folding leaks EV vs high bluff freq (exploits aggression long-term), raising impossible. Consistent with pre-river plan (check-call 25-40% pot bets), call realizes equity, balances range, maximizes stack vs over-aggressive villain.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with deep stacks (~263bb). T6 offsuit is marginal but perfectly playable heads-up — it has top-pair potential, straight connectivity and plenty of postflop playability. I want initiative and fold equity, so I’ll open-raise to the 2x BB default (200 total) rather than limp. Plan: if called, c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry overcard boards; fold or proceed cautiously to significant 3-bets (hand has limited equity vs a strong 3-bet range).

I am holding Jh Qh in the Big Blind. In a heads-up or short-handed scenario, this is a very strong hand. Minni opened with a small 2x raise to 200. I will 3-bet to 600 to build value and take initiative in the hand, as my hand performs well against her button-opening range.

Flash 3-bet to 600 after my 200 open (pot 800). T6o is marginal but heads-up in-position it has decent playability and enough equity to justify a call here. Calling costs +400 (600 total) and gives ~3:1 pot odds (~25% break-even) plus huge implied postflop depth — I can see a flop in position, c-bet favorable textures, and fold to heavy resistance when I miss. I will not 4-bet bluff this hand (too many of Flash's 3-bet combos dominate T6o) and I will fold to a large shove preflop. Therefore I call to realize equity and use position to play the postflop carefully.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Jh Qh and was called by Minni. The flop is 4c 8h 5h, giving me two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. While this board texture can hit a caller's range (connectors and middle pairs), as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet. A bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 pot is standard here to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately, while keeping my own risk managed if I'm raised. I will bet 450 into the 1200 pot.

Call 450. Flop (4c 8h 5h) is pretty dry but Flash's small continuation bet (450 into ~1200) is the type of c-bet I can profitably call in position. Pot is 1650, calling 450 requires ~21.4% equity (450/2100). My T6o has reasonable improvement chances: four 7s to make a 4-5-6-7-8 straight plus pairing tens or sixes — roughly ~10 naive outs (~40% by river using the rule of 4). With deep stacks I also have good implied odds if I hit. Raising as a bluff here is too risky — Flash 3-bet preflop and many of his combos will dominate me or can shove; I want to keep the pot controllable and realize equity in position. Plan: call, see turn; if I pair or complete the straight I will extract value, if I miss I’ll control the pot and fold to heavy aggression (or re-evaluate against a very safe turn card).

The turn 7s is a highly coordinated card that favors the caller's range more than mine. It completes potential straights for hands like 69 or any hand containing a 6 (like 56, 67, 68). Since I have no made hand and my overcards are vulnerable on such a connected board, continuing to bet (double-barreling) is risky as it's unlikely to fold out a pair or a straight draw. Checking is the most prudent play to see how Minni reacts and to potentially realize equity if she checks behind.

Turn brings 7s and gives me a 5-card straight: board 4c-8h-5h-7s with my 6s-Td = 4-5-6-7-8. This is a very strong value hand heads-up, though not absolutely invulnerable (villain could have rare 6+9 combos or another 6 that ties). Flash’s line (3-bet pre, small c-bet on the flop, then check the turn) looks like a lot of medium-strength hands (8x, 5x, some overcards and missed draws) that will call a reasonably sized value bet. I want to extract from those hands while leaving room to respond to aggression, so I’ll bet about half-to-two-thirds pot. Pot is 2,100 — I’ll bet 1,200 to charge pairs and weaker holdings and set up a river decision. If Flash raises very large I’ll re-evaluate (but with a made straight I will be inclined to call most reasonable raises).

On the turn, the board has become significantly more coordinated with the 7s, which completes many potential straights in Minni's calling range (any hand with a 6). However, with the Jh and Qh in my hand and two hearts already on the board (8h, 5h), I have a flush draw along with two overcards. Minni's bet of 1200 into a 2100 pot gives me pot odds of approximately 2.75 to 1. With roughly 15 outs (9 hearts, 3 Queens, 3 Jacks), my equity is approximately 30-32%, which is sufficient to justify a call based on direct pot odds and potential implied odds if I hit my flush on the river. Raising would be unwise against a range that now includes many made straights and sets.

On the river, the board has paired with a 5d, and I have missed my flush draw and overcards, leaving me with only Queen high. Given that the board is highly coordinated (potential straights with any 6) and Minni showed aggression on the turn, I have no showdown value. Leading out with a bet is unlikely to fold out any made hands that Minni called with or bet on the turn. Therefore, I will check and most likely fold if she bets, as I cannot justify a bluff or a call with my current hand.

I have 6s Td and made the 4-5-6-7-8 straight on the turn (board: 4c 8h 5h 7s 5d). The river paired the 5, which creates the possibility of full houses/quads (pocket 5s, or pocket pairs that turned trips earlier like 8s/7s/4s), but those combos are relatively rare. Flash’s line (3-bet pre, small c-bet on the flop, check then call on the turn) looks like a lot of medium-strength hands (8x, 7x, underpairs, some missed draws) that will call a modest value bet but not many full-house combos. Pot is 4500 and Flash checked the river — I want to extract value from worse pairs and showdown-calling hands while keeping the bet small enough to get called. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (1500). If Flash check-raises big (a large overbet/all-in) that most likely represents a full house/quads and I’ll fold; if he makes a small/medium raise I’ll re-evaluate but am willing to call reasonable raises.

I missed my flush and overcards, leaving me with only Queen high on a highly coordinated board (4-8-5-7-5). While the pot odds are attractive (1500 to call into a total pot of 7500, requiring 20% equity), my hand has no showdown value. Minni's line of calling a 3-bet and then betting both the turn and river strongly suggests a made hand, likely a straight, trips, or at the very least a pair that beats Queen high. Even some of her potential bluffs, like Ace-high flush draws, would still beat me. Folding is the only disciplined play here.

We are currently in a heads-up situation against Minni. In heads-up play, the button (Small Blind) should be opening a very wide range of hands, typically between 70% and 100%. Jack-Seven offsuit is well within the top 50% of starting hands and is a standard opening raise. Minni has a significant chip lead, so I want to maintain aggression while being mindful of the stack disparity. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to take control of the hand pre-flop.

Opponent (button/SB) raised to 300 — pot is 400 and I need to add 200 to call, so calling would put 300 total in. Calling costs 200 into 400 (200/(400+200)=33% break-even equity), which is cheap heads-up. My hand (Td4s) is marginal but playable in a heads-up defense: it can hit a ten or have backdoor draws. 3-betting as a bluff with T4o is unattractive — I have poor blockers and would be out of position postflop — so I won’t inflate the pot. With deep stacks (I’m ~285bb, opponent ~111bb) the correct play is to defend and see a flop, then proceed cautiously (check/fold to heavy aggression on hostile boards, continue with top pair or strong draws). Call to 300.

I defended preflop and called the SB’s flop bet because it was cheap in a heads-up spot and T4o has some backdoor/spade showdown potential. On 5♠8♦2♣ I have no pair and only very weak backdoor equity, so I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position. Pot control is the priority — check and see a turn card. Plan: fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a real draw; consider calling a very small turn stab but won’t commit with air.

On this very dry, disconnected board (5s 8d 2c), my Jack-high still has a good chance of being the best hand or at least can easily force a fold from many of Minni's air hands. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet of 50% pot is standard here to take down the pot immediately and deny equity to hands like random overcards or low suited connectors that missed. Minni's check is expected, and a 300-chip bet maintains my aggression in this heads-up match.

Call 300. The bet (300 into a 600 preflop pot, now 900 total) gives me ~25% break-even pot odds — cheap enough to continue heads-up. T♦4♠ has essentially six clean outs to pair (≈24% to hit by the river) and some additional fold equity vs a wide C-bet range, so calling one street to realize equity is correct. Raising as a bluff is unattractive: I’m out of position, have poor blockers and would bloat the pot with air. Plan: call to see a turn card; fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a real draw, and if I do pair the Ten I’ll start playing for value.

I have top pair (T♥ on board with my Td) but a very weak 4-kicker. Heads-up versus Flash — who opened pre and barreled the flop — his range is wide and contains lots of worse hands (A/K/Q highs, weaker pairs, bluffs). There are virtually no meaningful draws on 5♠-8♦-2♣-T♥, so betting for thin value and protection is correct. I’ll make a value bet about half the pot to extract calls from worse hands while keeping the pot controllable given my weak kicker and his aggression. If he raises small I can call and reevaluate on the river; I’ll respect a large raise/shove and fold unless the sizing is clearly for value of worse hands.

On the turn, Minni leads out with a bet of 600 into a pot of 1200. The board is 5s-8d-2c-Th. My hand (Js 7c) has no real equity and no draw. Minni's lead suggests she either connected with the Ten, has a pair of Eights or Fives, or is potentially bluffing. However, without any showdown value or a reasonable draw to improve, calling or raising is not a profitable play. In a heads-up match, I must pick better spots to contest the pot, and continuing here with air against a shown interest from the opponent is a leak.

Heads-up on the button with pocket Aces (As Ah) — the best possible starting hand. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to see the flop, but I want to build the pot and get value from worse hands while denying good odds to speculative holdings. Flash in the BB has ~107 BB effective; I’m much deeper, so I can play postflop and respond to any 3-bet. Standard, balanced sizing in heads-up is ~3x the BB: I’ll raise to 300 total (includes my posted SB) — big enough to extract value and price out some of the widest speculative hands, but not so large as to overcommit or polarize unnecessarily. If Flash 3-bets, I will re-evaluate (likely a call or a sizable 4-bet depending on sizing).

I have Ten-Six suited in the Big Blind. Getting 2-to-1 on a call against a standard opening raise from Minni in a heads-up situation, this hand is too strong to fold. I will call to see a flop and look for diamond draws, straight possibilities, or mid-pair value.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a Six kicker on a very coordinated board of Th-Jc-9h. This board hits many of a button-opening range's hands, including straights, straight draws, and higher pairs. Out of position, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. Leading here would likely only get called by better hands or draws with significant equity. I am looking to play a small pot or potentially catch a bluff.

I hold As Ah — the best preflop hand and an overpair to this flop (T J 9). This board is very connected: many hands in Flash’s cold-call range (KQ, Q8, 87, JT, pocket tens/jacks/nines, etc.) have already made straights or two-pair, and there are heart draw/backdoor possibilities. However Flash also has a lot of worse hands and single overcards that I can get value from and many drawing hands I want to charge. I’ll lead for value and protection with a half-pot bet (300): big enough to charge straight/heart draws and extract from worse pairs/overcards, but small enough to keep the pot manageable if I face a big check-raise. If he jams or check-raises very large I’ll assume a lot of his range beats me and will re-evaluate (often folding to extreme aggression); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously and re-assess on the turn.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a weak kicker on a very coordinated board (Ten-Jack-Nine). Minni's bet of 300 into a 600-chip pot represents a half-pot continuation bet. While the board texture is dangerous and likely hits a significant portion of a button-opening range, folding middle pair to a single bet in a heads-up situation would be overly exploitable. The pot odds of 3-to-1 make a call profitable for now. I intend to see a turn card and will proceed with caution if the board develops to complete straights or flushes, as my hand is unlikely to improve significantly and currently lacks a strong kicker.

The turn pairs the Jack, which is an interesting development. I now hold two pair (Jacks and Tens), but the board is extremely coordinated. Any Queen or Seven completes a straight (KQ, Q8, Q7, 87), and many of those hands are in Minni's opening range. Additionally, because the Jack paired, I am now losing to any Jack in my opponent's hand. My kicker (Six) is also very weak, meaning I lose to most other Tens (AT, KT, QT). Given I am out of position on a very dangerous board, checking is the best play to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. I am not strong enough to bet for value, and I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff.

Heads-up on the button with As Ah on a very connected, now-paired board (Th Jc 9h Js). Flash check-called my flop c-bet, which is a fairly passive line consistent with medium-strength hands (T,9, JT, single J, Kx/Qx), some disguised straights (KQ, Q8) and occasionally trips/sets. My Aces are still ahead of most of his check-call range, but the turn jack increases the risk of trips and doesn’t eliminate made straights. I want to extract value from pairs and overcards, deny equity to any remaining turn/river outs, and keep the pot manageable if he shows heavy strength. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (800 chips): big enough to charge T/9/Kx/Qx and to fold out pure floats, but not so large that I’m forced to commit a huge portion of my stack if he check-raises massively. Plan: if he calls I’ll reevaluate on the river and target more value on safe runouts; if he check-raises a small amount I can call and continue; if he check-raises all-in or shoves very large I’ll give him credit for trips/straights and fold.

The board texture (Th-Jc-9h-Js) is extremely dangerous for my specific hand (Td-6d). While I have two pair, Jacks and Tens, I am losing to any Jack in Minni's range, any straight (KQ, Q8, 87), and I am outkicked by better Tens (AT, KT, QT). The pairing of the Jack on the turn is a particularly bad card because it improves any Jack to trips and makes it less likely that Minni is bluffing with a middle-strength hand. Given that I am out of position and Minni's 800-chip bet represents significant strength on such a coordinated board, a fold is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better spots. I cannot comfortably call another bet on the river if I call here.

In a heads-up situation, Q6s is a strong starting hand on the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a favorable post-flop scenario.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I'm in the big blind, out of position, with A♥5♥. This is a standard defend: A5s has strong heads-up equity (blocks many premium A combos, nut-flush potential and top-pair possibilities). Calling 200 more (300 total commitment including my 100 BB) gives ~2:1 immediate pot odds (need ~33% equity) and keeps his wide opening range intact so I can realize equity postflop. A tiny 3-bet to 500 would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and invite bigger action; folding is too tight. I’ll call and play the flop cautiously, looking to proceed when I hit an Ace/heart or extract value when appropriate.

Out of position with A♥5♥ on 6♥7♦3♠: I have only Ace-high and a weak backdoor heart/five-out potential, so this is a pot‑control situation. Flash’s button opening range is wide and many hands missed this dry low flop; checking lets me realize equity and avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Plan: check and call a single, modest c‑bet (evaluate on the turn), fold to a large raise. If Flash checks back I get a free card to a pair or better and can proceed aggressively if I improve (pair of A/5 or runner‑runner hearts).

On a board of 6h-7d-3s, I have flopped middle pair with a Queen kicker. This is a relatively strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni checked to me after I raised pre-flop. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from weaker hands, protect my equity against overcards, and maintain the initiative.

I’m out of position with A♥5♥ on 6♥7♦3♠. I have Ace‑high, a backdoor heart flush (needs two hearts) and backdoor straight potential (a 4 completes 3‑4‑5‑6‑7). Flash’s small flop stab (300 into a 900 pot ≈ 1/3 pot) is a standard c‑bet from a wide BTN range. Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives me 25% break‑even equity; my chance to improve to at least a pair or better by the river is roughly ~38% from the main outs (3 Aces + 3 fives + 4 fours), plus a small (~5%) chance to hit a runner‑runner flush. Raising here OOP with just Ace‑high is unnecessarily bloating the pot and invites bigger action; folding is too tight given my equity and blocker value. I will call 300 to see the turn and reassess — if I miss and face a large turn barrel I will fold; if I pair the Ace/5 or complete a straight/flush I will look to extract value.

I check. The turn (Js) essentially kills my backdoor heart/flush chance — with the turn out there I can no longer make a five‑card heart flush by the river. I currently only have 6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 Fives), which is about 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river, so my equity to improve is low. Flash checked to me, showing weakness or pot control; OOP I should avoid bloating the pot with a one‑card Ace‑high bluff. Betting here would be a pure bluff that will be called by many pairs and slower Jx hands, and a raise would be wildly aggressive for little equity. I’ll keep the pot small, check to the river and reevaluate: if I pair the Ace/5 I’ll look for a small value bet; if I miss I may consider a targeted river bluff depending on the card and opponent action, but I will fold to significant aggression.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades is an overcard, but it also gives me a flush draw to go along with my pair of sixes. Minni has checked to me again. While I could bet to keep the pressure on, checking back is a safer play that allows me to realize my equity and see the river for free. My hand has decent showdown value as middle pair, and I have many outs to improve (spades, queens, and sixes). If I bet and get raised, I'd be in a difficult position, so pot controlling here with a medium-strength draw is the most strategic approach.

I check. The river (Kc) gives me nothing — I missed my backdoor heart/straight and only have Ace-high (A♥5♥). Flash checked the turn, showing weakness or pot-control; with his wide preflop/flop range many of his holdings are single-pair hands or missed draws. Betting here would be a thin bluff: many hands that beat me will call and a larger bet risks a raise from a hand that already has me crushed. Pot control is the correct play — I’ll check and go to showdown (and fold to any large, polarized river shove).

I have a pair of sixes with a Queen kicker. The board has two overcards (Jack and King) that hit a significant portion of a calling range. Minni has checked to me on the river after we both checked the turn. My hand has moderate showdown value, but it is unlikely to get called by a worse hand if I bet. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown and potentially win the pot without risking a check-raise.

Heads-up on the button (I’m also the small blind). Pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call. Th3c is a weak hand but in heads-up having position and deep stacks makes it playable — I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to Flash’s wide BB defending range. I’ll raise to the standard 2xBB (200 total) to win the pot now or play the flop last; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold (T3o has poor equity vs 3-bets), if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable boards and proceed cautiously on dry or heavily connected boards.

I am in the Big Blind and Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I only need to call 100 more to see the flop, which gives me 3-to-1 pot odds. Although 4s 2s is a weak hand, it is suited and has some potential. Given the favorable odds and the heads-up nature of the current stage, I will call to see the flop.

I am first to act on the flop as the Big Blind in this heads-up hand. The board is Kh 3h 6h, a monotone texture that I have completely missed with 4s 2s. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. Given the lack of any showdown value or potential to improve, checking is the only logical play. If my opponent bets, I will fold.

I raised preflop and have position; on K♥ 3♥ 6♥ with T♥3♣ I have a pair of 3s plus a 4‑card heart flush draw (9 outs). Heads‑up Flash defended a very wide range, so I have both decent showdown equity and fold equity. Checking surrenders initiative and gives free cards to many hands that beat me; betting charges single‑heart overcards and straight/flush draws and gets value from worse pairs and draws. I’ll c‑bet a standard half‑pot to balance fold equity and pot control. If Flash check‑raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large raise against a presumed flopped flush); if he calls I can continue with turn plan based on his action.

I have completely missed the flop with 4s 2s on a monotone Kh 3h 6h board. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. Continuing in this hand against a bet from Minni is not profitable as I have almost zero equity and no reasonable way to win the pot.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Deuce of Clubs and Five of Clubs. While these are suited connectors, they are at the very bottom of the range. Given that the tournament is down to two players and Minni has a significant chip lead and has shown aggressive play in recent hands, I prefer to fold this weak hand rather than risk chips in a marginal spot. I will wait for a better opportunity to engage.

Heads-up on the button with A6s — this is well within a standard opening range. I want initiative and to charge the big blind's wide calling range while keeping the pot-to-stack ratio reasonable. A 3x BB open (300 total) achieves that: it builds the pot with a strong positional hand, reduces the odds for speculative calls, and uses my blocker (ace) to lower the chance Flash has strong aces. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I'll fold (A6s plays poorly vs a polarized 3-bet); if he makes a small 3-bet I can consider calling given position and postflop playability. If he flats, I'll c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value/bluff as appropriate.

In this heads-up scenario, Minni has opened for a 3x raise from the button. While J7o has some raw equity against a wide button opening range, playing it out of position against a significantly larger stack that has shown consistent post-flop aggression is risky. J7o is at the very bottom of a defending range against a 3x sizing. To preserve my stack and avoid marginal situations where I am likely to be outplayed post-flop, I will elect to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or a better positional advantage.

In heads-up play, a suited Jack is a strong starting hand on the button. I want to maintain an aggressive profile to chip away at Minni's lead. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open, to either take the blinds or set up a post-flop play where I have the advantage of position.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). I’m in the big blind with pocket eights (8c8h) — a strong heads-up value hand against a wide opening range. I want to 3-bet to build the pot and get value/isolate rather than just call and give him a cheap flop. Raising to ~3x the open (900 total) is large enough to generate fold equity and extract value from worse hands (Axs, Kx, broadways, smaller pairs) while leaving room to maneuver postflop. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed postflop with aggression on dry boards and pot control on highly coordinated boards; if he 4-bets shove I’ll reassess given his shove range.

In heads-up play, Jh6h is a decent suited hand that has enough playability to continue against a 3-bet, especially since I will have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. The price to call is 600 more into a pot of 1200, giving me 3:1 odds, which is sufficient given the potential to realize equity post-flop.

Pot is 1,800 on a monotone flop Tc 3c 6c. I have 8c8h: an underpair to the Ten but with a four-card club draw (one club in my hand). Flash opened from the SB and called my 3-bet, so his continuing range contains lots of clubs, tens and pocket pairs that legitimately beat my eights, plus many broadways/Ax hands that I currently beat. This is a highly coordinated board where aggression can build a big pot when I am often behind. I want pot control: check to keep the pot manageable, realize my equity with the flush draw and second pair, and fold to extreme turn/river aggression if it appears. If he makes a small-to-medium c-bet I will call to see a turn; if he overbets massively I can fold. Betting here would risk bloating the pot with many hands that dominate me, so I check.

On a monotone board of Tc 3c 6c, I have middle pair with no club. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and has now checked. While my pair of sixes has some showdown value, it is extremely vulnerable to any club or overcard. Betting here doesn't accomplish much for value, as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, avoid a check-raise, and see a turn for free to evaluate how the board develops.

Flop check was pot-control and I’m sticking to that plan on the turn. Board is Tc 3c 6c 3s; I hold 8c8h — an underpair to the Ten but with a four-card club draw (9 club outs to the river, ~19.6% to make a flush). Flash called my 3-bet preflop and checked the flop, so his continuing range contains many clubs, pocket pairs, and broadway/Ax hands. Betting now would bloate the pot out-of-position into lots of hands that legitimately beat me (T, sets, some club combinations) and will get value from few worse hands; checking preserves pot control and lets me realize equity on the river. Plan: check and evaluate river — call small/medium river bets to see showdown when appropriate, fold to extreme overbets unless I hit my flush or villain’s sizing/tells indicate a bluff. Therefore I check to keep the pot manageable and keep my drawing/second-pair equity alive.

I have a pair of sixes on a board that is now paired (3s) and monotone (clubs). Minni 3-bet pre-flop but has checked twice (flop and turn). While it's possible she is giving up with hands like AK or AQ, she could also be trapping with an overpair or a made flush. My hand has some showdown value, but betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control and see the river for free, which is the safest way to realize my current equity.

I have 8c8h and the river (Tc 3c 6c 3s 3h) gives me a full house — eights full of threes. Flash called my 3-bet preflop but was passive (check-check) on flop and turn and checked the river as well, so his continuing range contains a lot of medium pocket pairs and broadway/Ax hands but is less weighted to the tens/quads that beat me. Betting for value is correct — checking surrenders value to worse full houses/pairs. I size modestly to maximize calls from 22–77 and other hands that will call a half-pot but fold to a larger sizing; half the pot (900 into 1800) extracts value without overinvesting against the few combos that beat me. If he raises big I’ll put him on a very narrow, beaten-or-beating range (T/3/quads) and re-evaluate, but for now bet 900.

The board has tripled the Threes, and I have a Six in my hand, giving me a full house (Threes full of Sixes). Minni 3-bet pre-flop but checked the flop and turn, which is a line often taken with high overcards like AK, AQ, or AJ that missed, or perhaps a cautious overpair. On the river, the board trips up, and Minni leads for 900 into a pot of 1800. My full house beats all of her non-pairing overcards (like AK/AQ/AJ) because they only have three-of-a-kind with high kickers, whereas I have a full house. While I lose to any Ten or a pocket pair higher than Sixes (77-AA), the pot odds (3:1) and the presence of many overcard combinations in a standard 3-betting range make this a mandatory call. I will not raise, as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 5♧7♣ is marginal but fine to raise with because I have position and can fold postflop if I miss. I’ll raise to 300 total (about 3x the big blind) to take the initiative, create fold equity, and avoid letting the big blind see a cheap flop for free. The sizing is large enough to deny correct odds to many of BB’s weak hands but not so large that I’m overly committed with a weak holding. If Flash 3‑bets or shoves, I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a large shove); if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or control the pot on dry boards.

I am holding Queen-Four offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 3x the big blind. While standard heads-up strategy suggests defending a wide range, Q4o is very weak and plays poorly out of position. Given Minni's aggression and my current stack size, I will fold this hand to wait for a more favorable spot to commit chips.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with 72o, which is statistically the weakest hand in poker. While position is important in heads-up play, 72o lacks any meaningful post-flop potential or connectivity. Given the opponent's significant chip lead and recent aggression, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity rather than committing more chips with the absolute bottom of my range.

Heads-up on the button with 5c8s — a marginal but playable hand in position. Facing 50 to call, I want to take the initiative and exploit position, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp. A 3x BB sizing (to 300 total) is standard here: it applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable versus Flash’s 8,400 stack, and mixes well with my wider heads-up range. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet with this weak offsuit hand; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on turns.

In a heads-up situation, defending the Big Blind with any King is generally standard, especially when offered 2-to-1 odds on the call. While King-Deuce offsuit is a weak hand, it has enough equity against a wide button opening range to justify seeing a flop, particularly given the stack sizes and the need to defend against Minni's frequent aggression.

On a flop of 6h-2d-4h, I have bottom pair with a King kicker. This is a marginal made hand that is worth a check-call against Minni's likely continuation bet given her aggressive tendencies. I will check to see how she reacts to the board.

I raised preflop and got called; heads-up and in position on a fairly dry 6♥-2♦-4♥ flop. My 5♣8♠ has no pair but has a two-way gutshot (3 or 7) — roughly 8 outs — so some equity versus a wide defending range. Flash checked, showing weakness. A continuation bet here should fold many overcards and small unpaired hands while getting value from draws and worse cards. I’ll make a half‑pot c-bet (300 into the 600 pot) to apply pressure but keep the pot controllable. Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls I’ll reevaluate on the turn and continue only with improvement or clear equity; fold to a large check‑raise/strong raise since this hand is marginal.

In a heads-up match, bottom pair (deuces) with a King kicker is a reasonable holding to defend against a standard continuation bet from an aggressive opponent like Minni. I'm receiving 4:1 pot odds (300 to call for a 1200 total pot), meaning I only need 20% equity to justify a call. Minni's range for betting this flop includes many overcards, flush draws, and straight draws that I am currently ahead of. Raising would likely only get called by better hands, so I will call to keep the pot controlled and see how the turn develops.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds is an overcard that likely connects well with a button opening range. Since I only hold bottom pair (deuces) with a King kicker, my hand is marginal. I will check to the aggressor, Minni, to see how she reacts to the turn card. Betting into her would likely only be called by superior hands, so checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river if she checks behind.

I opened the button with 5♣8♠, c‑bet the dry 6♥‑2♦‑4♥ flop and got called. The Q♦ on the turn is a complete miss for my hand — I only have 8 outs (3s and 7s) to make a straight (~17% to hit the river). Flash checked, showing either weakness or pot control with a made hand (Qx, small pair, or a slow‑played stronger holding). With no improvement and limited equity, I don’t want to inflate the pot or give Flash a cheap opportunity to shove and force me off a marginal bluff. Checking back controls the pot (pot is 1,200 vs his ~7,900 stack), preserves my big stack, and lets me realize my equity or bluff selectively on the river based on his action. If he bets the river small I can call or explore a bluff/raise depending on sizing; I’ll fold to large aggression that represents a strong made hand.

On the river, the board pairs the Four, giving me a two-pair hand (Fours and Deuces) with a King kicker. While I have improved to two pair, it is a very weak two pair. Any Six, Queen, or Four in Minni's range beats me. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a Queen, but she could still have a Six or be planning to check-back Ace-high. By checking, I can reach a cheap showdown and potentially win against her missed draws or weaker holdings that she wouldn't call a bet with. Betting would likely only result in being called by hands that have me beat.

Check. I have 5♣8♠ and missed the board (6♥-2♦-4♥-Q♦-4♠) — no pair, no straight/flush. Flash called my half‑pot flop bet then both of us checked the turn; that line looks like weakness or pot control from a wide calling range (small pairs, 6x/2x, Qx, missed draws). The river pairing the board (a second 4) actually strengthens any 4x holdings (they’d have trips) and keeps many pair hands in a calling range, so my bluff equity is limited. To bluff profitably I’d need a high fold frequency (even a small 400 chip probe needs ~25% folds), and given Flash’s range after calling the flop I don’t trust that frequency enough to risk chips for a tiny pot. Pot is only 1,200 and my stack is deep — preserving chips and avoiding thin bluffs is the better long‑term play. Checking gains showdown when he’s missed and avoids getting raised off a marginal bluff. If he had bet instead I would fold to large sizing and consider a thin call only to very small bets, but here I’ll check and take the safer line.

I am on the button with JTs, a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard procedure is to raise to gain control of the pot and build value. I have 9050 chips, and the blinds are 50/100. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and exerts pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I face a 3-bet.

Flash (SB/Button) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Tc6d — weak but defensible heads‑up. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total commitment from me 300 including my posted BB), so I’m getting roughly 2:1 on a call (~33% equity needed). Versus a likely wide SB opening range this hand has enough equity and postflop playability to defend; a 3‑bet bluff would be thin and unnecessarily bloating the pot against a shorter stack. I’ll call to see a flop, keep options open, and fold or apply pressure based on the flop texture and opponent action.

Flop: Kc 5d 3s — I completely missed with Tc6d (no pair, no realistic draws). Flash opened wide from the button pre and will c-bet a large portion of their range here, so I have to be careful OOP. I’ll check to control the pot and give myself the option to fold to heavy aggression or float against a small c-bet. Plan: if Flash fires a small-to-medium c-bet (around half-pot or less) I’ll call to see a turn and look to take the pot away or improve; if they jam or bet very large I’ll fold (my hand has too little equity). If they check back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only invest further with real improvement (pair, good blocker/river-shaping card) or a clear bluffing spot.

On this dry King-high board (5d Kc 3s), as the pre-flop aggressor, I should follow up with a continuation bet. This board favors my perceived range more than Minni's calling range from the big blind. A half-pot bet is sufficient to put pressure on her air and low pairs, while allowing me to potentially take the pot down immediately.

Flop: Kc 5d 3s — I completely missed with Tc6d. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bets a high percentage of range here; their half‑pot bet (300 into a 600 prebet pot, now 900) is standard. Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds (≈25% equity needed). My hand has very little showdown value but still has some backdoor/runner improvement (pairing a T or 6, and occasional two‑card runouts). Raising OOP as a bluff is too thin and will bloat the pot against a shorter but competent opponent. Folding is too conservative given the cheap price and wide opener range. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn, fold to heavy turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a clear bluffing line.

I have Tc6d on Kc-5d-3s-As. I completely missed and have no meaningful draws or blockers to Ax/Kx. Flash opened wide pre and c-bet the flop, but the turn Ace is a brutal card for my range — betting OOP here as a bluff is too thin because my fold equity is low and I can be forced off by value hands or a check-raise. Pot control is the priority: check behind, realize showdown equity cheaply, and keep the pot small. Plan for the river: if Flash fires a small bet (<= ~half pot) I can consider a call for pot odds or a targeted fold/call decision; fold to large aggression; if he checks the river I can reevaluate bluff/value options only if the runout helps my hand.

The Ace of Spades on the turn is a significant scare card that heavily favors my pre-flop raising range. Having bet the flop to represent a King or better, the Ace allows me to continue the story and put immense pressure on any middle-pair hands Minni might be holding, such as a King or a lower pocket pair. Since my Jack-high currently has no showdown value, but I do have a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway nuts (needing a Queen), a second barrel is the most effective way to take down the pot immediately while maintaining equity if called.

Flash barrels the turn for 750 into a pot that's now 1,950. Calling 750 would make the final pot 2,700, so I need ~27.8% equity to justify a call (750/2,700). My hand (Tc6d) has only 6 outs to pair on the river (3 tens + 3 sixes) → 6/46 ≈ 13.0% equity, well below the required threshold. Even if I hit a pair I often lose to Ax/Kx hands that continue on an A turn. Raising as a bluff OOP is too thin (no A/K blockers and Flash barrels reasonably often), so the +EV play is to preserve my deep stack and fold this turn facing substantial aggression.

Heads-up on the button with QcTh — a strong, playable hand that does well open‑raised in heads‑up play. Facing only 50 to call I want to seize the initiative, build the pot when I’m likely ahead of BB’s defending range, and create fold equity. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300) — large enough to apply pressure and consistent with table sizing while still leaving plenty of postflop maneuvering against Flash’s 9.6k stack. If called I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and re‑evaluate versus aggression; fold or proceed cautiously if I face a large 3‑bet without improvement.

I am holding Ace-Seven of Diamonds (A7s) in the Big Blind. In a heads-up situation, A7s is a premium hand. Minni (the dealer/small blind) has raised to 300, which is standard. Given our stack sizes (I have about 96 big blinds), I want to play this hand aggressively. A7s performs well as a 3-betting hand because it has high card value and great playability on most flops. Raising here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately if Minni is opening a wide range of hands. I will raise to 1000 to put pressure on her.

I opened to 300 in heads‑up with QcTh and Flash 3‑bet to 1,000. QTo is a very playable heads‑up hand in position but is too weak to thin 4‑bet for value, and a min‑4bet to 1,700 would be too small to polarize effectively. Calling 700 more (1000 total) gives me position, reasonable pot odds (700/(1300+700)=35% required equity) and the ability to play postflop — I can c‑bet favorable Q/T/low/monotone flops, float and apply pressure, or fold to heavy action on A/K‑heavy boards. With deep stacks and an opponent who 3‑bets a mix of hands, calling and using positional postflop skills is the best line.

The flop of Kc 2c 3d favors my 3-betting range, which includes strong Kings like AK and KQ. With my Ace-high and backdoor diamond draw, I have some equity and the range advantage. I will place a continuation bet of 750 (slightly more than 1/3 of the pot) to maintain pressure and attempt to take the pot down immediately.

Pot is 2750 and Flash has bet 750; calling 750 gives me ~21.4% pot odds (750 / 3500). I have QcTh in position — not a made hand but a decent hand to float: second-high card, some backdoor runner-runner club outs (very slim) and decent equity vs a wide c-bet range after a 3‑bet. Flash’s flop bet is a relatively small c-bet into a 2k pot, so his range will include bluffs I can exploit by playing post‑flop in position. Raising to 1500 is a pure bluff with limited fold equity (and risks getting jammed by his short effective stack), while folding would give up good odds and initiative. I’ll call 750, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy action if I miss, press advantage if I pick up a pair or a favourable card.

On the turn 9h, the board remains relatively static. I 3-bet pre-flop and continuation bet the flop, but Minni called both times. Her calling range here likely includes Kx hands, pocket pairs like 88-JJ, or flush draws. With just Ace-high and no significant improvement, firing a second barrel into a range that has already called twice on a King-high board is risky. Checking here allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Minni reacts. If she checks behind, I may still have the best hand or can re-evaluate on the river. If she bets, I will likely have to fold as my Ace-high has little equity against her calling range.

I have QcTh on 2c Kc 3d 9h — no pair, only a backdoor club. Villain was the preflop 3‑better and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn: that line usually signals pot‑control or marginal/value hands rather than folding hands. Betting the turn would be a thin, high‑risk bluff with limited fold equity (many Kx, small pairs and even some bluffs will simply call; a raise would put me in a very difficult spot vs his ~7.95k effective stack). I’m in position, so I’ll keep the pot under control and see a free river card — if I pair the Q/T or pick up a meaningful club I can apply pressure or value bet; otherwise I’ll fold to substantial river aggression. Check to realize equity and avoid bloating the pot with air.

On the river 4c, the board is Kc-2c-3d-9h-4c. I 3-bet pre-flop and continuation bet the flop, but checked the turn when the board didn't improve my hand. Minni's check behind on the turn suggests she likely has a marginal made hand like a medium pocket pair (88-JJ) or a weak King, or she was drawing. While my Ace-high has very little showdown value against her likely range, a bluff on the river is risky given her significant chip lead and her tendency to call down in previous hands. Checking here allows me to get to a cheap showdown if she also checks, or to fold if she decides to bet, preserving my remaining stack of approximately 60 big blinds.

River completed a third club (Kc,2c,4c). I hold QcTh — no pair and only one club, so I missed; the board now allows any two‑club combo to have a flush. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop bet, then pot‑control checks on turn and river) contains a mix of medium/strong value hands (Kx, sets, two‑club flushes) and some bluffs, but my blockers are minimal. Betting the river would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity — many Kx and small pairs will simply call a river probe, and any two‑club flush will call or raise. With ~7.95k effective vs a 3.5k pot, risking a big portion of the stack here is unnecessary with air. I’ll check and go to showdown rather than over‑bloat the pot with no showdown value.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Ace-Nine suited (Ad 9d), which is a premier starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to raise to build the pot and take the initiative against Minni, who currently holds a significant chip lead. A standard raise to 3 big blinds is appropriate here to put pressure on the Big Blind while maintaining a manageable pot size given the stack depth.

Heads-up in the big blind facing a button open to 300 (I must call 200). My hand 7♥2♠ is the absolute worst offsuit hand: no straight or flush potential and very poor showdown equity. Calling requires ~33% equity to break even — 7-2 is well below that versus a button’s opening range, and I’ll be out of position postflop. A reraise to 500 would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity against Flash’s stack and tendencies. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) — standard strategy is to open-raise a wide range to seize the initiative and not give the BB a cheap flop. Tc5d is marginal but perfectly playable heads-up in position. The pot is only 150 and the call is cheap, but limp/passive play lets the BB see flops with good odds. I'll make a standard min-open to apply pressure, keep fold equity, and preserve flexibility postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold this weak hand; if he calls I'll c-bet on many favorable textures and play straightforwardly in position.

In a heads-up situation, 89o is a strong enough hand to defend against a min-raise from the button. The pot odds are favorable (3:1), and my hand has decent playability post-flop. I will call to see the flop rather than 3-betting, as I want to keep the pot manageable with a medium-strength starting hand while out of position.

On an Ace-high board like 2d-Ah-Ts, the range advantage heavily favors the pre-flop raiser. With 9-high and no real draws (only a very weak backdoor straight draw), I have no reason to lead out. I will check and most likely fold if Minni continuation bets, as I have zero equity against her range here.

I have top pair (T♣ with board A♥ T♠ 2♦) but a weak 5 kicker. Heads-up in position, villain defended the big blind with a likely wide range and checked the flop — showing either weakness or a plan to float. This is a good spot to bet for value and protection: I want to charge worse tens, small pairs and any hands that might pick up equity later, while also getting information. I’ll make a value/protection bet of ~60% pot (250 into 400). If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (blank → continue, Ace or heavy action → proceed cautiously). If I face a sizable check-raise I’ll give credit to stronger Ax or two-pair and fold more often given my poor kicker.

I have 9-high on an Ace-high board with no draws (no flush draw, no straight draw). Minni's continuation bet on this board texture is very standard as the pre-flop aggressor, and I have almost zero equity to improve or win the hand. Continuing would be a waste of chips.

In a heads-up situation, King-Jack offsuit (KJo) is a premium starting hand. I have the button and the initiative. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and maintain aggression, as I have been doing in previous hands.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 from the small blind. I have K♥4♠ — a marginal but playable hand in heads-up: it has decent showdown value and can make top pair or two pair. Calling is the standard play here — I get good pot odds to see a flop, the opener's range from the button is wide, and 3-betting OOP with K4o is overly loose and puts me in tough postflop spots. Plan: call to see the flop (300 total committed), proceed cautiously — continue with a King or strong draw, fold to sustained heavy aggression when I miss.

Heads-up on a dry, slightly spade-heavy flop (9♦ T♠ 3♠). I hold K♥4♠ — no pair, just one overcard and only a very weak backdoor spade possibility. I'm out of position and the opener from the button has a very wide c‑betting range, so I want to keep the pot small and see how he acts. Checking preserves pot control, lets me call a reasonable c‑bet to realize my equity (and fold to strong aggression), and still allows me to take a free turn to improve to a pair or pick up additional info. Bet/bluffing here with such a marginal hand OOP is unnecessary.

On a board of 9d-Ts-3s, I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw to the Queen and one overcard. Minni has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. This is a good board to continuation bet (C-bet) as I have position and equity. A bet of 300 (half the pot) puts pressure on her middle-range hands and allows me to take control of the pot. If she calls, I still have several outs to improve on the turn.

I’ll call 300. I’m out of position with K♥4♠ on 9♦ T♠ 3♠ — no pair and only a very weak backdoor spade possibility. The opponent opened wide from the button and this c‑bet is likely a lot of missed hands, so calling to realize equity is the prudent play. The pot is 900 and the bet is 300 (about 4:1 pot odds), so a call is +EV versus a wide c‑betting range. Raising OOP with this marginal hand would bloat the pot and put me in uncomfortable spots; folding is too nitty given the price. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve, but consider raising/betting if I pair my King or pick up a strong draw.

I have K♥4♠ on 9♦ T♠ 3♠ / A♣. I missed — no pair and no real draw (the two spades on board mean a flush is impossible now). Flash opened wide pre and barreled the flop; I called to realize equity, but the turn Ace massively improves parts of his range and hurts mine. I’m out of position and there’s no bet to call, so the prudent line is pot control: check and see what he does. If he bets, I’ll fold to meaningful pressure (and only entertain calling very small, overpriced bets); if he checks back I get a free river to try to pair up or re-evaluate.

The Ace of Clubs on the turn is an excellent scare card for me to continue my aggression. Having raised pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, a second barrel on the turn strongly represents that I have connected with the Ace. This board structure (9-10-3-A) is likely to have hit my range better than Minni's, as she merely called pre-flop and on the flop. By betting here, I can likely force a fold from her middle-pair hands (like a Ten or a Nine) or her draws. This mirrors my successful play in Hand #80 where a turn Ace allowed me to take the pot. I will bet 750 into the 1200 pot to maximize fold equity while maintaining a manageable pot size if called.

I checked the turn with K♥4♠ planning to fold to significant turn aggression. Flash has opened wide preflop and barreled the flop, then fires a sizable 750 into a 1950 pot on an A♣ turn — a card that heavily favors his continuing/value combos (Ax, Tx, sets, two pair) and crushes my K‑high. Calling 750 requires ~28% equity; I realistically have only ~6 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 4s) and far less equity vs a value‑heavy barreling range. Raising OOP with this marginal holding would bloat the pot in a dominated spot. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to play, folding preserves chips and avoids a difficult river decision. Fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with K♦3♠. In heads-up play the button opens extremely wide and K3o is playable — I want initiative and to play postflop with position. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x BB): big enough to apply pressure and price out many speculative hands, but small enough to keep the pot manageable with a marginal holding. Flash has been aggressive but also willing to call; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold K3o, if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated/ace-heavy boards.

I am holding Qd 9d in the Big Blind during a heads-up match. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. Qd 9d is a strong hand in a heads-up context with good playability and high equity against a standard opening range. While 3-betting is an option, calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop and utilize the hand's potential without over-committing against the larger stack before the flop.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a monotone flop of all clubs. While top pair is a strong hand in heads-up play, this board is extremely dangerous as any club in my opponent's hand has significant equity, and I am already beaten by any made flush. Being out of position, the best play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. My hand is strong enough to check-call a standard continuation bet, but leading out myself would likely only get called by better hands or draws that have a lot of equity against me.

Check. The flop Qc‑7c‑6c is very wet for my opponent’s BB calling range — lots of clubs, Qx, and medium pairs — and my K♦3♠ has no club and no pair (virtually no showdown value). A continuation bet here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity (many hands in his range simply call with a club or a pair) and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position when a check‑raise almost always means a made flush or very strong hand. I’ll pot‑control and realize equity from the button: if Flash checks back the turn I get a free card and can re‑evaluate; if he leads the turn I’ll call very small bets for price but fold to medium/large bets or a check‑raise, since my hand can’t stand big pressure on this texture.

On the turn Jack of Spades, the board is Qc 7c 6c Js. I have top pair (Queens) with a 9 kicker. Minni checked back the flop, which often indicates they do not have a made flush or a very strong hand like a set, as those hands usually bet for value and protection on a monotone board. Since the turn didn't change the club situation, I will lead with a value bet of 400 (roughly 2/3 pot). This bet targets hands like Jx, lower pairs, or single high clubs that Minni might have checked on the flop. It also provides protection against a potential river club.

I checked the turn to pot‑control with K♦3♠ and this 4♠ bet is a relatively small c‑bet (400 into a 1000 pot). Pure pot‑odds are ~28.6% but I only have ~6 outs (~12.8%) to improve, so calling isn’t profitable as a pure draw. However, I explicitly planned to call very small turn bets in position to keep the pot manageable and exploit Flash’s wide BB calling/betting range — he often fires small with thin value and bluffs. Calling lets me realize equity, keep position for the river, and fold to meaningful raise/river pressure. If I hit a King or 3 on the river I can proceed for value; if I miss and he barrels big I’ll fold. Therefore I will call the 400.

On the river, the board is Qc 7c 6c Js 3d. I have top pair with a Queen. Minni's check-back on the flop is a strong indicator that she likely does not have a made flush or a very strong hand like a set, as those hands usually bet for value and protection on a monotone board. Her call on the turn suggests she has some piece of the board, likely a Jack, a smaller pair, or a Queen with a weaker kicker. Given that Minni has shown a tendency to call down bets in previous hands (like Hand #7 and Hand #13), I will place a value bet of 800 into the 1400 pot. This targets her middle pairs and Jacks, which she is likely to call with to see the showdown.

I have K♦3♠ and river 3♦ pairs me into a very weak second pair. Pot is 2,200 and Flash’s bet is 800, so I need ~26.7% equity to call. Given the line (both checked the wet Qc‑7c‑6c flop, Flash then leads a small 400 on the turn and barrels the river for 800), his range is heavily weighted to value: Qx, Jx, medium pairs and club flushes — hands that all beat my 3s. I beat only pure air and a small fraction of missed draws; I don’t block many of his value combos and raising would be pure speculation with almost no fold equity. With far less than the required equity versus his value‑biased range and no reason to believe he’s bluffing often enough here, the +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Eight offsuit is a standard hand to open from the Button/Small Blind. My strategy has been to maintain aggression by raising to 300, which puts pressure on Minni and allows me to play the rest of the hand with positional advantage. Folding would be too tight for this range, and limping allows the Big Blind to see a free flop with any two cards.

Heads-up vs the button’s small open to 300 — wide opening range from Flash. Qh2h is marginal but suited and has decent playability heads-up, so folding is too tight and 3-betting OOP with this hand is thin. Calling 200 more (total committed 300) is cheap relative to the pot (roughly 2:1 immediate pot odds) and lets me realize equity and use my deep stack postflop. Plan: see the flop, proceed cautiously OOP — fold to strong aggression on bad boards, value-bet or barreling selectively when I pick up equity or a good read.

Board is 7h 2d Kc; I hold Qh2h — I have middle pair (deuces) with a decent queen kicker and a backdoor heart draw, but I'm out of position and this is a K‑high board that favors the preflop raiser. Flash opened wide from the button, so his range includes lots of Kx, broadways and many missed hands. I want to keep the pot under control with a marginal one-pair hand OOP: checking preserves pot control, lets me realize equity, and gives the button the opportunity to bluff. Plan: check; call a standard small c-bet (~≤½ pot) and re-evaluate on the turn, fold to large bet/raise that strongly represents top pair or better, and if I improve (2, Q, or a heart) consider betting/raising for value.

On this dry board with a King high, a continuation bet is standard as the pre-flop aggressor. The King is a card that significantly favors my perceived range. Minni's check indicates she likely missed the flop or has a weak holding. By betting 300 (half pot), I can often take down the pot immediately against hands that have missed or middle-tier pairs that feel vulnerable.

Flash (button) opened wide and c‑bet 300 into ~900 (small, ~1/3 pot). I have Qh2h — a made pair of deuces with a good queen kicker and a backdoor heart possibility. Calling 300 is cheap: calling gives me ~20% pot odds to win a ~1500 pot and I have more than that equity vs a wide c‑betting range. Calling also controls the pot OOP, lets me realize equity, and keeps weaker hands in that I can extract value from if I improve. Plan: call the flop; on the turn re‑evaluate — fold to heavy aggression if I don’t improve, but if I pair my Q or hit a heart/2 I can start betting or raise for value.

I have Qh2h on 7h 2d Kc Jc: second pair (pair of 2s) with a decent queen kicker but no real draws — the turn killed the tiny backdoor heart plan, so I only have ~5 outs (3 Qs + 2 deuces) to improve (~11%). Flash opened wide from the button and c‑bet a small 300 on the flop, so his range contains a lot of Kx, broadways and bluffs, but it also contains many hands that will continue vs pressure. I’m out of position and the pot is already 1,200; my priority is pot control and showdown equity with this marginal made hand. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity, and gives Flash the chance to barrel bluffs — I’m prepared to call a small/medium turn bet (<= ~½ pot) but will fold to heavy aggression if he represents strong two‑pair/top‑pair+; if he checks back we go to the river and re‑evaluate. Action: check to control the pot and avoid getting bloated OOP with a hand that has few clean outs.

The Jack of Clubs on the turn is an excellent card to continue my aggression. It improves the board texture in a way that should be favorable to my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser. More importantly, holding the Ten of Clubs and Eight of Hearts gives me an open-ended straight draw (a 9 completes the straight). By betting again (double barreling), I can represent a strong King or a Jack, putting significant pressure on Minni's likely middle-pair holdings like 7x or pocket pairs. In recent hands (Hand #80 and Hand #84), Minni folded to similar turn bets on high-card boards. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot maintains this pressure while building a pot for if I hit my draw.

Fold. I checked the turn to keep the pot under control with Qh2h (second pair, decent kicker) and now Flash bets 800 into a 1200 pot (pot shown 2000; calling 800 gives ~28.6% pot odds). I only have ~5 clean outs (3 Qs + 2 deuces) ≈ 10–11% to improve on the river, well below the required odds. Flash opened wide preflop and c-bet small on the flop, but turning up a substantial bet here is consistent with a lot of Kx/top-pair value and polarizing bluffs — my hand has poor fold equity OOP and raising as a bluff is too risky. Calling commits chips with insufficient equity, so fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♣T♥ is a clear open-raise spot. Calling the extra 50 is passive and lets the BB see cheap flops; a min-raise to 200 is too small and invites frequent defense/3-bets. I want initiative and fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks, so I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300 total). If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate, but versus a call I have good postflop playability in position.

I have pocket Kings (KK), which is the second-best starting hand. Minni has been quite aggressive from the button. I should 3-bet for value and to protect my equity. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing to build the pot against an active opponent.

Facing a 3‑bet to 1,000 after I opened to 300 with A♣T♥ on the button in heads‑up. I have position and a hand that plays very well postflop (ace‑x, Broadway outs). Effective stacks (~12.25k) are deep enough that a tiny 4‑bet to 1,700 would be weak and won’t generate meaningful fold equity; a larger polarized 4‑bet would commit too much with ATo. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and lets me realize equity against a likely wide‑to‑medium 3‑bet range. I’m getting roughly 35% break‑even equity to call, so I’ll call and play the flop in position, folding to extreme aggression on unfavorable runouts or applying pressure on favorable ones.

I have an overpair (KK) on a very dry, paired board (Qd 4h 4s). My opponent, Minni, called a 3-bet pre-flop, which suggests a range that includes pocket pairs and broadway cards. I am ahead of almost all of that range, losing only to pocket Queens or the unlikely trip fours. I will place a continuation bet to get value from hands like pocket Jacks, Tens, or any Queen, while also maintaining control of the pot. A sizing of 750 (37.5% of the pot) is effective on such a dry texture.

Flop decision: Flash 3‑bet pre and now makes a small bet (750 into 2750). Calling 750 to win 3500 gives ~21% break‑even equity. I have A♣T♥ — no pair but an Ace‑blocker (reduces some AA/AK combos), six clean turn outs to a pair (A/T), and position. Flash's line represents a mixed range (value: QQ/44/AA/KK and some Qx; bluffs/missed broadways). Raising here would be a thin, easily‑punished semi‑bluff versus sets and strong Qx hands; folding is too nitty given pot odds and my ability to realize equity in position. Plan: call to keep the pot controlled, see a turn, fold to extreme turn aggression on bad cards, or apply pressure/raise if I pick up or the turn is safe.

I have pocket Kings (KK) on a board of Qd 4h 4s 6c. The turn card is a complete brick that does not change the board texture significantly. I am ahead of almost all of Minni's range, which likely consists of Qx hands (AQ, KQ, QJ), mid-pocket pairs (JJ, TT, 99), or possibly some floating hands from the flop. Since I am out of position, I will continue to lead for value to build the pot. A bet of 1800 into the 3500 pot (roughly 50%) is a strong sizing that targets her Qx hands while keeping the pot manageable for a potential river decision.

Flash 3‑bet pre, c‑bet the paired Q44 flop and now barrels the turn to 1,800. The pot is 5,300, so calling 1,800 to win ~7,100 requires ≈25% equity. With A♣T♥ I have only 6 clean river outs (3 Aces, 3 Tens) ≈13% to improve, plus a small amount of showdown equity vs pure bluffs — well below the needed break‑even. Flash’s line is value‑heavy/polarized (QQ, 44, AA/KK, some Qx) and there aren’t enough credible bluffs to justify a call. Raising here would be a thin, easily‑punished shove into sets and strong Qx hands. Fold and preserve a big stack for better spots.

In heads-up play, Jh 3h is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I have been maintaining a high level of aggression recently, which has allowed me to take down several pots pre-flop or on the flop. I will raise to my standard 300 to continue this strategy and keep the pressure on Minni.

Facing a 300 open from the button heads-up, Flash is likely opening a wide range. 6♦8♠ is a marginal but defensible hand heads-up — it has some connectedness and can flop pairs/draws. Out of position and deep-stacked, I prefer to control the pot size and see a flop rather than 3-bet light OOP (6-8o doesn’t block enough of the button’s strong hands). Calling 200 (total 300 committed) gives good immediate pot odds and preserves the stack for postflop play. Plan: continue with any pair or strong draw on the flop, otherwise check and fold to significant aggression; occasionally 3-bet light for balance, but not with this hand here.

I have 6d8s on 6c6s9d — trips (a very strong hand heads-up). Flash opened wide from the button, so his range contains lots of worse hands (overcards, single pairs, broadways, small pocket pairs) and only a few combos (pocket 99 or a 6+9 combo) can have me crushed on this flop. Pot is 600 and villain is deep. I should bet for value and deny free cards rather than check and give him a chance to realize equity or turn bluffs. A half‑pot bet (~300) is large enough to charge hands that can improve and small enough to get called by worse pairs and Ax/Kx type hands. Plan: bet 300 now; if called, keep extracting on safe turns; if he check‑raises big, re‑evaluate for a possible full house but lean to call reasonable raises because combos that beat me are limited.

On a board of 6c-6s-9d, Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into the 600 pot. My hand, Jh 3h, has missed completely and has very little equity against a range that includes many 6s, 9s, or pocket pairs which are likely to call from the big blind pre-flop. While I have been aggressive recently, there is no need to float or bluff on a board that favors the big blind's calling range, especially when facing a lead. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button with A♠4♠ — a very playable, suited ace with good blocker value and excellent postflop playability in position. Pot is 150 and the to-call is only 50; calling keeps the initiative with the BB and often allows them to realize equity cheaply. I prefer to seize initiative and build fold equity with a standard heads-up open. Raising to 300 total (≈3x the big blind) is a crisp sizing: it pressures the big blind, isolates heads-up, and leaves us plenty of stack to maneuver postflop if called. If Flash 3-bets large we can reassess, but vs a single call we have position and a hand that plays well.

In this heads-up situation, Minni has raised to 300 from the button. I am holding 9-7 offsuit in the Big Blind. While this hand has some connectivity, it is generally considered weak to defend against a 3x open-raise, especially out of position. I need to call 200 more to win a total pot of 600, giving me 3:1 odds. However, 9-7 offsuit underperforms significantly against a standard button opening range. Given that there are still many hands left in the tournament and my stack is healthy (146 BBs), I will opt for a disciplined fold rather than playing a marginal hand out of position.

In a heads-up situation, a hand like Queen-Five offsuit is strong enough to open from the button/small blind. I will put pressure on the big blind with a standard raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size recently. If I am re-raised, I can easily fold this hand, but it is too strong to fold immediately or limp.

Flash (SB) opened to 300; I’m in the BB with 6♣5♣. Heads-up and with deep effective stacks, 65s is a clear defend — good playability and strong implied odds. The price is cheap: calling 200 into a 400 pot (~33% pot‑odds) to see a flop is correct. I’ll call to 300 total, looking to play flops with pair/draws/backdoor club equity and avoid 3‑betting this hand out of position.

Check. On 2♦ J♦ 3♠ with 6♣5♣ I have no pair — only four outs (any 4) to complete a 2-3-4-5-6 straight and a very small backdoor club flush chance. Flash opened wide preflop and will c-bet a lot, so out of position I want to pot-control and realize my equity rather than barreling as a bluff. With deep stacks I can call a reasonable continuation bet to see the turn and fold to strong pressure, or take a free turn if Flash checks back. Checking preserves flexibility and avoids committing chips with a marginal hand.

On a relatively dry, Jack-high board like J-3-2, a continuation bet is standard after raising pre-flop. My Queen-high has very little showdown value, but I can represent a Jack or a strong pocket pair. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to put pressure on Minni's likely missed range and keep my betting patterns consistent with previous winning hands.

Heads‑up with deep stacks and Flash showing a small, wide c‑bet on 2♦ J♦ 3♠. I have 6♣5♣ — no pair but a lot of playability: four clean 4s to complete the 2‑3‑4‑5‑6 straight and six pair outs (10 total), roughly ~38% to improve by the river. Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives ~25% break‑even equity, so a call is profitable versus a wide c‑bet range. I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP or bluff‑raise into a wide range; I’ll call to realize equity and reassess on the turn — bet/raise if I improve (pair/4/club) or fold to significant turn aggression if I’m still behind.

Flash checked the turn, so I can either try to bluff take it now or control the pot and see a cheap river. I have 6c5c with no pair; my only realistic outs on the river are 4s (4 outs) plus three remaining 5s and three remaining 6s (6 outs) = 10 outs total. With one card to come that’s ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve. The pot is 1,200 and stacks are deep, so betting OOP here as a bluff is risky — Flash opened wide preflop and will call or raise light, which would put me in a tough spot with only ~22% to improve. Checking preserves pot control, lets me realize my equity to the river, and keeps my options open: bet for value or pot-control on a safe river card, or fold to heavy aggression if I miss. I’ll check.

The turn paired the deuce, which is a relatively blank card that is unlikely to have improved Minni's range given her check-call on the flop. Since Minni checked again, I will continue my aggression with a second barrel. I have successfully used this 'double barrel' strategy to push Minni off hands in several recent pots (Hand #80, #84, and #86). A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot maintains the pressure on her range, which likely consists of weak Jacks, middle pairs, or draws that may fold to continued strength.

Heads‑up on a paired turn (2♦ J♦ 3♠ 2♥). I hold 6♣5♣ — I have 10 clean outs (four 4s + three 5s + three 6s) = 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve on the river. The pot is 2,000 and Flash bet 800, so the break‑even calling frequency is 800/(2,000+800) ≈ 28.6%, meaning a pure call vs a value‑only range is slightly -EV. However Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his turn betting range will include a meaningful number of bluffs/missed overcards and diamonds; additionally the 800 is a small percentage of my 24.8k stack (~3%). Raising OOP as a bluff is unattractive and folding surrenders too much equity. I’ll call to realize my equity and see the river; if I hit (pair/4/straight) I’ll seek value, and if I miss I’ll fold to significant river aggression.

River complete: board 2d Jd 3s 2h Jh — the board itself is two pair (Jacks and Deuces). I hold 6c5c so I have no pair and am playing the board; only hands that beat me are full houses/quads (any J or any 2 in Flash’s hand). Flash has been a wide, aggressive raiser/c-bettor, and many of his value/turn‑bet hands (Jx/2x) now become extremely strong on this river, so bluffing OOP here is thin and likely to be called or raised. Betting would be a pure bluff with poor blockers; checking preserves the pot and lets me see if Flash checks back (we chop) or barrels a value/full‑house hand (in which case I can fold and avoid further loss). I’ll check to showdown and fold to significant river aggression.

The board has double-paired (Jacks and Deuces), which means the best five-card hand for both players is likely the two pair on the board with the highest kicker. I hold a Queen, which is a relatively strong kicker in this scenario. Minni has checked the river after calling my bets on the flop and turn. If she has a Jack, she has a full house; if she has an Ace or King, she beats my Queen kicker. Since Minni has been sticky in previous hands, betting here is unlikely to force a fold from a better kicker (Ace or King) and will only be called by hands that beat me. Checking behind allows me to reach a showdown with decent value against her missed draws or weaker kickers.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with A♣9♦ — a strong hand in this spot. Pot is 150, calling only costs me 50 more (getting to 100 total) but that’s passive and gives the BB cheap odds. I want the initiative and to deny them a free flop, so I’ll raise.
