A standard 3x BB open to 300 (total committed) gives good fold equity, builds the pot with a favored hand, and keeps the sizing familiar against Flash’s tendencies. Postflop I’ll C-bet most flops for value and fold equity; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll proceed cautiously (call small/medium 3-bets, fold to overwhelming shoves depending on size).

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five suited, which is a very strong hand to defend against a standard 3x button open. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win 400), and my hand has good post-flop playability. I will call and see a flop.
I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw. In heads-up play, as the Big Blind, I act first post-flop. Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni proceeds on this Queen-high board.

I raised preflop and have the initiative heads-up. Flop Qh–8h–2s is fairly dry but has two hearts; I hold A♣9♦ so Ace-high is often best and I want to charge/deny equity to heart draws and weaker unpaired hands. A half-pot continuation bet (≈300 into the 600 pot) gets value from Kx/Jx/weak Ax and folds out some hands while keeping the pot manageable. If Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel safe cards; if he check-raises big I’ll give up most of the time with just Ace-high.

I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw on a Queen-high board. Minni's continuation bet is standard for a heads-up game. With pot odds of 3:1 (calling 300 to win a total pot of 1200), I have enough equity with my overcard and backdoor potential to justify a call. I will see a turn card and re-evaluate based on whether I improve or if the board texture changes significantly.

I will check the turn. The 3c is a non-threatening card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Since I am out of position and only have King-high, checking allows me to see what Minni does. If she checks back, I get a free look at the river. If she bets, I'll have to consider if she's polarized or if my King-high still has enough showdown value to warrant another call, but for now, checking is the most prudent play.

I c-bet the flop with A♣9♦ to take the initiative heads-up. Flash’s call on the Qh–8h–2s flop means his range is likely Qx, 8x, pocket pairs, or heart draws. The turn (3♣) is a blank for my hand — I still only have ace-high — and Flash checked. Betting the turn as a bluff could fold out some worse unpaired hands, but will get called by Qx/pairs and most draws, and risks a check-raise that I’m not comfortable with given my marginal holding. With the stacks and pot size (pot ~1,200) I prefer pot control: check back to see a cheap river and avoid bloating the pot with a bluff that has poor equity versus the likely calling range. Plan: check the turn, fold to a large river bet, and call small/medium river bets only if the price is right or if the river improves my hand.

I have King-high on a board of Q-8-2-3-J. Minni raised pre-flop and bet the flop, but checked the turn. This check often signifies a medium-strength hand looking for showdown or a surrender with air. Since I only have King-high, I lose to any pair and any Ace-high. However, I don't have enough equity to turn my hand into a bluff here against an opponent who has shown willingness to call with marginal hands. Checking is the best play to either see a cheap showdown or fold if she bets, as my hand is unlikely to be the winner.

I have A♣9♦ on a 2♠–8♥–Q♥–3♣–J♠ river. I was the aggressor pre and on the flop but my hand never improved past ace-high. Flash called my flop bet, then checked the turn and checked the river — a line consistent with Qx, 8x, small-to-medium pairs or missed heart draws. Betting here would be a thin, often-bluff attempt into a calling-heavy range and risks a check-raise that I don’t want to face with just ace-high. With the pot only 1,200 and deep stacks behind, the +EV play is pot control and take the showdown: check and see the showdown. If Flash fires a small bet I’ll weigh a call for pot odds; I’ll fold to a large raise.

In heads-up play, the button should maintain a high opening frequency to apply pressure and capitalize on being in position post-flop. While Ten-Five offsuit is a marginal hand, it is within a standard opening range against an opponent who has shown a willingness to fold to continuation bets and turn aggression. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB; I’m in the BB with 6♥2♥ and need to call 200 (total commitment 300). Heads-up I should defend a wide range and this is a cheap price to see a flop — calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% break-even equity. 6-2 suited is weak but has backdoor flush/straight potential; 3-betting this hand would be an over-bluff out of position and folding surrenders good pot odds. I’ll call (total 300) and play the flop cautiously out of position: check/fold to heavy aggression unless I hit a pair or strong draw, or seize a clear spot to bluff.

Board: 3♦ K♣ 5♥. I hold 6♥2♥ — no pair, only a one-card gutshot to a 4 (4 outs → ~16.5% to hit by river) and a runner-runner heart flush (~3%). Heads-up the SB opened wide preflop and will c-bet this dry K-high flop often, but I’m out of position and have very little equity. Leading out is an expensive bluff with low fold equity. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my modest equity. If Flash fires a small c-bet I can consider calling to see a turn; I’ll fold to significant pressure (large c-bet or raise) unless I pick up a pair, the 4, or a clear read to continue. If he checks back I get a free card to improve.

I have middle pair (fives) on a relatively dry board (Kc-5h-3d). In heads-up play, middle pair is a strong enough hand to bet for value and protection. Since the King-high board favors my pre-flop opening range, a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to represent a King while also getting value from weaker hands like a three, pocket pairs, or Ace-high. If Minni raises, I can evaluate, but if she calls, I will likely look for a cheap showdown by checking back on future streets.

Heads-up the SB opened wide preflop and is firing a small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot). I hold 6♥2♥: no pair but a 4-card straight draw to a 4 (4 outs, ~16–17% to hit by the river) plus a tiny runner-runner heart flush chance. Calling 300 to potentially win ~1500 after my call requires ~20% equity — my real equity is marginal but close, and the bet size is small enough to defend a very wide range out of position. I won’t over-bluff or raise OOP with this junk; I’ll call to realize equity and see a turn. Plan on folding to heavy turn aggression unless I pair or hit my straight/clear draw; if I improve I can proceed aggressively.

Board is 3♦ K♣ 5♥ 5♣; I hold 6♥2♥ with no pair. After calling preflop and the flop c-bet I was prepared to call a small turn bet, but Flash checked to me on the paired turn. My only realistic out is a 4 (4 outs, ~8.7% on the river) — flush is no longer possible — so my equity is tiny. OOP betting here as a pure bluff is thin: Flash’s line (preflop open, flop c-bet, turn check) can be either weak or a trap, and I don’t want to inflate the pot with junk. Best play is to check and see a free river; if I hit the 4 I can bet for value, otherwise fold to meaningful river aggression (or consider a very small river bluff only in the right spot). Preserving stack and picking better spots is the highest EV line.

The turn Five has significantly improved my hand to trips. Given that Minni called my flop continuation bet on a King-high board, it is highly probable she holds a King or a medium pocket pair. I need to bet for value here to build the pot while my hand is likely the best. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot (roughly 66%) is large enough to get value from a King or a sticky pair, while also setting up for a meaningful river decision.

Fold. The turn bet is 800 into a 2000 pot; calling 800 would make the pot 2800 so I need ~800/2800 ≈ 28.6% equity to break even. I hold 6♥2♥ on 3♦ K♣ 5♥ 5♣ — my only realistic outs are the four 4s to make a 2-3-4-5-6 straight (4 outs ≈ 8.7% to hit the river). My equity is far below the pot-odds threshold. Raising as an out-of-position bluff is too thin here: Flash opened pre, c-bet the flop and is barreling the paired turn — a line consistent with many value hands (Kx, 5x, 3x) that won't fold enough. With a healthy 24k stack it's correct to preserve chips and wait for a better spot. I fold.

Heads-up on the button with K♥9♣ — a standard open-raise spot. I want to take the initiative and use position postflop, while gaining fold equity against a wide big blind. A 3x BB open (to 300) is a normal sizing here: it builds the pot if I’m ahead, keeps worse hands from seeing a cheap flop, and still leaves plenty of stack behind to fold or continue vs a 3-bet. Calling 50 (limping) surrenders initiative and invites playing out of position postflop; the min-raise to 200 is too small and gives too much odds to a loose defender. Raise to 300 to play the hand aggressively in position.

In a heads-up situation, defending the big blind with suited connectors or gappers like 8c 5c is standard, especially receiving 2:1 pot odds on the call. The hand has decent playability post-flop, and folding such hands too often in the big blind allows the opponent to steal blinds too profitably.

I am out of position on an Ace-high board with only 8-high and no significant draws. This board strongly favors Minni's range as the pre-flop raiser. Checking is the standard play here, intending to fold to a bet unless I improve significantly.

Flop is 3♥ A♥ 6♦; I have K♥9♣ — no pair but a decent blocker (K♥) and the initiative from my preflop open. Heads‑up and in position, Flash checked, so I want to keep pressure and fold out hands that missed (low pairs, broadways without an Ace, some backdoor draws) while still keeping the pot manageable. I’ll make a standard continuation bet ~1/2 pot (300). If Flash raises I’ll give up K‑high (fold); if he calls I can continue to apply pressure on favourable turns or control the size of the pot and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have completely missed the flop with 8-high. The board features an Ace, which is much better for Minni's pre-flop raising range than mine. Without even a backdoor draw or any pair potential, calling a continuation bet here would be an unprofitable play. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better spot.

In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I am on the button (SB) and will raise to 300 to take the initiative and build a pot, as pocket threes are well ahead of the average random hand Minni holds in the Big Blind.

Call — I’ll put in the additional 200 (total 300). Q♠2♠ is a marginal hand but in heads-up vs a standard SB open it’s worth defending: I get good pot odds to see a flop and the hand has some flush/straight potential. Flash has been opening to 300 frequently from the button, so his range is likely wide; 3-betting Q2s as a bluff is suboptimal (poor kicker/blockers and out of position postflop), and folding is too tight given the price and stack sizes. Plan: see the flop cheaply, continue with a queen, two pair, flush/strong draw, or proceed cautiously and fold to heavy postflop aggression if I miss.

Heads-up on Jc 8c 4h with Q♠2♠ — I’ve missed the flop: only a single overcard and a backdoor spade. I’m out of position and opponent’s open-raising range is wide, so betting here would bloat the pot with very little equity or fold equity. Best line is pot control: check and see what Flash does. If he fires a small probe I can call for pot odds or fold to a sizable c-bet; if the turn brings a Q or a spade I can play more aggressively. Checking preserves my stack and keeps marginal hands in a controlled, low-variance spot.

I have a pocket pair of Threes. The flop J-8-4 is relatively dry except for the club draw. Since I raised pre-flop and Minni checked to me, a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate to take down the pot now or protect my hand against overcards that could hit on the turn.

Call — Flash’s c-bet is small so the price is right. Pot is 900 and I need to call 300, so I’m getting to win 1,200 total and need ~25% equity to break even. Q♠2♠ has limited but real equity versus a wide SB continuation range: roughly ~24% to pair by the river plus some showdown value as a Q overcard and it blocks some Qx combos. The flop (Jc‑8c‑4h) is fairly dry and Flash is likely continuation-betting a lot of his range, so I can realize equity cheaply. Raising OOP here would be a thin bluff (poor blockers, out of position) and folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call 300, re-evaluate on the turn — if I pair a Q (or the opponent checks) I can get aggressive; if I miss and face a large turn barrel I’ll fold. (Note: with both spades in hand and no spades on the flop, a five-card spade flush by the river is impossible, so my outs are mainly pair-outs.)

Opponent checked the King on the turn. I hold Q♠2♠ — still completely missed: no pair, no backdoor flush (no spades on board), only ~6 clean outs. Out of position with deep stacks and a wide opponent range, betting here would be a thin bluff that bloats the pot and can be raised by lots of hands that already beat me (Kx/Jx/8x, clubs). Better line is pot control and showdown protection: check back, realize equity if I pair on the river, and avoid committing chips with a weak hand. If the river pairs my Q (or a cheap river bet arrives) I can consider calling/raising; otherwise I’ll fold to significant aggression.

The King on the turn is another overcard to my pocket threes. Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, which suggests she likely has a piece of the board (a Jack, Eight, or Four) or a flush draw. Since she checked again, I will check behind to see a free river card. My hand has very little showdown value, and betting again is unlikely to force a fold from better hands given that the King could easily have improved her range or she may be holding a Jack.

Check — I have Q♠2♠ and missed every meaningful improvement. I pot‑controlled the hand on the flop and turn and Flash checked the King on the turn and again on the river (A♣). With the Ace and King on board plus three clubs, a lot of villain’s value combos (A×, K×, J×, club flushes) still beat me and many of those will call a river bluff. I have very little fold equity and only block a few Qx combos, so firing a bluff here is thin and high variance for a small pot (1200). Checking preserves my stack and realizes the small showdown equity I have against villain’s wide opening range — fold to a bet if he leads — so I’ll check to see the showdown for free.

The river brings the Ace of clubs, which is another overcard and completes a potential flush. Since I checked the turn, my hand looks weak. Minni has checked for the third street in a row (including the turn). While she might be weak, my pocket threes are now essentially the lowest possible pair on this board (beaten by Jacks, Eights, and Fours). I have very little showdown value, but betting here as a bluff is risky because the board is so coordinated; she may have called the flop with a piece of the board that she won't fold now. I will check behind and hope to win against missed straight draws like 7-6 or 9-7, or simply concede the pot if she has any pair.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with A♠ J♦ — a strong, high-card hand that plays very well in position. Pot is 150 and I only have to call 50 to see the flop, but limping/investing just the call surrenders initiative and allows the BB to take control. I want to seize initiative, build value, and apply fold equity against a wide BB defending range. Standard heads-up sizing is ~2.5–3x the big blind; min-raise is 200 but I’ll raise to 300 to charge limpers/weak calls while keeping postflop play straightforward. If Flash 3-bets I’ll reassess (AJ has decent equity vs light 3-bets but can be behind vs strong ranges); if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value or fold when clearly outplayed.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni, who has been aggressive from the button, has raised to 300. In a heads-up situation, J8o is a standard defending hand against a 3x raise. I am getting 2:1 odds on the call (200 more to win a total pot of 600). Given the pot odds and the playability of the hand in a heads-up pot, folding would be too tight and a 3-bet is unnecessary with this specific hand. I will call to see a flop.

On a flop of 5d-Kh-5s, the board is paired and contains a high card (King) that fits the range of a pre-flop raiser like Minni. I currently have Jack-high with no direct improvement or draws. Standard play in the Big Blind after defending is to check to the aggressor to see their reaction. Leading out (donk betting) into this board texture rarely makes sense for my range, so I will check.

Heads-up I was the preflop raiser and Flash checked the K♣5♦5♠ flop. I have A♠J♦ — ace-high with a backdoor spade and an Ace blocker that reduces some strong Ax combos. This board is fairly dry and misses a large portion of the BB’s defending range (lots of overcards and weak holdings), so a continuation bet gets value from missed hands and small pairs while applying fold equity. I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot (400) to maximize fold equity against his wide checking range but leave room to fold or control the pot if he shows strength. Plan: if he folds I take it; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate; if he check-raises large I’ll respect it and likely fold my unimproved Ace-high.

On a board of 5d-Kh-5s, Minni has bet 400 into a 600 pot. My hand, Jack-high (J8o), has no piece of this board and no draws. While the board is paired, making it a potential candidate for a bluff, Minni's range as the pre-flop raiser heavily favors the King. I have very little equity and no profitable way to continue in this hand. Continuing with Jack-high here would be a waste of chips, especially given the tournament context where preserving the stack is important.

I am holding Jack-Seven suited on the button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the average range. Throughout the match, I have consistently opened for 300 chips from the button to maintain pressure and build pots when I have equity. Minni has shown a tendency to defend the big blind but often folds to continuation bets or turn barrels if the board doesn't connect. Raising here allows me to take the lead in the hand and capitalize on post-flop opportunities.

Heads-up on the button Flash opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with J♠2♦ — a weak, offsuit holding but still playable heads-up. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total) which gives me ~33% break-even equity. Versus a wide button opening range J2o has borderline but acceptable equity and calling preserves my stack and lets me realize equity out of position without turning this into a bluff 3-bet (I have poor blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff). Plan: call to see a flop, fold to heavy, polarized aggression if I completely miss, but continue/proceed if I hit a jack or get favorable texture/backdoor equity.

I have J♠2♦ on 2♠-4♥-6♦ — bottom pair with a decent kicker. Versus a wide button opening range this hand has reasonable showdown value but is vulnerable to any 4/6 or bigger pocket pairs. The flop is very dry and rainbow (almost no flush/major straight draws), so pot control is the priority: checking keeps the pot manageable, induces bluffs from Flash’s wide continuation range, and lets me call reasonable c-bets for value. Plan: check, call small-to-medium flop bets to realize equity and fold only to a large raise that prices me out or shows strong strength.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding Jack-high on a low, dry board of 2-4-6. This board texture is generally good for a continuation bet because it is unlikely to have hit Minni's calling range hard, and Minni has shown a consistent pattern of folding to flop aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #84, #86, #92). A standard bet of half the pot (300 chips) allows me to maintain initiative and take down the pot if Minni missed, while keeping some equity with my backdoor straight and flush draws.

Call 300. I have bottom pair (2♦) with a decent jack kicker on a very dry, rainbow 2♠-4♥-6♦ flop. Flash is a wide button opener who bets small frequently — his 300 sizing into the current pot gives me roughly 25% pot odds (300 to win ~1200), so calling to realize showdown equity is the right play. Raising would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and get me in trouble versus any 4x/6x or overpairs; folding concedes too much given my showdown value. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy, polarized turn/river aggression, or extract value if Flash checks or I improve (jack/trips).

I have J♠2♦ on 2♠-4♥-6♦-8♦ — bottom pair with a weak kicker, out of position. Flash is a wide button opener who c-bet the flop (small sizing) and then checked the turn: that sequence shows weakness or pot control more often than strength. The board is fairly dry (no possible flush by showdown and only thin straight possibilities), so my hand has decent showdown value versus missed overcards and bluffs, but is crushed by any 8x/6x/4x or overpairs. Betting the turn would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and get value-called by hands that already beat me; raising would be irresponsible. With deep stacks relative to the pot, pot control is the priority. I will check the turn, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the river: call small-to-medium river bets (targeting thin value/bluffs), fold to large polarized river aggression, and only pursue further aggression if I improve (pair a Jack or make trips).

The 8 of diamonds is an excellent card for my hand, as it provides me with a flush draw to the Jack and a gutshot straight draw. Minni has checked again after calling a continuation bet on the flop. Given the board is becoming more connected (2-4-6-8), a second barrel on the turn exerts significant pressure on Minni's range, which likely consists of middle-to-low pairs or high-card hands that missed the board. Throughout the session, Minni has shown a tendency to fold to turn aggression when they don't have a clear holding. By betting 800 into a 1200 pot, I maximize my fold equity while maintaining a strong 12-out draw (9 diamonds + 3 remaining fives) to improve on the river if called.

Call 800. Pot is 2000 and Flash’s bet of 800 makes the pot 2800; calling 800 requires ~22% equity. I have bottom pair (2♦) with a decent J kicker on a very dry board (2♠-4♥-6♦-8♦). Against a wide HU opening and frequent c-bettor like Flash, my hand has reasonable showdown value and beats a lot of missed overcards/bluffs that he will continue with. Raising would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and folding concedes too much given the pot odds. Plan: call to see the river and re-evaluate — fold to large polarized river overbets or strong lines that represent 8x/6x/4x/overpairs; call small-to-medium river bets and extract value if I improve (J or trips) or if the river blanks and he shows weakness.

I have J♠2♦ on 2♠-4♥-6♦-8♦-6♥ — two pair (6s & 2s) but the river pairing the 6 creates many hands in Flash’s likely line that beat me (any 6x trips, any 8x or 4x making two pair with a higher second pair, and 2x with A/K/Q kicker that would out-kick my J). Flash was the preflop raiser and continued with flop/turn aggression, so his range contains both these value hands and bluffs/overcards. Betting here would fold out some worse hands but also polarize the pot and induce raises from the hands that already beat me; I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal two-pair. Pot control and taking a cheap showdown is the highest EV line — check and show down (call a small river bet, but avoid bloating the pot or calling/leading into a large raise).

On the river, the board pairs with a six (2s-4h-6d-8d-6h). Throughout the hand, I have maintained aggression by betting pre-flop, on the flop, and on the turn. However, Minni has called every step of the way, indicating she likely has a piece of this board, such as a pair of eights, a pair of fours, or potentially a six. My Jack-high has missed its straight and flush draws. While a bluff is an option, Minni has shown in previous hands (like Hand #17 and #63) that she is willing to call down with marginal made hands. Since the board pairing doesn't significantly change the equity of a low-pair hand that called the turn, and because I still have a healthy stack to continue the tournament, I will check back and concede the pot rather than risking a large bluff against a sticky opponent.

Heads-up on the button with K♥5♠ — a hand strong enough to open but not worth over-committing. I want initiative and fold equity; calling lets the BB see a cheap flop with a wide range and cedes position. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 (total) to pressure worse hands, protect against free draws, and play the pot in position while keeping the pot manageable against deeper stacks.

I am holding Ten-Nine of spades in the Big Blind. In a heads-up game, T9s is a premium starting hand with excellent connectivity and flush potential. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the button. While calling is an option to see a flop cheaply, I am out of position for the rest of the hand, which makes playing 'fit-or-fold' difficult. I will put in a 3-bet to 600 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If called, I have a hand that flops very well and allows for effective continuation betting on a variety of textures.

Fold. Heads-up on the button with K♥5♠ — I opened to 200 and Flash 3-bet to 600. The pot is 800 and I must call 400 (≈33% pot odds). Against a realistic 3‑bet range (pocket pairs, Ax, Kx and strong broadways, plus some suited connectors), K5o is frequently dominated and has well under the ~33% equity required to justify a call. A 4‑bet to the 1,000 minimum would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and can get me into a difficult spot if called or 5‑bet; it’s unnecessarily risky with a marginal hand. With deep stacks and heads‑up play, I’d rather preserve fold equity and wait for a better spot or a hand with more blockers. Standard, +EV play here is to fold and conserve chips.

8-9 offsuit is a standard opening hand on the button in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive image and attempt to take down the blinds or see a flop with a hand that has some connectivity.

Heads-up vs the button (SB) who opened to 300. I have T♣6♥ — a marginal but defendable hand in the big blind heads-up. Pot is 400 and it only costs me 200 more (good pot odds) to see a flop. I don’t want to 3-bet out of position with a weak offsuit hand, so I’ll defend and call to see the flop; plan to proceed cautiously OOP (fold to heavy aggression if I completely miss, continue with top pair/draws).

Flop is 8♦ 7♥ 6♣ and I hold T♣6♥ — bottom pair with a decent kicker heads-up. Opener’s range is wide so this hand has good showdown value, but I’m out of position and don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal pair. I’ll check to control the pot and induce a continuation bet. Plan: call small-to-medium c-bets (good pot odds vs a wide range) and fold to large check‑raises that strongly represent sets/two-pair; if villain checks back I take a free turn card and re-evaluate.

I have top pair (8s) and an open-ended straight draw (9-8-7-6) on this flop of 8-7-6. This is a very strong hand/draw combination. Minni has checked to me, so I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to get value from weaker pairs, such as 7x or 6x, and to protect my hand against overcards or draws.

Heads-up OOP on 8♦7♥6♣ with T♣6♥ — bottom pair with a decent kicker. Villain opened wide preflop and is making a standard ~half‑pot c-bet (300). Calling 300 into the current pot gives me roughly 25% break‑even equity, and my hand has good showdown value vs a wide continuation range (many overcards/floats and missed hands). I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal pair or bluff‑raise into a lot of strong hands, so I’ll take the cautious + pot‑control line: call the 300, see a turn, and re‑evaluate. On the turn I’ll continue with improved hands (two‑pair/trips/top pair) and fold to heavy aggression if I completely miss and face strong pressure.

I’m OOP on a very connected 8♦7♥6♣5♥ board holding T♣6♥ — bottom pair with a decent kicker. Villain opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, so my hand has good showdown value heads‑up but is vulnerable: the turn completes many straight combinations (4s and 9s) and can improve two‑pair/trips hands I’m behind to. Betting OOP would bloat the pot with a marginal holding and put me in tough spots against calls or raises. I’ll control the pot and check. Plan: check to induce bluffs and keep the pot manageable; call small‑to‑medium river bets (this hand still has showdown value heads‑up) but fold to large river aggression or shove if I completely miss and face strong pressure.

On a board of 8-7-6-5, I have successfully turned the straight with my 9h (9-8-7-6-5). While any 9 makes this straight and a T9 would make a higher straight, I am currently holding a very strong hand. Minni checked to me again, and I want to extract value from hands like a 4 (which makes a lower straight), sets, or two-pair. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot (2/3 pot) is a standard value sizing here to build the pot while the board is coordinated.

I’ll call 800. I’m OOP with bottom pair (6♣ with T♣6♥) on a very connected 8♦7♥6♣5♥ board. Calling 800 into a 2,000 pot gives me ~28.6% break-even equity — with Flash’s wide HU opening and c‑betting range I still have decent showdown equity versus many bluffs/floats and missed overcards, and my hand is too cheap to fold given the pot odds. Raising OOP with a marginal pair is constructive only rarely (it bloats the pot and plays poorly vs value hands or straights), and folding concedes too much equity. Plan on seeing the river: fold to large river shove or clear value bet (if the river completes obvious straights like a 4 or 9), call small-to-medium river bets if the board is safe or villain checks and take showdown.

I have two pair (6s and 5s) on 8♦7♥6♣5♥5♣ — a strong showdown hand heads‑up but vulnerable to straights (4/9) and trip/full‑house combos (any 5, pocket 6s, pocket 5s). Flash bet the flop and barreled the turn big, then checked the river after the board paired; that line looks like a lot of missed draws or a pot‑control with a marginal hand rather than an unblockable value line. Betting for thin value risks getting check‑raised by the few hands that beat me and bloats the pot versus many hands I already beat; checking denies those bluffs the price they need to continue and takes the safest line to lock up my equity. I’ll check and show down rather than bluff or inflate the pot OOP.

The river 5c pairs the board. While I have a 9-high straight (9-8-7-6-5), the board pairing is a significant development. If Minni has a 5 in her hand (such as 56, 57, 58, or even A5), she has now improved to a full house or trips. Additionally, a T9 would make a higher straight. Since Minni has checked, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and avoid being check-raised by a superior hand, especially since it's difficult to get value from many worse hands on this board texture.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the 50 SB) with A♣ J♦ — a very strong hand in this spot. Pot is 150 and the call is only 50, but calling gives Flash a free or cheap flop with a very wide range. I want to take the initiative and build the pot while denying favorable odds to draws, so I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300 total). That sizing is standard for my table image, isolates the BB and makes Flash pay (~200 to call into ~600 total), and keeps the hand highly playable postflop in position. Plan: c-bet most dry/A-high flops, proceed cautiously on coordinated or two‑toned boards, and fold to a very large 3-bet (or evaluate a call vs a small 3-bet depending on size and reads).

I am holding 8c 3h in the big blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind. 83o is one of the weakest hands in heads-up poker. Although I am getting 2:1 odds on a call, the hand has very poor playability post-flop, especially out of position against an opponent with a larger stack. I will fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ad As), the strongest possible starting hand. In a heads-up situation, my goal is to build a large pot while keeping my range looking relatively standard. Over the past several hands, my opening raise size from the button has consistently been 300 chips. I will maintain this sizing to remain balanced and encourage Minni to defend or 3-bet with a wider range of hands, allowing me to extract maximum value.

Facing a button open to 300 as the big blind with 3♠2♦. To call I must put in 200 into a 400 pot (making the pot ~600), so I need ~25% equity to justify a call. 3-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands — it has only ~12% chance to hit a pair on the flop, is frequently dominated by the opener’s wide but higher cards, and has nearly no backdoor play. A 3-bet bluff would commit a lot of chips unnecessarily versus an aggressive button and is high variance. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left, preserving my stack is the correct long-term play. Fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♢3♣ — this is a strong, positionally advantaged hand. With pot 150 and only 50 to call, I want to take the initiative and build the pot while keeping opponents' calling range wide. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to apply pressure, gain fold equity, and play postflop in position; if Flash 3-bets large I can reevaluate (fold to very large 3-bets), but vs a single call I plan to c-bet A-high/dry flops and use position to extract value or control pot size on coordinated boards.

In a heads-up situation, J9o is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise. Minni has been quite active from the button, and the pot odds (2:1) are favorable for a call. I will see a flop and evaluate based on board texture, as J9o has decent playability and realization of equity.

On a low, connected board like 6-7-4, I have missed the flop with Jack-Nine offsuit, though I do have two overcards and some very thin backdoor potential. Standard play from the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading into Minni here doesn't accomplish much as I don't have enough equity to build a pot, and I'd like to see if she takes a free card or continues her aggression before deciding how to proceed.

I raised preflop and have position; on this dry, rainbow 6♦7♥4♣ flop my A♦3♣ is just ace‑high but my preflop raising range contains many Aces and broadways that miss this board. Versus a wide BB calling range this is an ideal spot to take the initiative and fold out many unpaired hands and small pairs. The pot is 600, so a half‑pot c‑bet gives good fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. I will bet 300 to apply pressure; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (check to pot control on scary cards, continue only with real equity or well‑timed bluffs). If Flash check‑raises strongly (large sizing / pot‑committing), I will concede this Ace‑high and fold.

Minni's continuation bet on this low, connected board is standard for her aggressive button play. With Jack-Nine offsuit, I have two overcards that could potentially give me the best hand on the turn. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (300 to win a total pot of 900). Given the wide range she opens from the button, my overcards have reasonable equity, and I can evaluate the turn based on whether I improve or if she continues her aggression.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds is an excellent card for my hand, giving me Top Pair with a decent kicker (Jack-Nine). The board texture is quite connected (6-7-4-J), but I am now likely ahead of many of Minni's continuation-betting range from the flop. In this situation, the most effective strategy is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to continue her aggression with bluffs or semi-bluffs (like straight draws with an 8 or a 5) and avoids forcing out her weaker hands that might still bet. If she bets, I have a very comfortable call and can evaluate the river.

Check. I opened pre and c‑bet the dry 6♦7♥4♣ flop and got called, but on the turn I only have A♦3♣ — just ace‑high. The J♦ is exactly the kind of card that helps the caller’s calling range (Jx, two‑pairs, pocket pairs and some semi‑bluffs) and doesn’t give me any meaningful extra equity or a nut draw. With stacks deep relative to the 1,200 pot I don’t want to bloat the pot with air or face a check‑raise on a card that favors his range. I’ll control the pot by checking, realize showdown equity, and make a river plan: call small river bets sometimes, fold large ones, or bluff selectively if a favorable card and line appear.

On the river, the board has paired with a four, and the final board is 6-7-4-J-4. I hold top pair (Jacks) with a nine kicker. Minni's decision to check back the turn is a significant indicator of weakness or a capped hand, as most players would continue betting for value or protection with a strong Jack, a set, or a straight on such a connected board. Since she checked, it is highly unlikely she holds a monster or even a better Jack. I will lead with a value bet of 600 (50% of the pot). This sizing is designed to get called by her middle pairs (7x, 6x), pocket pairs like 88 or 55, or even an Ace-high hand if she suspects I am bluffing with missed straight draws like 85 or 53.

The river 4♦ pairs the board and is exactly the sort of card that improves the caller’s range: any single 4 becomes trips and pocket 44 becomes quads, plus turn Jx/overpairs and many pocket pairs still beat ace‑high. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, half‑pot bet on the paired river) is highly value‑oriented here. Pot is 1800 and the call is 600, so I’d need ~25% equity to break even. A♦3♣ is only ace‑high and has poor showdown value vs the hands he’s likely betting; it blocks very few of his value combos and there aren’t enough plausible bluffs in his line to justify calling. Folding preserves stack and avoids getting drawn out or crushed by trips/quads/value hands.

In a heads-up situation, a suited hand like 9d4d is strong enough to open from the button. I want to maintain aggression and leverage my position for the rest of the hand. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size, and it puts pressure on the big blind while allowing me to play the pot with initiative.

Heads-up vs the button's 300 open. I'm in the big blind with Td3h — a marginal hand but absolutely within a defendable heads-up range. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call, so I'm getting 200/(400+200)=33% pot-odds. Against a wide button opening range I have enough equity to see a flop and the stacks are deep (effective ~165bb), so postflop playability and implied odds favor a call. 3-betting is an option, but with this weak holding I don't want to bloat the pot out of position or commit chips as a bluff. Plan: call, see the flop; proceed aggressively if I connect (T or strong draws) or play pot-control / fold to heavy action on dry boards.

I completely missed this flop with Td3h on 6c-6h-7d — no pair and only a tiny backdoor heart. Heads‑up the button's open range is wide and he will c‑bet this texture a lot, but many of those hands (A/Kx, 7x, small pairs) won't fold to a marginal bluff. I'm out of position with deep stacks (effective ~16.5k, ~165bb) so I don't want to bloat the pot with air. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me fold to real aggression, and preserves the option to take a free card or try a delayed bluff if the turn helps (T/3/heart) or the opponent shows weakness. A lead here would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and is likely to be called; a check-fold/call plan post‑bet is far cleaner.

I raised pre-flop and the flop comes paired (6-6-7). This board generally favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, although the Big Blind can certainly have a 6. By betting 300 (half pot), I am making a continuation bet to capitalize on the times Minni has missed entirely (which is frequent on this board). If she calls, I can re-evaluate on the turn, but for now, I want to take the initiative.

I checked the flop with Td3h and completely missed. Facing a 300 bet into a 900 pot (I must call 300 → ~25% pot odds), my hand has no pair and only a tiny backdoor heart. The button’s c-bet range heads‑up will include many hands that already beat me (6x trips/quads, 7x combos, pairs/overpairs) plus some bluffs — my equity to pair by the river is only ~24% and will be worse vs value-heavy portions of his range. Out of position with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with air and I lack good blockers/fold equity to make a profitable raise. Calling is marginal and just invites a bigger OOP pot; folding preserves chips and lets me wait for a better spot. Action: fold.

Heads-up on the button with Ten of clubs + Deuce of diamonds (T2o). In heads-up play the button opens a very wide range; although T2o is marginal it’s perfectly reasonable to raise for initiative and fold equity. I’ll min-raise to 200 (2BB total) to apply pressure, either pick up the pot preflop or take a positional advantage to the flop while keeping the pot small. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll C‑bet appropriate flops, value bet when I connect (tens) and play cautiously when I miss.

In heads-up play, Ace-Nine offsuit is a premium hand. Minni has opened with a small 2x raise to 200. I will 3-bet to 700 to take the initiative, build a larger pot with a likely best hand, and put pressure on the button's opening range.

I opened to 200 from the button and Flash 3‑bet to 700 (I must call 500 more into a 900 chip pot). My holding (Tc2d) is too weak to continue facing a sizable 3‑bet: it has poor playability, no meaningful blockers (no A/K), and is often dominated by Flash’s 3‑betting range. 4‑betting as a bluff is unsound with this hand, and calling to play post‑flop would put a lot of chips in with little equity. I’ll fold and preserve chips for better heads‑up spots, consistent with my preflop plan to fold to a large 3‑bet.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit, a premium starting hand in heads-up play. I am on the button and should exert pressure to build the pot. A standard raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take the initiative in the hand.

Heads-up vs Flash (button/SB) who opened to 300. I have 8♦9♠ — a connected, playable hand against a very wide button opening range. Stacks are deep (22600 vs 17000), so implied odds favor seeing flops, but I'm out of position. 3-betting OOP with 98o is marginal: a raise to 500 would commit more chips into a 3-bet pot where this hand loses some playability. I’ll call the extra 200 (300 total) to keep the pot controllable, see a flop, and then proceed based on texture — look to continue/raise when I pick up equity (pairs, straights, good draws) and fold to heavy, confident aggression on dry boards.

I have 9s8d on 7d-9h-As: top pair (nines) with a decent kicker and an open-ended route (6 or T). Heads-up vs a wide CO/SB opening range, many of Flash's hands contain an Ace, so my hand is strong but vulnerable. I'm out of position and stacks are deep — I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot OOP with a hand that can be second-best to many A-x combos. Check to keep the pot manageable, allow Flash to bluff/continuation-bet, and plan to call a standard small-to-medium c-bet to realize equity; fold or re-evaluate if Flash shows heavy/ polarizing aggression (large check-raise or shove).

I have top pair with a strong kicker (Queen) on a relatively dry board of Ace-Nine-Seven. Since Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from weaker Aces, middle pairs, or draws.

Call 300. I’m out of position with second pair (pair of nines) plus an open-ended straight draw (6 or T) — a strong combination vs a wide button opening range. Flash’s 300 is a small-to-medium c-bet that can be many Ax hands, broadways, or pure bluffs; raising OOP here would bloat the pot when I’m often behind to A-x and lose fold equity against stronger Aces. Calling controls the pot, realizes my equity (I have additional outs to two‑pair/trips as well — roughly ~13 raw outs, ~28% to hit on the turn, ~46% by river) and keeps worse hands and bluffs in. Plan to re-evaluate on the turn: if I improve (two‑pair/straight) or get a favorable runout I’ll bet/raise for value; if the turn bricks and Flash shows heavy, polarizing aggression I’ll fold or proceed cautiously given my vulnerability to A‑x.

I check. Reasoning: I’m out of position heads‑up with 9♠8♦ on 7♦‑9♥‑A♠‑K♦ — second pair with an open‑ended (6 or T) but the turn K significantly strengthens Flash’s range (more Kx and Ax combos) and makes my pair more vulnerable. With deep stacks I want pot control OOP: betting here bloat­s the pot when I’m often behind to A‑x/K‑x and has poor fold equity. By checking I (1) realize equity on the straight outs, (2) induce bluffs from worse Aces/broadways, and (3) keep options open on the river. Plan: call small‑to‑medium turn bets (roughly ≤ ~300–400) where my combined pair+draw equity + fold equity can be profitable; fold to large, polarized turn/raise sizes that strongly represent two pair/set/A‑K. If Flash checks back I’ll evaluate the river — shove/bet for value if I hit a straight or two‑pair; otherwise check‑call small river bets for showdown and fold to big pressure.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (Queen) on a board of As-Kd-9h-7d. Minni has check-called the flop and checked the turn. This line often suggests a marginal made hand like a weaker Ace (AJ, AT, A8) or potentially a mid-pair. The King on the turn is a significant card, but I still likely have the best hand against her calling range. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to extract value from those weaker Aces and to charge any straight draws (like JT). If she raises, I will need to consider if she has improved to two pair or better, but for now, betting for value is the most profitable play.

Call 800. I’m OOP with second pair (9s) plus an open‑ended straight draw on 7d‑9h‑As‑Kd. Flash’s 800 is a sizable turn bet, but the pot odds make a call correct: calling 800 into a ~2000 pot gives a break‑even equity of ~28.6%. I have roughly 16 clean river outs to make two‑pair/trips/straight (~34–35% to hit), and I also have showdown value against a number of his missed broadway/bluff/call ranges, so my effective equity exceeds the price. Raising OOP here is unattractive — it bloats the pot with a hand that is often behind to A‑x/K‑x and won’t fold enough better hands. Plan: call the turn, then on the river—if I hit two‑pair/trips/straight I’ll extract value; if I miss, I’ll fold to large polarized pressure but call small/medium bets for showdown or check back vs passive lines.

I have 9♠8♦ on 7♦-9♥-A♠-K♦-4♥ — second pair with a weak kicker; my straight outs (6/T) missed on the river. Flash showed aggression on flop and turn but checked the river, which looks like pot control with a marginal A/K or a missed draw. Betting OOP here would be a thin value bet or bluff that won’t fold enough better hands (Ax/Kx, two-pair, sets) and will often get called; checking gets to showdown cheaply and wins when he’s bluffing or has a weaker one-pair. I’ll check and take this to showdown rather than bloating the pot OOP with a vulnerable second pair.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (Queen) on a board of As-Kd-9h-7d-4h. My opponent, Minni, has check-called both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a marginal made hand like a weaker Ace (AJ, AT, A8) or potentially a King that hit on the turn. Since she checked the river as well, it is unlikely she improved to two pair or better (as she likely would have raised the turn or led the river to get value). I will place a value bet of 1400, which is exactly half the pot. This sizing is designed to look like a standard value bet that can be called by her 'bluff-catching' range of weaker Aces, while allowing me to fold comfortably if she responds with a check-raise, which would signify a slow-played monster or a backed-door two pair.

Fold. On the river I only have second pair (9♠) and my turn straight outs missed — there are no more cards to improve. Flash’s line (open to 300, c‑bet 300, turn bet 800, river bet 1400) is a steady value/pressure line that heavily contains A‑x/K‑x and other value hands; his river sizing (~1/3 pot) is engineered to get called by worse one‑pair hands. Calling 1,400 to win ~5,600 (post‑call) requires ~25% equity; I beat primarily bluffs and a small portion of his worse pairs, which I assign well below that. Raising OOP with a vulnerable single pair would be spewy and won’t fold enough better hands. Fold to preserve chips and wait for a clearer spot.

Heads-up from the button (I’m the small blind) with 7♦9♦ — a very playable hand in heads-up where I’ll have postflop position. Pot is small (150) and calling only 50 to see a flop is cheap, but raising to the min (200 total) applies pressure to the big blind, often takes the pot preflop, and lets me play postflop with initiative and position when called. Stack is deep (21250), so implied odds and postflop playability justify an aggressive open here. Plan: open-raise to 200; fold to a large 3-bet, but continue postflop versus a single caller with standard c-bets and selective barrels.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 7h, a solid suited connector. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Button. I need to call 100 more into a pot that will be 400 total. This gives me excellent pot odds (4:1), requiring only 20% equity to justify a call. 97s is a hand that plays very well post-flop, especially in a heads-up scenario. Given my recent momentum in winning the last few hands, a simple call allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's equity without bloating the pot out of position.

I have bottom pair (Sevens) on a board with several overcards and potential draws (straight and clubs). While I have some showdown value and a backdoor heart flush draw, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor with bottom pair is generally unwise. I will check to see how Minni acts, intending to call a standard continuation bet given my pair and potential to improve.

Heads-up on the button with 9♦7♦ on Q♥7♣T♣. I flopped a middle pair (sevens) with a reasonable kicker and have position — opponent checked, showing a wide range that includes many air hands and hands that missed this board. Standard play is to continuation-bet to take down the pot and get value from worse pairs, tens, and some floats. The Q and T are worrying (Qx/Tx beat me) so I don’t want to bloat the pot too much, but I want meaningful fold equity against overcards and draws. I’ll make a c-bet ~60% pot (250). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and give up to big aggression (check-raise) from him; if he check-raises large I’ll assume a strong Q/T/club-hand and fold this marginal pair. Bet 250 to apply pressure while keeping options open.

I have bottom pair with a Nine kicker and a backdoor heart flush draw. Minni's bet of 250 into a pot of 400 is a standard continuation bet on a board that contains several draws and overcards. While my hand is relatively weak, the pot odds (3.6:1) are sufficient to justify a call to see if the turn improves my hand or if Minni slows down on an unfavorable card. I will check-call here and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card is the Five of Clubs, which completes a potential flush. My bottom pair (Sevens) remains vulnerable and has not improved. Given the board texture and my position, checking is the best course of action. I want to control the pot and see if Minni continues her aggression or checks back, which would provide more information about the strength of her hand.

I flopped middle pair (7♦9♦ on Q♥7♣T♣) and c-bet the flop. Turn 5♣ makes the board Tc-7c-5c with a queen — three clubs now on board, which hugely lowers the equity of my second pair. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn) is consistent with a wide range: made clubs (now a flush), Qx/Tx, medium pairs/pocket pairs, or a float. Betting here would bloat the pot and get value mostly from hands that already beat me or commit me against a club — and I have poor fold equity vs the hands that beat me. Pot control is the correct line: keep the pot manageable, realize showdown value, and force Flash to show aggression first. Plan: check back the turn; if he bets the river I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his line (fold to large river aggression, call smaller bets for bluff-catching), if he checks back we go to showdown.

On this river, the King of Hearts is another overcard to my pair of Sevens, and there are already a Queen and Ten on the board, along with three clubs representing a possible flush. My pair of Sevens has very little showdown value given this runout. However, Minni checked back the turn after betting the flop, which suggests she may have a marginal hand or a draw that hasn't fully realized. By checking the river, I am hoping to get to a cheap showdown or induce a check-back from a hand that might have me beat but isn't strong enough to value bet. If she bets, I will likely have to fold as my hand is now essentially a bluff catcher against a range that includes many better pairs and completed draws.

I hold second pair (7♦9♦) on Q♥7♣T♣5♣-K♥. After c-betting the flop and getting a call, the turn brought a third club which seriously hurts my equity vs club two‑card combos; Flash’s passive line (call flop, check turn, check river) is consistent with Qx, Tx, a club flush, or medium pairs/weak made hands. Betting the river would have very thin value (will be called by hands that beat me) and little fold equity against a passive caller. Pot control / showdown value is optimal here — check to show down. If Flash now bets a small amount I’ll consider a call as a bluff-catcher; fold to large river aggression.

I have been dealt a very strong premium pocket pair, Ten of Hearts and Ten of Clubs, on the Button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation. This is a top-tier starting hand. My standard open from the button has been 300 chips throughout the match, and I will maintain that sizing to remain balanced while building a pot with a significant equity advantage. Pocket Tens are well ahead of Minni's random big blind range.

Heads-up vs the Button who opened to 300 from the SB. I have AcQs — a very strong hand heads-up that dominates a large portion of the raiser’s opening range (Kx, Qx, medium pairs, many weaker aces and broadways). Calling 200 to play OOP lets him realize equity cheaply but forfeits initiative; a small flat will lead to difficult postflop decisions out of position. I want to 3‑bet for value and fold equity to isolate his wide SB opens and price out speculative hands (connectors/suited cards). I size to 1200 — big enough to get folds from weaker hands and to extract value from Kx/Qx/small pairs, but small enough relative to our deep stacks to keep multi-street play profitable. Plan if called: continue as the aggressor on A/Q high flops and proceed cautiously if facing heavy 4‑bet pressure (fold or decide based on 4‑bet size and reads).

I have been dealt pocket Tens (Th, Tc) on the button. This is a premium hand in a heads-up match. Minni has 3-bet me to 1,200, which is a strong 4x sizing. However, given the high level of aggression in this match and the strength of my hand, I believe a 4-bet is the best course of action for both value and protection. A 4-bet to 2,800 puts the pressure back on Minni, allows me to take control of the pot in position, and extracts value from lower pairs or high-card hands that might be 3-betting light.

Heads‑up vs a small‑sized 4‑bet to 2800. I hold AcQs — a top‑tier heads‑up hand that does well against a polarized 4‑bet range but can be behind premium aces/pairs (AA/KK/QQ/AK). Calling the extra 1600 (total 2800) gives me ~2.5:1 pot odds (need ~28.6% equity) and keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks. A shove or large 5‑bet here is too polarizing and unnecessarily commits me out of position against hands that dominate me; a small 5‑bet also risks a jam and I’d often be flipping or behind. Plan after calling: continue aggressively on A/Q high flops (c‑bet/raise for value), proceed cautiously on dry low flops and fold to heavy postflop all‑in pressure when I miss. Therefore I will call to see a flop and realize equity.

I have A♣Q♠ on 3♣‑6♠‑Q♥ — top pair, top kicker out of position against a button who 4‑bet preflop. His small 4‑bet sizing is somewhat polarized but still contains a lot of hands I beat (weaker Qx, KQ, some Kx, medium pairs) as well as a few premiums (AA/KK/QQ/AK). This is an A/Q‑high flop where I want to extract value and deny equity to backdoor draws while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll make a modest value bet (~1/3 pot) — 2,000 into 5,600 — which gets called by worse queens and many broadway/medium pair hands and charges club/spade backdoors. If he raises substantially I’ll re‑evaluate (a large shove often indicates a nut/near‑nut holding and I’ll fold or proceed based on sizing); if he calls I’ll continue to extract on safe turn cards and pot‑control if the board becomes dangerous.

In this 4-bet pot, the flop comes Queen-high, meaning I have a strong overpair to the low cards (3 and 6) but am second-best to any Queen in Minni's range. Minni's lead (donk bet) for 2,000 into a 5,600 pot is an unusual line after calling a 4-bet. It could represent a Queen (like AQ or KQ) or a medium pair (like JJ, 99, or 88) trying to define the hand, or even a semi-bluff with AK/AJ. Folding is too weak given the pot odds (3.8:1) and the strength of my hand. Raising would likely only be called by hands that beat me (AQ, AA, KK, JJ). Therefore, calling is the best option to keep the pot manageable, maintain position, and allow Minni to continue with her bluffs or inferior hands on the turn.

I have AcQs OOP with top pair/top kicker on 3c-6s-Qh-Jc. Effective stacks (~13.9k) and the action (I 3‑bet pre, he 4‑bet small and called my flop C‑bet) put Flash on a polarized but call‑wide 4‑bet/call range that includes many worse Qx, some Jx/medium pairs, and the premium combos (AA/KK/QQ/AK). The turn J is a potentially helpful card for Jx and two‑pair combos, but it doesn’t change that a lot of his calling‐range on the flop still has worse queens and hands I want to charge. Checking gives him free cards and misses value; betting protects my hand and prices out turn/river equity for draws while extracting from worse Qx and some Jx. I’ll take the initiative and make a value/protection bet about 1/2 pot (4800). Plan: if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and continue extracting on safe rivers or pot‑control on scary rivers; if he check‑raises big/all‑in (polarized to very strong), I will respect that line and fold rather than commit with a hand that’s often dominated.

On the turn, the Jack of Clubs is a very problematic card for my pocket Tens. It is the second overcard to my pair on a Queen-high board. In a 4-bet pot, Minni's range for calling pre-flop and then leading out (donking) on both the flop and the turn is extremely strong. Her range likely includes hands like AQ, KQ, and now JJ or AJ which improved on the turn. Even if she is semi-bluffing with a hand like AK, she has significant equity against me. By betting 4,800 into a 9,600 pot, she is putting me in a position where I will likely have to face an all-in bet on the river. Given that the Jack connects so well with the range of hands that call a 4-bet, my Tens have plummeted in value. Folding allows me to preserve my remaining 13,900 chips and wait for a more favorable spot rather than risking a large portion of my stack on a marginal second-pair hand.

Heads-up on the button with 6♠6♦. With only one opponent and deep stacks, a pocket pair is worth raising to take the initiative, get value from worse hands, and retain set-mining implied odds. The pot is 150 and it costs 50 to call; I'll open to 300 total (≈3x BB). That sizing applies pressure, narrows Flash’s calling range, and keeps post‑flop play straightforward: c‑bet dry boards, check/set-mine on coordinated boards, and fold to very large 3-bets while considering calls to small 3-bets for implied odds.

I am holding 4-3 offsuit in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call Minni's 3x open, 4-3 offsuit realizes its equity very poorly out of position. Given Minni's recent aggression and my need to stabilize after the significant loss in Hand #106, I will choose the disciplined path and fold this hand to wait for a better spot.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build a pot for value and maintain aggression. Given my previous raising patterns in this match, a standard raise to 300 is appropriate.

Heads‑up vs the button/SB who opened to 300 (pot 400). I hold Q♠7♠ in the big blind and must call 200 more (300 total). With deep stacks and a wide opening range from the button, Q7s is a defendable, playable hand — suited, a decent high‑card blocker, and it has reasonable equity vs a wide raising range. The pot odds (200 to win 400 → ~33% break‑even) and strong implied odds on deep stacks make a flat call the right choice. 3‑betting would polarize my range and expose me to 4‑bets or awkward OOP post‑flop decisions with a marginal hand. I’ll call to see a flop and then play the hand based on texture: press when I hit top pair/strong draws, otherwise control the pot or fold to heavy aggression.

Heads‑up versus a wide button open who then checked the flop. I have top pair (Q♠7♠) on A♥Q♥4♦ — a decent holding heads‑up but with a weak kicker and several A‑x combos that beat me. The flop has two hearts but villain needs both hole cards to have a meaningful heart draw, so that’s a smaller concern than being dominated by an Ace. Against a checked‑back, wide opening range I want value and some protection, so I’ll C‑bet about half the pot to get called by worse queens/Kx and to charge any backdoor draws. Bet ~300 into 600. If I get a large raise I’ll assume strength (A/set) and fold; if I’m called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and continue for value on blank cards, but proceed cautiously on a heart or other scary card.

I have flopped Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King on an Ace-Queen-Four board. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into the 600 pot. This action often represents a marginal Ace, a Queen, or a draw looking to set their own price. Since I hold the best possible one-pair hand and there are several drawing possibilities (hearts, JT), I want to raise for value and protection. Raising here forces weaker Aces and draws to pay to continue. I will raise to 900 total.

I c-bet ~300 on A♥ Q♥ 4♦ for value/protection and Villain responded with a large check‑raise to 900. That puts 1800 in the pot and costs me 600 to continue (≈25% break‑even). A cold check‑raise of this size from the button after opening wide is weighted toward strong value (lots of A‑x, some sets, very few pure bluffs) — my Q7s only blocks a small portion of his combos and my kicker is weak. Against that polarized/value line my equity is likely well under the 25% needed, and any raise would commit me OOP with a marginal hand into a player who has shown aggression. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button with K♠J♥ — a strong, playable hand with positional advantage. With deep stacks (255bb) I want initiative and fold equity, so I should raise rather than limp/call. Opening to 3x the big blind (300 total) is a standard sizing: it builds the pot with a hand that plays well postflop, discourages the BB from seeing a cheap flop with weak holdings, and leaves plenty of room to maneuver on later streets. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and range; otherwise I’ll proceed to play in position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-2 suited. Minni has opened for a standard 3x raise (300). While 9-2 suited has some playability in a heads-up environment, it is at the very bottom of my defending range, and I am out of position against a player who has shown considerable aggression and currently holds a significant chip lead. I have been playing reasonably well and don't feel the need to defend such a weak hand and risk playing a bloated pot out of position. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous spot.

In a heads-up situation, T4o is a very weak hand, sitting in the bottom 25-30% of starting hands. While the button (SB) has a significant advantage by being in position post-flop, playing such a weak hand against a chip leader like Minni, who has shown frequent aggression and willingness to defend, is risky. At this stage of the tournament, with 40 hands remaining and a stack deficit, it's better to preserve chips and wait for a more playable hand than to attempt a marginal steal or get involved in a pot with poor equity and bad playability.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve posted the 50 SB) with Ad7c — a strong heads-up hand that plays well in position. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to call, but raising gains fold equity, defines my range, and denies cheap equity to speculative hands from the BB. I’ll open to 300 total (≈3x BB): a standard size that gets value from worse aces/broadways and applies pressure without committing a large portion of my deep stack. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold most of the time with this weak kicker; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and extract value or fold to heavy resistance when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with 93o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. 93o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, even with the decent pot odds provided by the call. Given the deep stacks and my current position, there is no need to defend such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

In a heads-up situation, King-Six offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button. I have been consistently opening to 300 chips in this position throughout the match. This sizing puts pressure on the big blind while allowing me to play a pot with a card as high as a King. Given the current stack sizes and Minni's aggressive playstyle, I want to maintain my standard opening range to avoid being exploited.

Heads‑up vs the button who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with J♦5♦ — suited, but a marginal hand. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (300 total committed including my BB). Against a wide heads‑up opening range J5s has reasonable equity and good postflop playability (backdoor flush + pair/straight potential). Pot odds (~3:1) and the ability to see a flop cheaply make calling the standard defensive play here. I don’t want to 3‑bet with this weak holding out of position (min‑raising to 500 is too small to credibly represent a strong range and a larger 3‑bet would be overcommitting with a marginal hand). Plan: call to see the flop; continue only if I hit a strong pair, strong diamond draw, or get favorable action; otherwise keep the pot under control and fold to heavy aggression.

I have top pair (J♦) with a weak 5 kicker on 7♥ J♠ A♠. Against a wide heads‑up opening range this hand has decent value but is vulnerable to any A‑x and to two‑pair/sets. I want to extract value from worse Jx and broadway hands while denying free cards and keeping the pot manageable. A half‑pot protection/value bet (~300 into 600) achieves that — it folds out some overcards and gets called by worse hands, while allowing me to fold to a strong check‑raise (likely an Ace or better). Plan: bet 300, call small calls, and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to a large raise unless I pick up a clear read or improvement.

On a flop of Ace-Jack-Seven, my King-Six offsuit has no pair and no draw. Minni leading into the pre-flop raiser on an Ace-high board is a strong signal that she has at least a piece of the board or is trying to take the pot away immediately. Since I have no showdown value and very little equity to improve to a winning hand, folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with Ks6c — marginal but perfectly openable. In heads-up play you should be opening a wide range from the button to seize initiative and use position postflop. I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to apply pressure and extract fold equity; the size is big enough to discourage loose calls but small relative to our deep stack. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll likely fold this marginal holding; if he calls I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot when I miss. Note: 300 is the total I’ll have committed preflop (I’ve already posted 50 SB, so I’m putting in an extra 250).

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 6d. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 300. Given our current heads-up situation and stack depths (I have 137.5 BBs), 76o is a reasonable hand to defend against a standard 3x open. Minni has been quite active, and folding here would be too tight. I will call the additional 200 to see a flop and look for opportunities to outplay her or hit a strong connected board.

I have flopped middle pair with my seven on a K-9-7 rainbow board. This is a respectable hand in a heads-up pot, but I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Standard play here is to check to the raiser to see how they interact with the board. If Minni bets, I have enough value to call at least one street, especially since she has been active and could be continuation betting with air or a draw.

Heads-up, I raised preflop and got called. The flop Kc 9h 7d gives me top pair (Ks) with a weak kicker — still a very strong holding in heads-up against a wide calling range. Flash checked; the board is relatively dry (only one club, no straight completes), so a value c-bet is correct to extract from worse Kx, middle pairs and to fold out pure overcards. I size to ~2/3 pot (bet 400 into 600) to get value while denying good odds to draws/floaters. If Flash check-raises big I will sharply re-evaluate and likely fold to avoid getting stacked with a weak kicker; if he calls I’ll control the pot on the turn and re-assess based on the card and his action.

I have flopped middle pair with my seven on a K-9-7 rainbow board. In a heads-up match, middle pair is a significant hand and certainly strong enough to continue against a single continuation bet. Minni's bet size is 40% of the pot, which is a standard sizing that could represent a wide range including air, draws, or value hands. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like a King or a better Nine), so calling is the most profitable line to keep her range wide and see a turn card.

The turn card is a deuce, which is a relative blank and doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still hold bottom pair (sevens). Against an aggressive opponent like Minni, checking is the best play to control the pot and potentially induce a bluff or see a cheap river. I'm not strong enough to bet for value, and leading out would likely only result in being called by better hands or raised.

I have top pair (K♠) with a weak kicker (6♣). Heads-up this is a strong holding vs a wide calling range — Flash check-called my 400 flop c-bet, which often contains worse Kx, second pairs (9x/7x), and occasional floats. The turn 2♣ is a blank: it doesn’t give me any additional equity (no realistic flush draw with one club in hand and only two clubs on board), and Flash’s check once again signals pot control or a medium-strength hand. I want to extract value and deny a free river card, so I’ll bet for value here. Betting ~2/3 pot (900 into the 1400 pot) charges worse pairs and Kx hands while remaining small enough to fold to a large check-raise; if Flash check-raises very large I will heavily favor folding given my weak kicker. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river and either value-bet safe runouts or check back on dangerous cards (A/K/9/7).

On the turn, the board is 7d-9h-Kc-2c. I hold bottom pair (sevens) with a weak kicker (six). Minni has fired a second barrel of 900 into a 1400 pot (approximately 64% pot). While Minni is aggressive, calling a significant bet out of position with bottom pair is generally unprofitable, as I am likely behind a King, a Nine, or even a better Seven. Furthermore, many of her potential bluffs still have significant equity with overcards. I will fold to preserve my stack and look for a better spot to commit chips.

I am on the button (small blind) with Qs 2s. This is a speculative hand that plays reasonably well in heads-up play, especially when suited. The current pot is 150, and it only costs me 50 more to call, or I can raise to put pressure on Minni. Minni has a significant chip lead (26,750 vs my 13,100), but I need to maintain an active presence to avoid being blinded down. Given our history, Minni has been quite aggressive. I'll make a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand pre-flop, as Qs 2s is ahead of a wide range of random hands and has flush/overcard potential.

Facing a button open to 300 heads-up with J♠9♥ in the big blind. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total). Against a standard HU open this hand has good playability (can make top pair on a J, second pair, and straight/ backdoor draws) and I get reasonable pot odds to see a flop. Flash has been opening to 300 frequently, so flat-calling keeps their range wide and lets me realize equity out of position without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. A small 3-bet to 500 achieves little and J9o is not strong enough for a standard 3-bet range here. I’ll call to 300 and proceed based on the flop texture.

I flat-called preflop to keep my range wide and realize equity out of position. On Q♦‑7♥‑A♥ with J♠9♥ I have no pair and only a weak backdoor heart/runner‑runner outs; this flop heavily favors the opener’s range (lots of Ax/Qx and blocked A/Q combos). Leading out OOP is thin and won’t fold enough of their range, so I’ll check for pot control. Plan: fold to large, polarizing aggression; call a small c‑bet to realize backdoor equity and look to float/steal the pot on the turn if they show weakness or I improve.

On a board of Qd-7h-Ah, I have second pair with a weak kicker (Queens, 2 kicker). While I was the pre-flop aggressor, the presence of the Ace makes this a precarious spot. Minni checked to me. Checking back is a strong play here to control the pot size and realize my equity. It avoids a potential check-raise from an Ace or a better Queen and keeps the pot manageable for a medium-strength hand. I can re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and Minni's action.

I called preflop to keep my range wide and checked the flop for pot control with J♠9♥ on Q♦‑7♥‑A♥. Flash checked the flop and again checked the turn (K♦) — that’s weakness/pot‑control from the preflop aggressor. I have no pair and only four outs to a Ten (~8–9% equity), so I shouldn’t bloat the pot if Flash shows strength. However their passive line gives good fold equity now, so the best play is a semi‑bluff on the turn to try to take down the 600 pot. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (400) to represent a K/Q/A and force lots of marginal Qx/Kx and small pairs to fold. If Flash raises I’ll fold (we have too little equity to continue); if called I still have the 10 outs to hit the straight on the river and will plan my river play based on the card and opponent’s action.

The Turn card (King) makes the board extremely dangerous. I have a Queen with the lowest possible kicker (2). On a board of A-K-Q-7, I am behind any Ace, any King, and every other Queen. While Minni might be betting a draw or bluffing because I checked the flop, the range of hands that beat me is too wide to justify calling 400 chips. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with K♦J♦ — a strong, playable hand with good equity and nut-flush potential. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to take the initiative and build a pot where my range (and position postflop) is advantaged. Flash has shown a wide defending/opening range, so a standard ~3xBB sizing is appropriate: it extracts value from worse kings/queens/broadways, makes draws pay to see a flop, and keeps the pot manageable versus aggression. Plan: c-bet favorable flops (K/Q/J or diamond textures), control the pot on dry/hostile boards and re-evaluate versus a 3-bet (fold or call depending on sizing and read). Raise to 300 (total) to apply pressure and play the hand in position.

In Hand #115, I'm in the Big Blind with 9h 3s. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. My hand is very weak, significantly below the range of hands that should be defended in a heads-up situation against a 3x raise. Looking at recent hand history, Minni has been quite active and aggressive, but defending with 9-high out of position is a losing strategy long-term. My stack is around 127.5 big blinds, and I can afford to wait for a better spot rather than calling with a marginal-to-poor hand and playing a pot out of position.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with King-Deuce offsuit. In heads-up play, Kx is a strong enough hand to enter the pot with a raise from the button to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the hand. While K2o is a marginal hand, the blinds are 50/100, and I want to maintain an aggressive profile. I will raise to 300 total.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300. I hold QTs — a strong, playable hand heads-up with good equity against a wide opening range. Calling 200 is reasonable, but Flash is aggressive and I’ll be out of position postflop; I prefer to apply pressure preflop, get fold equity, and define the hand. A 3-bet to 1000 is large enough to fold many marginal opens and get value from worse broadways/pairs while keeping the pot manageable if he calls. Plan: if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and control size OOP; I’ll re-evaluate/fold to a large 4-bet depending on sizing and reads.

In this heads-up situation, I opened with K2o from the button/small blind for 300. Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. While I would have position post-flop, K2o is a very weak hand to defend against a significant 3-bet. Minni has shown both aggression and the ability to hold strong hands in previous 3-bet pots (like Hand #106). Since my hand has poor playability and is likely dominated by her 3-betting range, I will fold and preserve my stack of approximately 125 big blinds for better opportunities.

I’m on the button (small blind) with A♠T♠ — a very strong heads-up hand and great post-flop equity. Limping is too passive heads-up; I want to seize initiative and use position. The pot is 150 and the minimum raise is 200, but a standard 3xBB open to 300 is cleaner: it builds the pot, folds out weaker hands, and avoids giving good odds to speculative holdings. Against Flash’s frequent 300 open-raising behavior, sizing to 300 is in line with the table and keeps my range strong; if I get 3-bet I can comfortably continue with deep stacks. Raise to 300.
