I am in the Big Blind with King-five offsuit (K5o). Minni has been very aggressive from the Small Blind/Button, raising frequently and putting on significant post-flop pressure. While K5o has some high-card value in a heads-up situation, it plays poorly out of position and is easily dominated by Minni's raising range (which likely includes better Kings). Given the current trend of Minni winning most small-to-medium pots by being the aggressor, I'll choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot where I have either position or a stronger holding to combat her aggression. I still have over 120 big blinds, so there is no need to defend with the bottom of my range right now.

In a heads-up situation, 9-6 offsuit is a marginal but playable hand from the button/small blind. Standard strategy suggests opening a wide range of hands to maintain pressure. Given the current stack depths (Flash: 12,300, Minni: 27,550), I will put in a standard raise to 300. This allows me to take control of the pot early or fold if faced with heavy resistance, while also seeing flops when called. Minni has been quite aggressive in recent hands, but a 3x open is a standard way to test the waters.

Facing a button open to 300 heads-up while I’m in the big blind with K♠6♣. This is a marginal but playable hand against a wide SB opening range. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 more to call (total commit 300). I’ll call (total 300) to keep the pot manageable out of position, realize equity, and see a flop — I don’t want to inflate the pot with a weak offsuit hand by 3-betting. Plan to proceed aggressively on favourable flops (king or good textures) and fold to heavy action when I miss.

I completely missed this A♠? (Ah)-T♢-8♥ flop with K♠6♣ — no pair, no heart, no meaningful backdoor. The button’s opening range is wide but is heavily weighted toward A‑x and broadways that connect with this board, so I’m frequently dominated. Pot is small (600) and I don’t want to bloat it out of position with a weak holding. I’ll check to control the pot, see what Flash does, and only continue to the turn if I pick up a king or if he gives a very small probe bet I can justify calling as a float; I’ll fold to any sizable, value/polarized c‑bet.

On a board of Ah-Td-8h, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is a standard play here to represent an Ace or a strong Ten. This board is quite coordinated, so a bet might fold out some of Minni's air or weaker draws. If called, I can look for a 7 on the turn or decide whether to continue the semi-bluff. If raised, I can easily let go of 9-high.

I checked the flop to keep the pot small and see what Flash would do. With K♠6♣ on A♥–T♦–8♥ I have no pair, no heart backdoor and very little showdown equity. Facing a 300 c‑bet into a 900 pot I need ~25% equity to justify a call; my raw outs (three kings + three sixes) give roughly a 24% chance to improve by the river and many of those improvements still lose to common c‑bet hands (A‑x, T‑x, pairs). Raising out of position with this holding is pointless. With a large stack advantage and plenty of hands left, this is a fold — conserve chips for better spots.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the SB) with 4♣5♣ offsuit (4h5c): in heads-up play this is a playable, speculative hand because I have position postflop and the stacks are deep. Open-raising is standard here to take the initiative, punish the big blind’s wide defending range, and build pots when I hit. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (to 300 total) — big enough to apply pressure and get value/isolate weaker hands, but small relative to stacks so I can continue postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand (it doesn’t play well multi-way or vs heavy 3-bet aggression); if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable low/peel flops and control pot size on coordinated/high boards. Overall aggression and position maximize long-run EV with this holding, so raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o, a hand that is quite weak. Minni, who is currently the chip leader, has opened for 3x (300). While I am getting 2:1 on a call, T4o doesn't play well post-flop, especially against an opponent who has been aggressive and has a significant stack advantage. My goal is to maximize my stack over the remaining 32 hands, and defending wide with weak holdings against the chip leader is not a high-EV strategy in this specific context. I will fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

I am on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation with Jack-Nine offsuit. This is a solid hand to open in a heads-up format to maintain aggression and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or post-flop. I have a healthy stack of 125 big blinds, and though Minni has a significant chip lead, my strategy should be to play standard winning poker. Raising to 300 is a standard open size I've been using frequently in this session.

Heads-up vs a 3BB open from the button, Q♠7♠ is a defendable, playable hand — suited queen with decent blocker value and good implied odds with deep stacks (I have ~273bb, opponent ~122bb). Calling 200 more (total 300 committed) gives ~2:1 immediate pot odds and keeps my range wide; 3-betting OOP with Q7s is too thin (would create tough postflop spots and the min-raise to 500 won’t fold out enough of his range). Plan: see a flop, continue with top pair, strong draws or good equity, fold or give up to heavy pressure when completely missed.

Check. I have a marginal middle pair (7s) on A♥9♥7♣ — dominated by most Ax and many hands in the preflop raiser’s wide heads‑up range. Betting OOP here bloats the pot with a hand that has poor protection and is often behind; checking keeps the pot small, lets me realize equity, and induces bluffs from missed broadways/heart draws. If Flash leads small I’ll usually call to see a turn (I beat many bluffs and worse pairs); if he barrels large I’ll fold to avoid getting stacked with a second‑pair hand. If he checks back I’m happy to see a turn and re-evaluate (look to value-bet small vs 9x/7x or bluff-catch selectively). Pot control and position discipline maximize long‑term EV here.

I have middle pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker on an Ace-high board. While my hand is relatively strong in a heads-up context, an Ace is a significant part of Minni's calling range from the Big Blind. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and avoid a potential check-raise. This also allows me to see the turn for free and evaluate how Minni plays the rest of the hand, as I have sufficient showdown value to continue.

I hold Q♠7♠ — second pair (7s) on A♥9♥7♣2♣. Flash was the preflop raiser and checked the flop; his range is wide and contains many A‑x and broadways that dominate me. With deep stacks and out of position, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal pair. Checking controls the pot, induces bluffs from missed hearts/overcards, and lets me call small probes while avoiding getting stacked by big turn/river aggression. If Flash bets a small probe I’ll usually call to realize equity; I’ll fold to heavy pressure. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river and look to extract small value or check again. Action: check to keep the pot under control and preserve fold equity vs big bets.

I have second pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker. After checking back the flop for pot control on an Ace-high board, Minni has checked again on the turn. Her double check suggests she likely does not have an Ace, or is playing very passively with a marginal hand or a draw. With the turn adding a second flush draw (clubs) to the existing heart draw, my hand is now more vulnerable. I will bet 300 (half the pot) to extract value from weaker pairs like a Seven or small pocket pairs, and to charge any potential draws she may be holding. If she calls, I will likely look to check back most rivers to reach showdown.

Flash was the preflop raiser, checked the flop and now fires a small turn probe (300 into a 900 pot ≈ 1/3 pot). I have Q♠7♠ — second pair (7s) with a decent kicker for heads‑up play. Raising OOP with this hand is too thin (I lack fold equity vs Ax and stronger hands and would create tough spots), and folding to a 1/3‑pot probe surrenders too much equity — the price to see a river (300 into 900) gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds and I beat many of his floats/worse pairs. I’ll call the 300 to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the river: fold to a large raise/overbet, call smaller rivers where I still beat bluffs/worse pairs, and extract small value when appropriate.

I improved to top pair on the river (Q♠7♠ with Q♦ on board). Flash (the preflop raiser) checked the flop, fired a small turn probe and now checked the river — a line consistent with a wide, medium-strength/air-heavy range (floats, Kx, 9x, 7x, some A‑x pot‑controllers). I want to extract value from worse pairs and thin callables while keeping the pot manageable versus A‑x. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse and folds out pure air, but leaves me an easy fold if he raises big (a large raise almost always represents A‑x or a Q with a better kicker that beats me). Action: small value bet to extract and protect pot control.

On the river, the board is Ah-9h-7c-2c-Qd. I have a pair of Nines with a Jack kicker. Minni has led for 400 into a 1200 pot, which is a one-third pot bet. This small sizing is often used as a 'blocking bet' or a thin value bet with a marginal hand like a Seven or a weak Nine, but it could also be a value bet from a Queen she just paired or an Ace she played slowly. However, given the excellent pot odds (4:1), I only need to be right 20% of the time to make this call profitable. In a heads-up match, folding a middle pair to such a small bet is generally too tight, especially since she could be bluffing with missed heart or club draws. I will call to keep her honest and see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 2♣9♠ — a weak, marginal hand but still playable in heads-up. In this spot opening a wide raising range from the button is standard: it buys the pot often and gives me the initiative while preserving position postflop. The pot is 150 and calling costs only 50, but a min-raise to 200 (total) is a small, low-risk raise (extra 150) that extracts fold equity and keeps pots manageable if called. Plan: raise to 200 to try to take the pot now; if Flash 3-bets heavily I’ll fold this hand (poor playability vs big aggression); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously in position—c-bet favorable flops, check/control on dry or dangerous textures.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc5h. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has made a minimum raise to 200 (100 more for me to call). While J5o is a weak hand, the pot odds are excellent (100 to win a pot of 300, meaning I need 25% equity). In heads-up play, defending the Big Blind against a min-raise with a wide range is standard. I will call to see a flop, but I must play cautiously if I don't improve significantly.

I have flopped middle pair (5s) with a Jack kicker on a 6-5-2 board. In a heads-up situation, middle pair is a strong enough hand to continue with, but it is best played as a check-call to control the pot size and allow the opponent to continue bluffing with their wide range. Leading out might force out hands I beat and get called only by better hands.

Heads-up I was the preflop aggressor and I flop a pair of deuces with 2♣9♠ on 5♣‑2♥‑6♣. This is well inside my c-bet range: I have position, a live hand that beats many of BB’s air/overcards, and some backdoor club equity while also blocking a few club combinations. I want to apply pressure to take the pot now and deny equity to overcards and straight/club draws. I’ll size to ~60% pot (250 into 400) — big enough to get value and fold equity but not so large that I overcommit if I face strong resistance. Plan: if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn; if he check‑raises big I’ll typically release this weak pair.

I have flopped middle pair (Fives) with a Jack kicker on a relatively low, semi-coordinated board (6-5-2 with two clubs). Minni, the chip leader, has made a standard continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 400. In heads-up play, middle pair is a solid hand to continue with, especially when getting nearly 2.6-to-1 odds on a call. Raising here would likely only be called by better hands (6x, 77+, or strong draws), so calling is the most effective way to control the pot size and keep Minni's bluffs or weaker hands in the range. I will call and see how the turn develops.

On the turn, the 3c completes several potential draws, including a flush and a straight (any 4). My pair of fives is now a marginal hand on a very coordinated board. However, I do hold the Jack of Clubs, which is a strong blocker to a flush. Checking is the most appropriate move here to control the pot size and observe Minni's action. I am looking to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate based on the size of a potential bet from Minni.

Turn (3c) completes a three‑club board and greatly increases the chance that any club in Flash’s hand is now a made flush. I currently have a marginal pair (2c9s = pair of twos) plus one club (four clubs to a flush), but only ~9 outs (~9/46 ≈ 19%) to improve on the river and I’m vulnerable to any higher pair, two‑pair, trips or a made flush. Flash’s check could be weakness or a trap with a made club; betting here would often get called by hands that beat me and will fold few of the better parts of his range, so my fold equity is limited. With position and a small pot relative to stacks, the prudent line is pot control — check back the turn, avoid bloating the pot when I’m likely behind to some of his calls, and see a cheap river to make a clearer decision (call small river bets, fold to large aggression).

The river card completes a straight on the board (2-3-4-5-6). Currently, both players are playing the board for a straight unless one of us holds a Seven (for a higher straight) or two clubs (for a flush). Minni checked back on the turn, which may indicate she does not have a strong flush draw or a completed straight at that point. However, betting into a board that already contains a straight is generally unwise unless I have a Seven or a flush myself, as I would only be called by hands that beat me or split with me. Checking is the most logical play to reach a showdown and likely split the pot, while minimizing loss if Minni has improved to a superior hand.

Board is 5c‑2h‑6c‑3c‑4s — a 2‑3‑4‑5‑6 straight is on the board. My pair of twos is effectively dead: the best five‑card hand is already the board straight, and many hands that called the flop (4x/7x making straights on the river, trips, two‑club flushes, or slow‑played made hands) beat me. Flash checked the river, but betting here would be a thin bluff with very limited fold equity (I block some club combos with the 2c but not enough) and I’d simply get called by all better hands while folding out very little. Pot control and showdown are the prudent line — don’t bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I will check and take it to showdown.
QTo is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the average holding. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and maintain my aggressive strategy from the button.

Heads-up vs the Button's open to 300. I have 4♥7♥ — weak but suited with straight/flush potential and playable postflop. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 (immediate pot odds ≈33%), which is reasonable versus a wide heads‑up opening range. Stacks are deep enough for implied odds, and 3‑betting as a bluff is unnecessary and risky with this holding. I’ll call to see a flop and play postflop.

I have 7♥4♥ on 9♥ 2♥ 6♣ — a big heart draw (9 hearts left) plus extra pair outs (three 7s and three 4s). That’s ≈9 flush outs and ≈15 unique outs to improve by river (≈35% to hit by the river to a flush on two cards remaining, ≈54% to hit at least a pair/flush by river). I’m out of position vs the button and there’s no bet yet, so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than inflate it with a marginal semi‑bluff. I’ll check to keep lines simple: call a reasonable continuation bet to see the turn and reassess (or raise later only if I pick up additional information or a good blocker/line).

I have two overcards on a relatively disconnected board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will place a continuation bet after Minni checked to me. This bet aims to take down the pot immediately by forcing folds from weaker hands that missed the flop, while still maintaining equity if called.

Pot is 900 and I must call 300 (pot odds ≈25%). I have 7♥4♥ on 9♥ 2♥ 6♣: nine hearts remain (≈35% to make a flush by river) and three other 7s + three other 4s (6 more outs) for ~15 unique outs total (≈54% to hit at least a pair/flush by river). Versus a wide heads‑up c‑bet this is comfortably profitable to continue. I’m out of position so a raise here would bloat the pot and be more speculative; calling realizes equity, keeps bluffs/weaker hands in, and lets me reassess on the turn. Plan: call the 300 now; if I hit a heart or pair I’ll bet/raise for value, if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to heavy turn aggression.

I have 7♥4♥ on 9♥ 2♥ 6♣ 8♠ — a nut heart draw (9 outs) plus six pair outs for ~15 total outs, ≈15/46 ≈33% to improve on the river. Flash checked the turn after betting the flop, which reads as either weakness or pot control. I’m out of position and the pot (1,200) is small relative to the stacks, so I don’t want to bloat it with a marginal semi‑bluff that only has ~1/3 equity with one card to come. Checking preserves pot control, realizes my equity, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me fold to heavy river aggression if I miss — or extract value if I hit. Action: check.

The turn 8s makes the board more coordinated (9-8-6), which likely connects with many hands in Minni's big blind calling range. My Queen-high has no showdown value, but the board texture is risky for a second barrel since many of her flop-calling hands (like middle pairs or straight draws) have now improved or gained equity. I will check behind to control the pot size and see a free river card, potentially improving or finding a better spot to bluff if she checks again.

River pair: the 4♠ pairs my 4♥ and gives me a weak pair (4s) while my heart flush draw missed. Flash’s line (open preflop, c-bet flop, check turn) looks like a wide/capped range or pot‑control with a medium pair — there are still many hands that beat me (9x/6x/22/66) and many missed draws/bluffs I currently beat. Pot is only 1,200 and stacks are deep, so betting here risks folding out the very hands I want value from and can get raised by hands that already beat me. Best EV line is to keep the pot small and go to showdown: check and see if Flash bluffs or checks back. If he makes a small river bet I’ll call for showdown; I’ll fold to a large overbet/raise. Action: check.

On the river, the board is 9-2-6-8-4. I have Queen-high, which has very limited showdown value but might beat some of Minni's missed draws (like lower heart draws). Minni called the pre-flop raise and the flop bet but checked both the turn and the river. While she could be holding a weak pair that beats me, she is often sticky and rarely folds once she has invested in the pot, making a bluff attempt less likely to succeed. I will check behind and see if my Queen-high is enough to win the pot.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a wide range — 5♦6♠ is a playable connector with good postflop playability and equity against a wide BB defending range. I want initiative and to play the flop in position, so raising is better than limp/call. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure, charge the BB to see flops, and leave room to maneuver postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value or realize equity.

I am holding 6c 4h in the big blind. Minni, who is the chip leader, has opened for a standard 3x raise to 300. 64o is too weak to defend against a 3x open in a heads-up scenario where I am significantly outstacked. I will fold and preserve my chips for a more favorable hand.

King-Queen offsuit is a premium hand in heads-up play, especially from the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and build a pot with a hand that is well ahead of my opponent's range.

Heads-up facing a 3bb open to 300 from the button. Ts6c is marginal but much stronger heads-up than in a full ring. Calling the 200 more (300 total committed including my 100 BB) is cheap relative to both stacks (I’m 29k, opponent ~10.5k) and gives ~3:1 pot odds to see a flop against a wide button opening range. I don’t want to make a weak isolation raise or a light 3-bet with no blockers — those lines are unnecessary and riskier deep-stacked. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed: play aggressively if I pair the Ten or pick up strong equity, otherwise be cautious out of position and fold to heavy action on dry boards.

Heads-up vs a wide button opening range on a dry flop (2c 4c 9d). I missed — no pair — but I have one club (6c) and a Ten as a live overcard, plus runner-runner club/backdoor equity. Against a wide range a small continuation bet should pick up the pot often and charge hands that have some equity, while keeping the pot controllable out of position. I don't want to overcommit with a marginal hand; plan is to c-bet small, fold to a raise, and if called re-evaluate on the turn (value/press if I pair the Ten or improve, otherwise slow down). Bet ~1/3 pot to maximize fold equity and preserve stack.

I have two overcards (King and Queen) and a backdoor club flush draw with the Queen of clubs. Minni has led out with a small donk bet of 200 into a 600 pot. This small lead often indicates a marginal hand like a weak pair (9, 4, or 2) or a draw. Given my position and the pot odds (4:1), calling is the most profitable play to see how the turn develops and potentially realize my equity.

Turn pairs the board (4s) and I still have Ts6c — no pair, only a Ten overcard and a backdoor club that requires runner-runner. I c-bet small on the flop and was called, so Flash’s range is wide but contains a lot of hands that have decent showdown value now (9x, 2x, pocket pairs, and any 4x that becomes trips). The paired turn reduces my fold equity and makes a second barrel as a pure bluff thin and costly out of position. With a big stack advantage there’s no need to force the issue here. I’ll check to control the pot, see what line Flash takes, and only continue to the river if I get a cheap price or improve. If he leads out small I can call to see the river; if he overbets/shoves I’ll give up and preserve chips for better spots.

The turn 4 paired the board, and Minni has checked after her small flop lead. While I have overcards and a club flush draw, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to see a free river card to potentially improve to a top pair or a flush, while also maintaining some showdown value with King-high against her missed draws. I avoid the risk of a check-raise from a potentially stronger hand.

Heads-up vs a wide button raise/call line. I hold Ts6c on 2c 4c 9d 4s As — no pair, backdoor club missed. I was the flop aggressor (raised) and was called, then both checked the turn; that line shows a lot of medium-strength and busted hands in Flash’s checking range. A small-to-medium river bluff makes sense: it represents a hand that improved (A or a 4/9) and pressures many Kx/Qx/Jx and other missed hands to fold. Using the bluff math: a bet of 400 into a 1000 pot only needs Flash to fold >400/(1000+400) ≈ 29% to be immediately +EV, which is realistic given his passive turn check. The downside if called is small relative to our deep stack, and I’ll fold to any large raise since I have no showdown value. Bet 400 to try and take it down now.

On the river, the Ace of Spades is a significant scare card. Minni led the flop for a small amount, checked the turn when the board paired, and is now leading for 400 on the river Ace. While I have King-high, which beats some of her missed straight or club draws, it is highly likely she either has an Ace, a Nine, or even a Four. Given the board texture and the way the hand played out, my King-high has almost no showdown value against her range, and calling here would be a low-frequency winning play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range — position and initiative are worth a lot. 4c2c is a playable suited hand with backdoor flush/straight potential and good postflop playability in position. I want a raise size that applies pressure but is standard for this table, so I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300 total). If Flash 3-bets big I can fold this weak holding; if he calls I’ll play the flop in position and leverage fold equity and my implied odds with a flush/backdoor draw. Raising also prevents him from seeing cheap flops and keeps my overall range aggressive heads-up.

I have Queen-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind. In a heads-up situation, QTo is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard 3x button raise. While I could 3-bet for value or as a bluff, calling allows me to realize my equity and play a pot in position or out of position with a hand that flops well enough. Given the stack sizes and the opponent's aggressive history, calling is a standard and safe play here.

On a monochrome flop of 6s 3s Ts, I have flopped top pair with a Queen-high flush draw (Qs). This is an exceptionally strong hand in a heads-up match. As the Big Blind, I am first to act on the flop. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most balanced play here. It allows Minni to continue with her range, including bluffs or semi-bluffs with a single spade. If Minni bets, I have a very easy call or a potential check-raise depending on the bet size, but for now, checking is the best way to manage the pot and see how she reacts to this texture.

Heads-up, I opened a wide range from the button and Flash checked the flop. Board is 10s-6s-3s — a monotone spade flop that hits lots of defending hands (spade draws, some tens) but is otherwise fairly dry. My 4♣2♣ has no pair and only backdoor club potential, so I’m bluffing here to take advantage of position and the checking range. A sizable continuation bet denies correct pot odds to spade draws, folds out many overcards and small pairs, and keeps me in control of the hand. I’ll make a 2/3-pot c-bet (400 into 600). If he raises big I’ll fold this weak holding; if he calls I’ll reassess on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I have flopped top pair with the Queen-high flush draw on a monochrome board. This is a very powerful hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has made a standard continuation bet of 400 into a 600 pot. While a check-raise is a viable option for value and protection, calling is often superior here as it keeps Minni's range wide and allows her to continue bluffing on the turn with hands that have a single spade or air. It also ensures I can comfortably see a turn card with my significant equity without bloating the pot prematurely out of position.

The turn brings the Ace of Diamonds, which is a classic scare card that significantly improves the range of the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. I currently hold second pair (Tens) with a Queen-high flush draw. Leading into the Ace is risky because if Minni has an Ace, she will likely raise or call, and if she doesn't, I might force out her bluffs. By checking, I control the size of the pot and allow Minni to potentially continue bluffing or check back with a weaker range. Given my significant equity with the flush draw and pair, I am planning to call a reasonable bet, but checking is the best way to handle being out of position on this board texture.

I opened wide preflop and c‑bet the monotone spade flop to represent a range that contains spades, tens and strong broadway hands. I hold 4♣2♣ — essentially air now: with the A♦ on the turn my backdoor club flush is dead and my only realistic out is a 5 for the wheel (4 outs). Flash called the flop but checked the Ace on the turn, which looks like weakness (missed draws or marginal pairs) or pot‑control. I have almost no showdown value, so checking relinquishes the initiative and likely loses at showdown. A sizable turn barrel will credibly represent a strong hand (Ax, a ten, or a spade flush) and can fold out medium pairs and many non‑made/spade‑draw hands. I’ll bet to apply pressure but will not overcommit — 900 into a 1400 pot (~65%) gives good fold equity while leaving room to fold to a check‑raise. If Flash raises I’ll concede and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the Ace of Diamonds is a scare card that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. However, I still hold second pair (Tens) and a very strong Queen-high flush draw. Minni's bet of 900 into a 1400 pot gives me roughly 2.5:1 on a call. With 9 outs to a flush (approx 18-20% equity) plus potential outs for a Ten or Queen, and considering the implied odds if I hit my flush on the river, calling is the most mathematically and strategically sound decision. It allows me to realize my equity without bloating the pot further while out of position against an aggressive opponent.

On the river, the board is 6s 3s Ts Ad 9h. I have a pair of Tens with a Queen kicker (second pair). The river was a blank that did not complete my flush draw. Given the board has three spades and an Ace, my hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Aces, Flushes, or better). By checking, I control the size of the pot and can evaluate Minni's action. If she checks back, I may win the pot; if she bets, I will have to decide if her range contains enough bluffs to justify a call, but leading out is not the optimal strategy here.

Flash called both my flop and turn barrels, which indicates real showdown value (pairs, tens or a spade flush). The river 9♥ does not give my 4♣2♣ any meaningful improvement and I have almost zero showdown equity. To bluff profitably I’d need a high fold frequency (B/(P+B)) that I don’t expect from an opponent who has already shown willingness to call two streets. A small blocker bet is unlikely to fold enough of his range and a large bet would be chasing folds I probably won’t get. With a 3,200 pot and deep stacks, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play is to check and preserve chips; if he leads out or raises I can fold and avoid losing a bigger stack portion.

I am on the button with King-Trey offsuit in a heads-up situation. While K3o isn't a premium hand, in heads-up play, it is a standard hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the big blind. Minni has a significantly larger stack, but I need to continue playing aggressively to regain ground. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to take control of the hand early.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a button (SB) open to 300. Flash's opening range from the SB is wide, so with T♥3♣ I have reasonable equity and good pot odds to defend — calling 200 (300 total committed including my 100 BB) keeps the pot manageable and lets me see a flop without bloating an oop pot with a weak, unsuited rag. A small 3-bet is an option as a bluff, but with this hand out of position I prefer to call and evaluate the flop; I can fold to a large 3-bet or proceed cautiously post-flop. Action: call 300 total.

Check. On an A♣ J♦ 6♦ flop my T♥3♣ has missed completely — no pair, no diamond, and no realistic backdoor outs. I’m out of position in a heads-up pot vs a wide SB opener; bluffing here with essentially zero equity is suboptimal. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets Flash c-bet into me so I can fold to large aggression, and still allows me to call a small continuation bet to see a cheap turn against a wide c-betting frequency. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he c-bets I’ll only continue vs a small bet (~≤1/3–1/2 pot) and fold to larger sizes.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a board featuring an Ace and a Jack. This texture favors the raiser's range significantly. While I only have King-high with no direct draws, a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard play to exert pressure and represent a strong hand like an Ace. This allows me to take down the pot immediately if Minni has missed the flop.

My Th3c has missed the A♣ J♦ 6♦ flop — no pair and virtually no realistic draws (flush is impossible, backdoor broadway is tiny). Flash's c-bet of 300 into the ~900 pot is small (~1/3 pot), so calling is cheap and consistent with my earlier plan to continue only vs small continuation bets. Heads-up the SB opens/c-bets wide, so this small bet is often a pure frequency C-bet I can float to see a turn or fold to real strength. I'm out of position, I have no blockers and no hand to credibly raise as a bluff, so a raise would be overly ambitious. Call the small c-bet to keep the pot manageable (and fold to large turn aggression unless I improve).

I check. Holding T♥3♣ on J♦6♦A♣2♣ I have missed completely — no pair and no realistic draws (a five-card club flush is impossible with only one card to come). I called the small flop c-bet to keep the pot manageable and to float, but out of position I don’t want to bluff the turn or bloat the pot with nothing. Checking preserves stack, lets Flash reveal strength, and keeps my options open: fold to meaningful turn aggression (large bet ~½ pot or bigger) and only call very small bets (~≤1/3 pot) to see the river. If Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

I c-bet the flop to represent the Ace and Minni called. The turn is a deuce, which is a brick that doesn't change the board texture. While I could continue my story with a second barrel, Minni's flop call indicates she likely has a Jack, a six, or a diamond draw. Checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot, keep my King-high showdown value alive, and potentially see a free river. I can then evaluate my options based on the river card and her next move.

I have T♥3♣ on J♦6♦A♣2♣6♣ — I missed completely. There’s no straight and a flush is impossible with only one club in my hand (board has three clubs). Flash’s line (wide SB open, small 300 c-bet on flop, both checked turn) is consistent with lots of Ax/Jx and medium pairs; the river pairing the 6 actually improves villain’s range (pocket 6s, or hands that will now value-bet or call small bluffs). I have virtually no showdown value and very weak blocker coverage (no A/J), so bluffing is thin and will be called too often. The lowest-variance, highest-ROI play is to check and preserve my stack — fold to any river bet and concede the pot if he bets. If he checks back we lose the small pot but avoid a bad bluff. Action: check 0.

I have King-high on a board with an Ace and a Jack. After I checked the turn, my story of having an Ace became less convincing to an observant opponent. The river paired the Six. Minni has checked to me for a third time (including the turn). While I could attempt to bluff her off a mediocre hand like a Jack, Minni has shown she can be sticky with pairs. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free. I might occasionally win against missed diamond draws that she decided to check-call on the flop, and this prevents me from losing more chips if she is holding a Jack or a Six.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Qc7d. Pot is 150 and I face only a 50-call. In heads-up play I should be aggressive with a wide range; Q7o is strong enough to raise for initiative and fold equity. I’ll raise to the min (200) to keep the pot manageable vs a deep-stacked opponent while taking position postflop. Plan: if called, use position to c-bet favorable/Q-high or dry boards and proceed cautiously vs strong resistance; fold to a large 3-bet since this hand is marginal OOP in a big pot.

I am in the Big Blind and facing a min-raise from Minni. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, 5-2 offsuit is one of the weakest possible hands and plays very poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive opponent with a significant chip lead. With only 23 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity than to call with a hand that will likely have to fold on most flops.

I am holding Ace-Five offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand for two-player play. My strategy throughout this match has been to maintain aggression by opening for 3x the big blind when on the button. Raising here allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with the lead against Minni.

Heads-up vs Flash with deep stacks. Flash opened to 300 from the button — a wide range in heads-up. My hand (4♠6♥) is weak, offsuit and lacking blockers, so it’s a poor 3‑bet candidate: a re‑raise will bloat the pot out of position with little equity and few fold-inducing blockers. I should defend a wide range from the big blind, but keep the pot controllable. Calling the extra 200 (committing 300 total) lets me see a flop and realize equity against a wide opening range while avoiding unnecessary risk. Postflop plan: fold to big, well-sized c‑bets on dry boards; continue/call on connected/low boards or with backdoor/paired flops; raise or lead only if I hit strong two‑pair/trips or a good running draw. Therefore I will call to 300.

Out of position on a dry-ish 2♠ 9♦ T♦ with 4♠6♥. I defended preflop vs a wide button open to realize equity, but on the flop I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor spade/runner-runner possibilities. Betting or raising here would bloat the pot with almost no showdown equity and few useful blockers, especially against a wide range that contains many diamonds and ten/nine combinations. I will check to control the pot and see how Flash reacts — call very small continuation bets to keep seeing a turn, but fold to medium/large c-bets unless I improve (pair, backdoor completes, or a favorable runout).

I have Ace-high and was the pre-flop aggressor. On a board of 10-9-2, Minni has checked to me. A standard continuation bet of half the pot is appropriate here to represent a strong range and capitalize on the initiative. This bet aims to fold out hands that missed the flop while keeping the pot manageable if I'm called.

Heads‑up on a dry 2♠-9♦-T♦ board with 4♠6♥ OOP. I have no pair, only weak backdoor spade and a few pair-outs (3 fours + 3 sixes = 6 outs) — marginal improvement chances. Flash’s bet is small (300 into a 900 pot), so calling gives ~4:1 pot odds (need ≈20% equity). Versus a wide button opening/c‑bet range my hand has roughly that level of equity, and my stack is deep so a single 300 call is low risk. Raising would bloat the pot with almost no fold equity or real equity; folding would be too tight vs a small c‑bet. I’ll call 300 to realize equity, keep the pot controllable, and re‑evaluate on the turn: fold to large barreling unless I hit or pick up a strong draw, call small turns, and only apply aggression when I improve to a clear 2‑pair+/nut draw.

Board: 2♠ 9♦ T♦ A♣. I hold 4♠6♥ — no pair, no realistic draw. Flash opened wide from the button and c‑bet the flop; I called to see a turn. The Ace on the turn heavily improves Flash’s range (A‑x, T‑x, 9‑x, two‑pair/sets) and reduces any fold equity I might have. I have only 6 clean outs to pair (three 4s + three 6s) ≈ 13% to hit the river. The pot is 1,200 and stacks are deep, so betting or raising here would bloat the pot with virtually no equity or blockers and is a poor bluff. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me fold to a sizable bet, and allows me to call very small stabs or take a free river if Flash checks. Numeric plan: check now; if Flash leads a very small bet (≈≤200) I can consider calling (pot odds ~7:1 → break‑even ≈12.5%, close to my ~13%); if Flash bets ~300 or more I will fold (required equity ≈16.7% > my equity). If Flash checks back I’ll evaluate the river and only bluff or invest further with real equity or a clear plan.

I have improved to top pair with the Ace of Diamonds on the turn. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, and Minni has checked again, I should continue betting for value. This helps extract chips from her draws (flush or straight) and weaker made hands (tens or nines) that might call one more street. A bet of 750 into a 1200 pot provides a good balance of value and protection.

Turn decision vs Flash (button): I hold 4♠6♥ out of position on 2♠-9♦-T♦-A♣. Pot is 1950, opponent bet 750; calling requires break-even equity = 750/(1950+750) ≈ 27.8%. I have only 6 clean outs (three 4s + three 6s) ≈ 12.8% to hit the river, well below the required equity. Flash opened wide preflop, barreled the flop and now barrels the Ace — the A strongly improves a lot of value combinations in his continuing range (Ax, Tx, 9x, two-pair/sets). Raising as a bluff is unattractive: I have no useful blockers, little fold equity, and would bloat a pot out of position against deep stacks. I planned to fold to medium/large turn bets unless I improved; this sizing is beyond my calling threshold. Fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I’m on the button (small blind) heads-up with A♣5♠ — a very playable hand against a wide BB range. Raising here takes the initiative, builds the pot for value vs worse A-x and many broadway/connector hands, and gives fold equity. With deep stacks I’ll keep sizing standard and manageable: raise to 200 total (I’ve already posted 50, so +150). If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and extract value when ahead.

In a heads-up situation, 8-7 offsuit is a solid hand to defend from the Big Blind, especially against a min-raise (to 200) from the Button. The pot odds are 3:1 (100 to call for a total pot of 300), and 8-7 has enough connectivity to realize its equity post-flop. I will call and see the flop rather than 3-betting out of position against a chip leader.

I have flopped top two pair (Eights and Sevens) on a board of 4-8-7. This is a very strong hand, though the board is somewhat 'wet' with potential straight draws (like 5-6) and a diamond flush draw. Since I am out of position as the Big Blind and Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to her to see if she continues her aggression. If she bets, I plan to check-raise to build the pot and protect my hand against various draws. If she checks back, I will lead out on the turn.

Heads-up on the button with A♣5♠. I opened preflop to 200 and got called; flop is 4♦-8♠-7♦. This board misses a large portion of the BB’s calling range (many overcards, some weak pairs) but contains two diamonds and some connected pieces (possible 6 for a straight and diamond draws). I have Ace-high with a gutshot to a 6 (4 outs) and I block some A-x combos — a good spot to apply pressure and deny equity to draws. Flash checked, so a continuation bet should fold out many unpaired hands and extract value from worse A-x and some 7/4-type hands. I’ll make a half-pot c-bet to keep the pot manageable (200). If he raises big I’ll give up (my current equity is low); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously, sizing or checking depending on the turn card.

I have flopped top two pair on a relatively coordinated board (4d-8s-7d). While my hand is very strong, the board offers several straight and flush draws. Minni's continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot is standard. To protect my hand and build value while I am likely the favorite, I will check-raise. A raise to 700 (adding 500 to her 200) forces her to pay to see the turn with draws or potentially commit more with an overpair.

I opened to take the initiative and c-bet 200 on 4♦-8♠-7♦. Flash’s check-raise to 700 is sizable, but heads-up his raising range contains both strong made hands (77/88/44, some 6x straights) and a fair number of semi-bluffs (diamond draws, A6, etc.). Pot is 1,300 and I must call 500 more (my total committed on the street would be 700). Pot odds = 500 / (1,300+500) ≈ 27.8%. My realistic outs: 4 sixes to make the 4-5-6-7-8 straight + 3 remaining aces to pair = ~7 outs; probability to hit by river ≈ 27.9% (turn+river), so my equity is roughly inline with the pot odds. Given I have position, block some Ax combos, and Flash shows aggression that can include bluffs, calling to see the turn and re-evaluating is the best play. Raising to 1,200 would be a big, risky bluff with limited fold equity and folding now surrenders too much +EV. Plan after calling: if I hit a 6 or an A I’ll extract value; if the turn bricks I’ll check and fold to heavy pressure, control pot size with a marginal hand.

I currently hold top two pair (Eights and Sevens) on a board of 4d-8s-7d-3h. The turn 3h is essentially a blank, as it doesn't complete the obvious straight draw (5-6 still needs a 9 or a 5) or the diamond flush draw. Following my check-raise on the flop, which Minni called, I need to continue to extract value and protect my hand against those draws. A check here might look like I'm slowing down, but betting maintains the initiative and forces Minni to pay to see the river if she is on a draw, or to commit more chips with a weaker one-pair hand like A8 or K8. I will bet 1150 into the 1800 pot, which is roughly 64% of the pot, a sizing that balances getting value and providing unfavorable odds for the draws.

Flash check‑raised my flop C‑bet and now bets the turn 1,150 into a 2,950 pot. Calling 1,150 requires about 28% equity (1,150 / (2,950+1,150) ≈ 28%). With A♣5♠ on 4♦-8♠-7♦-3♥ I only have ~7 realistic outs (four 6s to complete the 3-4-5-6-7 straight and three remaining Aces) → ~7/46 ≈ 15% to improve on the river. That equity is well below the pot odds. Flash’s line (check‑raise flop then a sizable turn bet) is heavily weighted to strong made hands (sets, two‑pair, completed straights) and some disciplined semi‑bluffs; I don’t block enough of his strong combos to credibly bluff/raise here and a raise risks a larger, costly response. Calling is a -EV snap given the math; folding preserves a large stack and waits for a better spot. I fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 4c, which is a very weak hand. Given that I am currently trailing Minni in stack size and she has been playing aggressively, I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity to engage, as this hand has very little potential post-flop and I want to preserve my chips for more favorable situations in the remaining 20 hands.

Heads-up on the button with A♣T♣ — a very strong, playable heads-up holding (top-card + suited). Flash has a 13.8k stack and the table dynamics show frequent 3x opens; raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard here: it builds the pot with position, gains fold equity, and isolates the BB. With 26k stack a 300 open is small risk and keeps post‑flop options wide — C-bet on many textures, protect my club equity, and proceed cautiously on Ace‑heavy boards. I’ll raise to 300 (total committed to the pot).

Holding Ten-Six of hearts in the Big Blind, I am facing a standard 3x raise from Minni on the button. With a stack of 138 big blinds, I am getting 3:1 odds to see a flop. 10-6 suited is a reasonable hand to defend in position/defend the big blind in heads-up play, as it has straight and flush potential. While Minni has been aggressive, folding here would be too tight, and raising is unnecessary given the strength of the hand. I will call to see the flop.

I have hit middle pair with a Ten, but the board is quite dangerous. There is an Ace and three diamonds, and I do not hold any diamonds myself. In this situation, it is best to check to the aggressor. Betting into this board would likely only get called by better hands (Aces, flushes, or better Tens) and force me to fold to a raise. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Minni reacts to this flop texture.

I have AcTc on Td-4d-Ad: two pair (Aces and Tens) — a very strong heads-up holding. The flop is monotone (3 diamonds) so the main concerns are (1) opponent having two diamonds (a made flush) or (2) pocket Aces/Tens making trips; those combos exist but are relatively uncommon. Flash checked the flop, so I should bet for value and protection: deny equity to single-diamond hands and charge worse Ax/Tx/Kx/Qx hands that will call. I don't want to overcommit vs a potential check-raise representing a flush, so I size to ~2/3 pot. Bet 400 into the 600 pot to extract value while leaving room to fold or re-evaluate to a large check-raise; if he just calls I'll control size on the turn, if he check-raises big I'll assume a very strong made hand and re-evaluate for a fold or a pot-sized call depending on sizing.

I have middle pair (Tens) on a board that is quite dangerous due to the three diamonds and the Ace. However, in heads-up play, folding a middle pair to a single continuation bet is generally too tight, even without a diamond in my hand. The bet size from Minni (40% of the pot) is standard and doesn't necessarily indicate a made flush or an Ace. I will call to see how the turn develops, planning to proceed cautiously if the board gets even wetter or if Minni continues with heavy aggression.

The turn has brought a fourth diamond to the board (Qd), making it incredibly dangerous. I have a pair of tens but no diamond in my hand. Any diamond held by my opponent now makes a flush. Additionally, the Queen is an overcard to my pair. Betting here serves little purpose as I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. Checking is the most prudent move to see how Minni responds and potentially reach a cheap showdown if she checks back.

I have A♣T♣ — two pair (Aces and Tens). The turn Q♦ completes a three‑diamond board (T♦ 4♦ A♦ Q♦), so the main risk is that Flash holds two diamonds (a completed flush) or a disguised trip (Ad or Td). However Flash called my sizable flop bet, so his calling range still contains a lot of one‑pair hands (Ax, Tx, pocket pairs, Qx) that I beat. I want to extract value from those hands and protect against any remaining backdoor equity, but I don’t want to overcommit if I face a big check‑raise that strongly indicates a flush/trips. I’ll make a value/protection bet of ~2/3 pot (900) to charge worse A/T/K/Q holdings and deny easy river odds, while remaining prepared to fold to a large, polarized check‑raise and re‑evaluate if he simply calls.

The turn has brought a fourth diamond to the board, making it extremely dangerous. I currently hold middle pair (Tens) but have no diamond in my hand, meaning any single diamond held by Minni makes a flush. Furthermore, the Queen is an overcard to my pair. Minni has been aggressive throughout the hand, and even if she doesn't have a flush, she could easily have an Ace or a Queen. Without a diamond to provide any flush equity or a redraw, calling a 900-chip bet into a 1400-chip pot is not mathematically or strategically sound. I will fold.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with King-five of spades. This is a strong starting hand in this format. I have a stack of 13,150 while Minni has 26,700. I will continue my standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 total to build the pot and take control of the hand in position.

I have A♠4♦ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 heads-up. Heads-up A4o is worth defending, but I’m out of position and Flash’s opening range from the button is wide. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more — I need roughly 200/(400+200) ≈ 33% equity to justify a call, which A4o often has vs a wide opener. A small 3‑bet to 500 is thin value and only builds a bigger pot OOP against an aggressive opponent, so I’ll keep the pot manageable and see a flop. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — continue aggressively with an ace or good draws, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy aggression.

Flop Kc-4c-Qc vs my A♠4♦: I have second pair (4s) with an ace kicker but the board is three clubs — any two clubs in Flash’s wide BTN opening range is an instantaneous made flush. I’m out of position and there’s currently no bet, so betting to “protect” is thin value (I have no club blocker and will get called by Kx/Qx and many club combos, building a big OOP pot with a marginal hand). I’ll pot‑control and check. Plan: check and give up to heavy aggression (fold to large C‑bets); consider calling a very small C‑bet to see a safe turn, but if Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and look to bet if a safe/A card appears.

I have flopped top pair with King-Five of spades on a very coordinated board of King-Queen-Four, all clubs. While I have top pair, the monotone board is dangerous since I do not hold a club. Minni has checked to me. I will make a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from weaker hands like a Queen or a lower pocket pair, and to charge any single-club draws. If she raises, I will have to proceed with extreme caution given the texture.

I hold A♠4♦ on Kc-4c-Qc — second pair with the best possible kicker I can have here, but the board is three clubs so my hand is very vulnerable to any two-club combo. Flash opened wide from the button preflop and now fires 300 into the pot; calling 300 into a 900 pot requires ~25% equity. Raising OOP with a marginal second pair and no club blocker is thin — it builds a big pot I don’t want to play out of position and will get called by Kx/Qx and many club/club+ combos, or punished by a shove. My read: Flash’s range contains some made flushes and top-pair hands but also a fair number of missed broadways and weaker holdings I currently beat. Calling is cheap and lets me realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: call the 300, keep the pot controlled, fold to heavy turn/river aggression (especially if a club shows) but if a safe card (A or a blank that doesn’t complete a club flush) arrives I can proceed for value or pot-control appropriately.

Turn decision. I hold A♠4♦ on K♣-4♣-Q♣-K♦ — second pair with the best possible kicker for my pair, but the board is extremely dangerous: three clubs on the flop and now a paired king on the turn. Flash (BTN) opened wide and c-bet the flop, so his continuing range contains a lot of made club flushes, Kx (now trips) and strong Qx/KQ combos that all beat me. Betting out OOP with a marginal pair would bloat the pot and get called or raised by most hands that beat me while folding only worse hands — not a profitable plan. I’ll pot-control and check. Plan: fold to large turn aggression if he leads big; call small-to-medium bets to realize equity against bluffs/missed broadways; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and look for a safe A/blank to extract thin value or otherwise keep the pot small.

I have improved to three of a kind with the second King appearing on the turn. However, the board is quite coordinated with three clubs, and my kicker (five) is very weak. If Minni also has a King, I am likely out-kicked. Furthermore, Minni's call on the monotone flop suggests she has a piece of this board or a draw that may have completed. By checking behind, I control the pot size, avoid being check-raised by a better hand, and ensure I reach the river to see Minni's final move.

I hold A♠4♦ — second pair (4s) with the best possible kicker I can have here, but the board is extremely hazardous: Kc‑4c‑Qc‑Kd with three clubs on the flop. Flash opened wide and c‑bet the flop (where a flush was possible) then checked the turn, so his continuing range includes made flushes, Kx (now trips), Qx and a mix of missed broadways. Betting river OOP with this marginal hand mainly gets called by better hands (flush/trips/Qx) and only folds some complete air; it also bloats the pot unnecessarily. I’ll keep the pot small and take the cheapest line to showdown — check. If Flash fires a small river bet I can call to realize equity vs bluffs/missed hands, but I will fold to a large overbet that polarizes to value.

On the river, the board is [Kc, 4c, Qc, Kd, 6s]. I have three of a kind with [Ks, 5s]. My best five-card hand is K-K-K-Q-6. Minni has checked to me three times. While I have a very strong hand, the board texture is dangerous with three clubs, and my kicker is essentially non-existent (I'm playing the 6 on the board). If Minni has any King with a kicker higher than a 6 (like K-7 through K-A), she beats me. If she has a flush, she beats me. Given that she called the flop bet on a monotone board, she likely has a piece of it. By checking behind, I realize my showdown value and win against all her Queens and bluffs without the risk of being out-kicked or check-raised by a flush.

Heads-up on the button with Ad7d (A7 suited) — a strong, positionally advantaged hand that wants to be in the pot. With the SB acting first preflop I should open-raise rather than limp to take the initiative, build the pot with a high-card plus nut-flush potential, and deny the BB cheap equity. The min-raise is 200, but I prefer a standard 3xBB sizing (300) to reduce the range of hands that can profitably call and to get fold equity. Postflop I’ll c-bet many dry boards and play carefully if we face a large 3-bet (fold to very large 3-bets, call small ones to realize equity).

I am holding 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While we are heads-up and I am getting reasonable pot odds to call, this hand realizes its equity very poorly out of position. Minni has been quite aggressive, and there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand when I still have a comfortable stack of 137 big blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

In this heads-up situation, Q3o is a very weak hand. While it is standard to open a wide range from the button, Minni has been aggressive and currently holds a significant chip lead. I have 136 big blinds, so there is no immediate pressure to play marginal or sub-par hands. Folding here allows me to wait for better spots to challenge her lead without bleeding chips on hands that are easily dominated or difficult to play post-flop.

Heads-up on the button (I have position after the flop) with K♠9♦ is a standard open-raise spot — a raise gets the initiative, builds the pot when I'm ahead, and forces folds from weaker BB holdings. Opponent (Flash) has a mid-sized stack but will defend widely from the big blind; I want to apply pressure while keeping postflop playability. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) — a size I use frequently to thin the field, gain fold equity, and play position postflop. If Flash calls I can use position to realize equity or apply pressure; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (avoid committing to big pots with a marginal hand).

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Deuce offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a standard 3x raise from the button. Minni has been quite active, and while A2o doesn't play exceptionally well post-flop, it has significant equity against a wide opening range. I will call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable while out of position.

On a dry flop of Q-5-4 rainbow, I am out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor. My Ace-high has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out (donk bet) into the raiser. The standard and most disciplined play here is to check to Minni and see how they react. If they bet, I can evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, I will check to maintain pot control and see if I can realize my equity.

Heads-up with position and initiative after opening preflop. The Qc-5h-4d flop is pretty dry and unlikely to have hit a large portion of Flash’s calling range (broadways, suited aces, small pairs). I missed with K9 but can apply pressure: a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600) will fold many unpaired hands and keep the pot controllable if called. If he raises big I can fold or re-evaluate; if called I can continue selectively on favorable turns (K/9 or blanks) or check back vs heavy action. Bet to seize the initiative and pick up the pot or define his range.

On a dry board of Queen-Five-Four rainbow, I have Ace-high and a backdoor wheel draw (needing a tray for a gutshot). Minni is an aggressive opponent who frequently continuation bets. To call 300 into a pot of 900, I am getting 4:1 odds, requiring roughly 20% equity to justify a call. Ace-high holds significant equity against a wide button-opening range on this texture. I will call to see a turn and evaluate based on whether the board improves my hand or provides more drawing potential.

On the Turn 8c, the board remains relatively dry. My Ace-high (As 2h) has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, and betting as a bluff is unlikely to be effective against Minni's range here. I will check to maintain pot control and see if Minni checks behind, which would allow me to see a free river and potentially win at showdown or improve to a pair or straight.

I have K♠9♦ and missed the turn (8♣). I was called on the flop, so Flash’s range is wide (Qc, mid pairs, small pocket pairs, club draws, some floats). The 8♣ actually helps his drawing/clubbing combos and gives him additional pair/8x hands — I have no pair and no club. With position and healthy stacks I want to control the pot rather than bloating it with a marginal hand. Checking preserves flexibility (see river, induce bluffs, fold to heavy turn aggression) and avoids getting raised off a hand I can’t comfortably continue with. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the river; fold to significant turn pressure, consider bluffing or a thin value/lead on a favorable river.

On the river 4s, the board pairs. After I checked the turn, Minni checked behind, which suggests she either has a medium-strength hand looking for a cheap showdown or has completely given up on a bluff. My Ace-high has a reasonable amount of showdown value in heads-up play, especially in a pot that has been checked through. Betting here wouldn't accomplish much, as it's unlikely to force a fold from a better hand or get called by a worse one. I will check and see if I can win the pot at showdown.

Heads-up on the button with K♠9♦. Board: 4♦ 5♥ Q♣ 8♣ 4♠. I c-bet the flop and was called, both players checked the turn, and the river paired the board (second 4). Flash checked to me. Given his flop-call then passive turn/river, his range is weighted toward Qx, small-to-mid pocket pairs and some missed club draws/floats. A river bet here would be a pure bluff most of the time — Qx and pairs will usually call, and I have no pair, no club, and no meaningful blocker to 4s. With stacks healthy (I'm the bigger stack) and only marginal fold equity, the high-variance bluff is unattractive. I check and take showdown rather than bloat the pot or risking a costly bluff.

In a heads-up situation with a stack of 14200 (142 BB), I have a solid starting hand, Ace-Five offsuit. Being on the Button (Small Blind), I want to play aggressively and maintain pressure. A standard open-raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately, while still being strong enough to play if called.

Facing a button open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with 2c7d — the worst offsuit holding. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 (pot odds ~3:1 / ~25%), but 2-7o has very poor equity vs a button’s opening range and is frequently dominated. I’m out of position postflop and stacks are deep, which means calling leads to difficult multi-street decisions and potential big losses. 3-betting would be highly spewy with this hand. Preserve my large stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up from the SB/button with Kc5c — a playable, suited king. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is passive; I want the initiative and to apply pressure to BB's wide defending range. A standard 3x BB open (raise to 300 total) is the right size: it folds many worse hands, keeps stacks deep enough for postflop maneuvering, and lets me c-bet favorable flops or concede to a large 3-bet. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold this marginal hand; if called I’ll continuation-bet appropriate sizing on dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards.

I have King-Five offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), K5o is a standard defending hand in a heads-up situation. It has sufficient equity against a wide button opening range. I will call to see a flop and evaluate the hand further from there.

On a paired board like Ts-Th-Jc, King-high has very little value or connectivity. As the defender in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Donking (betting into the raiser) here doesn't represent much and doesn't accomplish a fold from better hands or value from worse. I will check and see if Minni chooses to continuation bet, then evaluate the strength of her bet versus my King-high.

Heads-up from the button with Kc5c on Ts Th Jc. I opened preflop and was called, but on this flop I have no pair and only a remote backdoor club (need both turn+river clubs — ~4% to hit). Flash’s calling range from the BB is wide and on a paired board like TTJ it contains a lot of hands that have at least a pair (T, J, many pocket pairs) or reasonable calling hands (overcards/Ax/Kx) that will often call a c-bet. That makes a pure bluff c-bet here low EV — my fold equity is limited and I’d be playing for stacks out of position on later streets if I fire and get called.

I’m the chip leader and in position, so I’ll control the pot and use position to see another card. Plan: check to pot-control and induce bluffs or smaller bets I can call/float; if Flash checks back I get a free turn to improve or to re-evaluate; if he bets small I can call and attempt to take it away later; if he barrels big I can fold and preserve my stack. (If I were to c-bet instead I’d choose a small size (~1/3 pot) to conserve chips, but here checking is the prudent line.)

The turn brings the Ace of Diamonds, which is a significant card. I currently hold King-high with a gutshot straight draw (to the Broadway straight with any Queen). Minni checked back the flop, which often indicates she doesn't have a very strong hand like a Ten or a strong Jack, though she could be slow-playing or have a marginal hand. I will check here to see how she reacts to the Ace. If she checks behind, I get to see a free river card to potentially hit my straight. If she bets, I can evaluate the price to continue based on my gutshot equity and potential for King-high to be good or to bluff later.

Board Ts Th Jc Ad, I hold Kc5c — no pair and no live flush draw anymore (a flush would have needed both turn+river clubs; turn was a diamond). Flash checked the turn after both of us checked the flop, which is consistent with a wide checking/calling range but also with medium/strong hands pot‑controlling (T, J, Ax, pocket pairs). Bluffing the turn here has very limited fold equity and turns a marginal hand into a bloated pot out of position on later streets. I'm the chip leader and in position, so I prefer pot control and extra information from the river. Plan: check and see the river; if Flash bets a small/medium river bet I can consider calling as a bluff‑catcher depending on texture and sizing; if he barrels big on the river I’ll fold K‑high.

The river is a 3s, which is a total brick. The board is currently Ts-Th-Jc-Ad-3s. I have King-high (Kd 5h). Minni has checked back on both the flop and the turn. This suggests she likely doesn't have an Ace, a Ten, or a strong Jack, as she would typically bet those for value or protection on such a coordinated board. While King-high has very little showdown value, it may occasionally win against total air (like 98, 87, or 76). I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if she decides to bet, as King-high is a weak candidate for a bluff-catch.

I have K♣5♣ and missed my only realistic draw. Both players checked flop and turn, and Flash checked the river as well. The board (T♠ T♥ J♣ A♦ 3♠) doesn’t improve me — I have no pair and no made flush — so my only path to win is a pure bluff. Flash’s checking range here likely contains Ax (pot-controlling), Jx/Tx, and many medium pairs or overcards that will often call a small probe, so my fold equity is low. The pot is only 600 while stacks are deep; risking chips for a marginal bluff is poor risk/reward. As chip leader I prefer pot control and preserving stack equity. I'll check and concede unless Flash fires a very small bet, in which case I can re-evaluate, but betting myself here is low EV.

I have been dealt a premium pocket pair, Jacks (Jd Js), on the button in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, is currently the chip leader, so I want to build a sizable pot but also extract value. Pocket Jacks are very strong pre-flop. Standard opening from the button in this structure has been around 300, and I will continue that trend to maintain consistency with my previous opening ranges while building value for my strong hand.

J♥3♠ is a very weak, marginal hand to play out of position in heads-up. Flash opened to 300 from the button, so calling 200 to play OOP faces a wide but often dominating range (lots of Jx and higher broadways) and the hand has poor post‑flop playability and few backdoor prospects. A small 3‑bet to 500 would be a light bluff without good blockers and would often be called, leaving me OOP with a subpar hand. Pot odds (~3:1) are borderline but not sufficient given domination and positional disadvantage. I’ll conserve chips and fold to the open rather than play a difficult OOP spot.

Heads-up on the button with 5c5d — a small pocket pair that plays very well in position. I’ve already posted the SB (50) and calling is only 50 more, but I prefer to open-raise to take the initiative: raise to the min allowed (200 total) applies pressure to weaker broadways/ace-highs, builds the pot when I hit a set, and keeps the pot manageable versus an aggressive 3-bettor. If Flash calls I’ll use position to see the flop cheaply and look to extract value on sets or realize equity; if he 3-bets large I’ll let this one go (but I’ll consider calling smaller, reasonable 3-bets to set-mine given deep stacks). Raising is the least exploitable, highest EV line here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-King suited, a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has opened for a min-raise to 200. I should 3-bet here for value and to seize the initiative. A raise to 700 is a standard sizing to put pressure on Minni's opening range while building a pot with a dominant hand.

I opened the button (SB) to 200 with 5c5d and Flash 3‑bet to 700. With a small pocket pair in position and very deep stacks (I have 25,350 vs his 13,750) the +EV line is to call and set‑mine rather than fold or poorly polarize with a small 4‑bet. Pot is 900 and the extra call is 500 (break‑even equity by raw pot odds ≈ 35.7%), while my chance to hit a set by the river is ≈ 19.2% — so pot odds alone don’t justify a pure call, but implied odds and position do. If I flop a set I can extract large value from his wide-ish heads‑up 3‑betting range; if I miss I can use position to control the pot and fold to heavy aggression. A small 4‑bet here has poor fold equity and committing more preflop with just a small pair isn’t profitable. Call to see the flop in position and pursue set‑mining/value lines postflop.

I hold Ace-King of hearts on a flop of 5s-Jd-3d. I was the pre-flop 3-bettor, and this board favors my range, which contains all overpairs (QQ-AA) and strong Broadways. Even though I missed the flop, I have two overcards and the initiative. A continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on Minni's calling range and to represent a strong hand like an overpair or a Jack. I will bet approximately one-third of the pot.

I flopped a set (5c5d on 5s Jd 3d) after opening and calling Flash’s 3‑bet preflop. Flash’s small c-bet of 450 into this pot looks like a fairly wide or under‑betting range (top pairs, flush/diamond combos, air). With deep stacks and a very strong made hand, I want to build the pot and charge any Jx/diamond draws while denying free cards. A thin/min-raise (900) is too weak — I’ll make a substantive value/protection raise so worse hands and draws pay to continue. I raise to 2250 total on the flop (I’m adding 1800 more); if he calls we’ll have a big pot (≈5450) and I can extract more on later streets. If he jams, my set is strong enough to call; if he folds I win the pot now.

I hold AhKh on a flop of 5s-Jd-3d. After my pre-flop 3-bet and a standard continuation bet, Minni has responded with a large 5x raise. This sizing is highly polarized, typically representing either a very strong hand (like a set of 5s or 3s, or a strong Jack like AJ) or a semi-bluff with a diamond draw. While I have two overcards, I am out of position and the board texture offers me no direct draws. Calling 1800 more into a 4100 pot requires roughly 30% equity, and while AK has some equity against a range of draws and one-pair hands, playing a bloated pot out of position with just Ace-high is extremely difficult. Given we are near the end of the tournament (hand 139 of 150), preserving my 12,600-chip stack to find better opportunities is more prudent than calling a massive raise on a board that doesn't favor my specific holding.

I am on the button with 6h 4c, which is a very weak starting hand even in heads-up play. Given that I am currently trailing Minni by a significant margin and there are only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. While folding the button in heads-up is generally avoided, 6-4 offsuit offers very little playability against an aggressive opponent like Minni. I will fold this hand and wait for a more advantageous situation to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button (I posted SB 50) with T4o — a marginal hand but fine to open in heads-up. I have a big stack advantage (26700 vs 13150) and want to seize initiative and apply pressure. Limping/calling gives the BB correct odds and cedes initiative; min-raising to 200 is too small and invites cheap calls. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB, total commitment 300 including my posted 50) to generate fold equity and define the hand. If called I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and control the pot when I miss.

With [Qc, 6s] in the Big Blind during heads-up play, I have a reasonable hand to defend against a standard 3x open from Minni. The price to call is 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While Q6o is not a premium hand, it is well within the defending range for a Big Blind in this situation. I will call to see the flop and play post-flop based on the board texture.

On a King-high board like 9d-Kc-2s, I have no direct connection with my Qc-6s. As the Big Blind defending against a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Queen-high has very little equity here and I am not looking to build a pot out of position without a pair or a strong draw. I will check and evaluate Minni's response.

Heads-up I opened preflop and kept initiative; flop 9d Kc 2s is very dry and misses my T4. Flash checked, showing some weakness or pot-control. In heads-up spots like this I c-bet a high frequency to fold out unpaired hands and get value from worse Kx/9x hands. I'll make a small, aggressive continuation bet (≈1/3 pot) — 200 into 600 — to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate; if he raises I’ll give credit to a strong hand and fold to a large, polarized raise.

On a board of Kd-9d-2s, my hand (Qc-6s) has completely missed. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 1/3 of the pot. While the price is relatively cheap to call (200 into a total pot of 800), I am out of position with very little equity and no significant draws. Continuing with just Queen-high against a frequent aggressor on a King-high board is likely to lead to further losses on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot in the remaining hands.

I am holding 2-4 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible hands in poker. Although I am on the button in a heads-up match, the hand has almost no playability and very low equity. Given the stack sizes and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips on a pure bluff with a hand this poor. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous starting hand to challenge Minni's lead.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with T5s (5c Tc). In heads-up play ranges are wide and I want to take the initiative rather than limp. T5s has decent postflop playability (backdoor clubs, straight possibilities, and a chance to hit a ten) and deep stacks + position favor an opening raise. I’ll make a standard open to the min-raise (200 total) to build the pot, gain fold equity, and keep the lead; if Flash 3-bets I can fold or proceed based on sizing, if he calls I’ll play the flop in position.

I am holding Ace-Queen of Hearts, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has opened from the button with a min-raise to 200. With a stack of 12,800 and only 7 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to capitalize on strong holdings to close the gap on Minni's lead. 3-betting here allows me to take control of the pot, realize equity, and potentially win the pot immediately pre-flop or build value against weaker Broadway hands and smaller Aces. I will raise to 700.

Facing a 3-bet to 700 after my open to 200. I have Tc5c — a hand with decent multi-street playability (backdoor club flush, straight potential, and can hit a ten) and I'm on the button so I'll have position postflop. Flash's 700 sizing is relatively small, implying a fairly wide 3-bet range heads-up. Calling 500 more (700 total committed) gives ~35.7% break-even equity and excellent implied odds with deep stacks, so I can realize equity and keep control of the pot. A tiny 4-bet bluff to 1200 is unattractive (won't fold many hands and unnecessarily escalates the pot); folding surrenders position and future opportunities. Plan: call to see the flop in position, c-bet and apply pressure on favorable textures, and check/defend or fold to large aggression on dry/unfavorable flops.

On this paired board of J-J-7, I have Ace-high with a Queen kicker. My pre-flop 3-bet indicates strength, potentially representing big pairs or strong Jacks. While the board texture favors my 3-bet range, I want to be cautious. Since I am out of position and have a solid showdown value hand that doesn't necessarily need to turn into a bluff yet, I will check to see how Minni reacts. Checking allows me to control the pot size and catch potential bluffs, or re-evaluate if Minni bets small.

Flop is 7c Js Jd and I have Tc5c — a marginal hand but with a backdoor club flush (two clubs in hand + 7c) and a few tens outs. Flash 3-bet preflop, then checked the flop, so his range is wide (broadways, pockets, suited connectors) and contains many hands that will fold to a continuation bet. Betting here takes the initiative, gets value from/forces folds by A/Kx and many unpaired hands, and protects my backdoor equity. I don’t want to overcommit with a marginal holding, so a half‑pot c-bet keeps the pot manageable and achieves good fold equity (700 into 1400 = 50% pot; break‑even ~33%).
