Plan: bet 700; fold to a large check‑raise, call smallish raises or calls and re-evaluate on the turn (look to continue only if I pick up a club/ten or the street becomes favorable).

On this paired board of J-J-7, my Ace-Queen still holds significant showdown value against Minni's range, which includes many smaller pocket pairs, suited connectors, and occasional air. Minni's half-pot bet of 700 is a standard probe on such a board. While I am behind any Jack or pocket pair, I have two overcards and the best possible Ace-high. Folding at this stage would be too tight given the pot odds (700 to call into a 2100 pot) and my hand's strength. Raising would likely only be called by hands that beat me (trips or better pairs). Therefore, I will call to see the turn, maintaining pot control while keeping the door open to improve or reach showdown.

The turn 5h is essentially a brick that doesn't change the texture of the board significantly. I checked the flop and called Minni's 700 bet to see where I stood with my Ace-Queen. Since I am out of position and the board is paired with Jacks, my hand still has decent showdown value but is vulnerable to any Jack or pocket pair. I will check again to see Minni's reaction. Checking allows me to potentially see a cheap river if she checks back, or it gives me the option to catch a bluff if she decides to continue betting with a wider range of air or semi-bluffs.

Turn pairs my hand: I picked up two pair (Jacks and Fives) when the 5h arrived. Flash 3‑bet preflop and check‑called my flop c‑bet, which is a fairly wide but often medium‑to‑strong range (overpairs, broadways, single Jacks, some mid pairs and bluffs). His check on the turn shows some caution/pot‑control or missed overcards. This is a clear turn value spot — I beat lots of hands that will call (A5, K5, Ax and many broadways that called the flop) and I want to deny a free river to overcards. I’ll make a value/protection bet of a little over half the pot to extract from worse hands while keeping the pot manageable if I’m up against a very strong holding. Bet 1600 into 2800 (~57% pot). If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely trips/set/quads), but for now charging thin callers and protecting my hand is the +EV line.

On a paired board of J-J-7-5, my Ace-Queen hearts still holds significant showdown value in a heads-up context, especially since I was the pre-flop aggressor. Minni's 1,600 bet on the turn is a polarized sizing that could represent a Jack, a pocket pair, or a pure bluff. Given the dry texture of the board and the fact that I've checked twice, I am under-representing the strength of my Ace-high. With only 7 hands remaining in the tournament and a large chip deficit, I cannot afford to fold the top of my non-pairing range to a second barrel. I am getting roughly 2.75:1 on a call (1,600 to win 4,400), and my hand beats all of Minni's bluffs while still having outs against her small pocket pairs. I will call to reach the river.

The river has delivered a third Jack, making the board 7-J-J-5-J. While I have three of a kind with an Ace kicker (J-J-J-A-Q), this is an extremely dangerous board. Because there are three Jacks on the board, any pocket pair in Minni's range now makes a full house (e.g., 2-2-J-J-J), and any Jack makes quads. Minni has shown consistent aggression by betting both the flop and the turn after calling my pre-flop 3-bet, which strongly suggests she holds a pocket pair or a Jack. By checking, I can attempt to reach a showdown against her potential bluffs or missed draws (like suited connectors that didn't pair), while avoiding further losses against her likely superior made hands. Betting would serve no purpose as I would only be called by hands that beat me.

Board: 7♣ J♠ J♦ 5♥ J♣ — I have 5♣ T♣, which gives me jacks full of fives (JJJ55). This is an extremely strong value hand in HU — the only hands that beat me are the fourth jack (quads) or any hand that pairs the 7 on board to make JJJ77 (or a pocket pair higher than fives that pairs the board, etc.). Flash’s line (3-bet pre, check-call my flop c-bet, check-call my turn value bet) is consistent with a wide but often-medium range: underpairs, pocket pairs, slow-played sets or one-pair hands. His river check is ambiguous (pot control or trap).

Given the strength of my hand and the likelihood that many hands in his continuing range (single 5, 6s–TT pockets, A5/K5/Ax that called down, even some 7x combinations) will call a value bet, I should extract value. I don’t want to overbet and fold out many calling hands, nor do I want to bet so small I miss value. A ~2/3-pot value bet is appropriate here — big enough to get called by worse full houses/sets/top pairs and to charge any weaker hands, but not so big as to only get called by quads/77. If Flash check‑raises large, it’s likely the very small portion of his range that beats me and I’ll have to re-evaluate then (but given stacks I can call a reasonable shove). 

Action: bet 4000 into the 6000 pot to extract value from worse hands while keeping weaker callers in the pot.

On a board of 7-J-J-5-J, my Ace-Queen of hearts has improved to three-of-a-kind Jacks with an Ace-Queen kicker. However, the presence of three Jacks on the board is extremely dangerous. Because there is a triple on the board, any pocket pair in Minni's range (which is very likely after calling a 3-bet pre-flop) now constitutes a full house (e.g., 2-2-J-J-J). Additionally, any 7 or 5 in her hand also makes a full house. Minni has shown strength on every street, betting the flop, turn, and now leading for 4,000 on the river. While she is an aggressive player, calling here would require me to believe she is betting absolute air, as nearly all of her 'value' range now beats my Ace-high. With only 7 hands remaining and a 9,900 stack, risking nearly half my chips on a pure bluff-catcher when most of her range has improved to a full house is not a winning strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Holding 95o in the small blind heads-up, this hand is significantly below the average strength. Given Minni's recent displays of aggression and my current stack disadvantage, I choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity in the final few hands of the tournament rather than entering a pot with a weak starting hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with Kc6c and deep stacks (30100 vs 9750). K6s is a playable, positionally advantaged hand — good top-card potential plus backdoor club/straight possibilities. I don’t want to limp and give Flash free equity; I want initiative and fold equity. A standard 3xBB open (300 total) is a clean size here: it’s big enough to apply pressure but keeps the pot manageable out of position for the BB. Plan: if Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll play the flop in position; if he 3-bets large I’ll largely concede K6s and fold (or re-evaluate vs a very small 3-bet). Raising is the +EV, aggressive play in heads-up with this holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 3h, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 3x the big blind. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and being out of position, there is no reason to defend such a poor hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining 97.5 big blinds.

I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play and well within the standard raising range. Given the tournament stage (Hand 146 of 150) and the stack sizes, I want to maintain pressure and build the pot with my range advantage. I will put in a standard raise to 300.

Heads-up the button (Flash) opens very wide to 300 frequently. 9♦7♥ is marginal but has decent equity and straight potential against a wide range, and calling 200 more into a 400 pot gives ~33% pot odds to see a flop. I'm deep-stacked (30150 vs 9450) and don't want to bloat the pot OOP with an offsuit rag — a small 3-bet here would be a bluff without good blockers. I’ll call (300 total) to see a flop and either realize equity or fold to strong aggression on dry boards; if I connect or pick up fold equity on the turn I can apply pressure.

Board is Jh Ac 3c and I hold 9♦7♥ — a complete miss. Flash opened very wide preflop so his range contains many A or J combos that connect this flop; I have no pair, no club backdoor and very little equity. I'm out of position and deep relative to him, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a pure bluff OOP or turn this into a multi-street war with a weak hand. I’ll check to keep the pot small and fold to standard continuation bets; if he checks back I can re-evaluate on the turn for a delayed bluff or a free card.

On an Ace-high board as the pre-flop raiser, I have a significant range advantage. Representing the Ace with a continuation bet is a standard play to exert pressure on the opponent's wider calling range. Minni checked, which is expected. I will bet 300 (half pot) to put pressure on any non-Ace hands or weak draws. Given that the tournament is nearing its end (Hand 146 of 150) and I am behind in chips, I need to take these profitable bluffing opportunities on boards that favor my perceived range.

I’m out of position with 9♦7♥ on Jh Ac 3c — a complete miss with no club backdoor. Flash opened wide preflop and his flop bet is small (300 into a 900 pot, ~1/3 pot). Calling 300 requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)); versus a very wide button range my hand has roughly that kind of showdown or backdoor bluff-equity. Raising OOP here would be a pure blockerless bluff and risks inflating a multi-street pot I don’t want to build with nothing. Plan: call the 300 to keep the pot controlled and realize equity; fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I pick up a real draw or pair, and look for a cheap river decision or a turn to attempt a delayed bluff if the action and card are favourable.

I have 9♦7♥ on J♥ A♣ 3♣ T♥ — a complete miss with no club backdoor or real showdown equity. Flash opened wide preflop and made a small 300 c-bet on the flop, then checked the turn, which looks like weakness or a missed marginal holding/draw. With no pair and no real equity, my goal is to take the pot now rather than giving him a free river. I’ll apply controlled pressure with a half-pot probe to fold out weaker holdings (small pairs, missed broadways, some draws) while keeping the risk modest if called. If he folds I win the 1200 pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river and fold to significant aggression (a raise/jam) since I have no pair. Betting here exploits his passive turn check and his short(er) stack without overcommitting.

On the turn, the board has become increasingly coordinated with [Jh Ac 3c Th]. I currently hold King-high with no pair and only a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). Minni's lead of 600 into the 1200 pot after calling my flop continuation bet suggests she has either an Ace, a Jack that has potentially improved to two pair, or a strong draw. Given my chip disadvantage and the fact that we are in the final hands of the tournament, calling here with very little equity is a poor use of my remaining stack. Folding allows me to preserve my 9,150 chips for a more favorable situation in the remaining four hands.

Heads-up on the button with K♦3♣. Deep stacks and position justify an aggressive, wide opening range — K3o is marginal but playable. Raising to 200 (2BB) seizes initiative, applies pressure to Flash (≈90BB), and picks up the 150-chip pot often; if called we keep position and can realize fold equity or hit top pair. If Flash 3-bets big, I’ll let this weak-kicker hand go to avoid a dominated, difficult spot. A small raise (to 200) balances fold equity with not overcommitting on a marginal holding.

I have Ace-Trey offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni (the chip leader) opened for a small 2x raise from the Small Blind. Given the pot odds (getting 3:1) and the fact that an Ace has significant high-card value in a heads-up situation, this is a standard defend. I will call to see the flop rather than 3-betting, as A3o doesn't play particularly well against a 4-bet and I want to keep the pot manageable while out of position.

On a Jack-high board with two diamonds, my Ace-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni reacts. I don't have a piece of the board and leading out doesn't accomplish much against a chip leader who has been aggressive. I will check and evaluate based on their bet sizing.

I was the preflop raiser and have position. Flop J♦-6♦-2♥ is low and fairly dry for the calling big-blind range, although it has two diamonds which introduces single-card diamond draws for someone holding two diamonds. My hand K♦3♣ has no pair but I block some Kx combinations and I have a small backdoor diamond equity. In heads-up play the correct approach is to c-bet a large portion of my missed range to collect folds and charge floats. I’ll make a half-pot continuation bet (200 into 400): big enough to generate fold equity and protect my range, but small enough not to overcommit with a marginal holding. Plan: fold to a large check-raise (I’m too weak to continue), call small single raises and re-evaluate on the turn (look to barrel if I pick up equity or pot-control if I miss).

I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, but Ace-high with a weak kicker is a difficult hand to defend out of position on a Jack-high board, especially against a chip leader who has been consistently aggressive. Minni's range for a small button open and a continuation bet is wide, but it includes many hands that currently beat me or have significant equity against me (any Jack, any 6, or any pocket pair). Without a solid draw or a piece of the board, I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities in the final hands of the tournament.

With Hand #148 of 150 and playing heads-up, Jc 8s is a respectable hand to open from the button (Small Blind). I have a significant stack disadvantage compared to Minni, and with only three hands remaining in the tournament, I need to be proactive in trying to chip up. Raising here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or see a flop in a controlled manner.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I hold 6♠9♣ in the big blind; to call is 200 more (bringing my total committed to 300). Heads-up the button’s opening range will be wide, so my hand has reasonable equity and the pot odds (~33%) justify defending. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by making a small 3‑bet; I also have a large chip lead and don’t need to gamble. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot controllable, and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression unless I pair or pick up a strong draw, and be ready to apply pressure if I improve.

I have top pair (6s) with a weak 9 kicker on a very dry 6♣7♦2♠ board. Flash opened wide from the button heads‑up, so his range contains lots of overcards and bluffs but also the occasional 6x/7x/22 that already beat me. Out of position I want to control the pot — a protection bet isn’t necessary on this dry board and risks getting check‑raised by stronger pairs. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs/cbets, and call reasonable bets so I can reevaluate on the turn (folding to extreme aggression given my weak kicker). With a big chip lead and only a few hands left, low‑variance pot control is the best line.

On a dry, disconnected flop of 6-7-2, I have two overcards to the board. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. A bet of half the pot (300) puts pressure on Minni's non-pairing hands and can often win the pot immediately. Given the tournament is nearing its end and I am at a significant chip disadvantage, I need to maintain aggression to pick up these smaller pots.

I have top pair (6s) with a weak 9 kicker on a very dry 6♣7♦2♠ board. Flash’s small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot) heads‑up is consistent with a wide range of overcards and frequent bluffs as well as some paired hands. Out of position and with a marginal kicker I don’t want to bloat the pot by raising — that invites a check‑raise or turns marginal pots into big ones. The pot odds are attractive to call (300 to win ~1200, ~25% equity needed) and my hand has reasonable showdown equity vs a wide c-betting range. With a large chip lead and only a few hands left, low‑variance pot control is the right line: call, see the turn, then fold to clear strong action or extract value on safe cards.

I have top pair (6♠) but a very weak 9 kicker on a dry 6♣7♦2♠ board with a J♦ on the turn. Flash checked the turn, which looks like either missed overcards, pot‑control with medium Jx, or weakness. Out of position and with a big chip lead and only a couple hands left, I want a low‑variance line — betting here mainly gains thin value and can build a pot that’s uncomfortable versus Jx/sets that already beat me. I’ll check to keep the pot small, see a river for cheap, and fold to clear heavy aggression if necessary.

On the turn, I have improved to top pair with Jacks. The board is [6c 7d 2s Jd]. Minni has checked to me twice now, once on the flop and once on the turn. My hand is likely the best at the moment, but it is vulnerable to various straight draws (89, 58) and now a diamond flush draw. Given that there are only three hands remaining in the tournament and I have a significant chip deficit, I need to extract value from my made hands. A bet of 750 into a pot of 1200 (62.5% pot) is a standard value bet that can be called by middle pairs (7x, 6x) or draws, while also protecting my hand against those same draws.

Call — I have top pair (6♠) with a weak 9 kicker on 6♣7♦2♠ J♦. Flash’s line (small flop c‑bet then a larger turn bet of 750) is consistent heads‑up with a wide, somewhat polarized range: Jx/7x/2x value hands plus plenty of bluffs/overcards. Calling 750 to win the 1950 pot means I need ~27.8% equity (750 / (1950+750) ≈ 27.8%), which my hand has versus a continuing wide range. I’m out of position but have a large chip lead and only a few hands left, so I prefer the lower‑variance line — calling keeps the pot controllable and allows me to fold to clear, heavy river aggression. If the river is a blank I’ll either check‑call a reasonable bet or make a small thin‑value bet; if the river brings a Jack/paired board and Flash applies big pressure (large bet/shove), I’ll give up unless I improve. Raising here would bloat the pot and risk uncomfortable all‑ins; folding is too tight with top pair and favorable pot odds. Call to see the river and re‑evaluate.

I have top pair (6♠) with a very weak 9 kicker on 6♣7♦2♠ J♦ T♥. Flash opened wide preflop and has c-bet/continued (300 on the flop, 750 on the turn), a line that combines both straightforward value (Jx, 7x, 6x/sets) and bluffs. The Ten on the river is a fairly dangerous card — it gives him Tx two-pair possibilities and completes some straight combos — and he checked the river, which looks like pot control or weakness. I’m out of position with a marginal kicker and a big chip lead late in the tournament, so I prefer the low-variance line: check to keep the pot manageable and show down. If he bets a small/medium size I’ll call for thin value; I’ll fold to clear, large river shoves from a line that strongly represents two pair/sets. Checking minimizes risk and preserves stack while giving me the best chance to realize my equity.

I have top pair with a mediocre kicker. After betting the flop and turn and being called both times, the river brings a Ten which completes a possible straight (8-9). Minni has checked to me for a third time. While my hand is likely the best, betting for value is risky as Minni might only call with a better Jack or a straight, and she might fold her marginal made hands like 7x or 6x. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and take down a healthy pot without risking a check-raise on the final street of action.

Heads-up on the button with A♦4♣ — in this spot Ace-x is a clear open-raise. I’ve already posted the SB (50) and facing the BB, calling 50 is cheap but gives the BB good odds and initiative; raising puts pressure, uses my Ace blocker and takes advantage of position postflop. Standard table sizing here is around 3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300 total. That size gives solid fold equity, keeps the pot manageable versus a ~100bb effective stack, and lets me play a lot of postflop hands in position. If Flash 3-bets a large amount I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold A4o vs a big 3-bet), but if he calls I’ll proceed to use positional advantage and board texture to extract value or bluff as appropriate.

With only two hands remaining in the tournament and holding a very weak hand like Jack-Trey offsuit out of position, it is best to fold. Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively from the button. Defending with such a weak hand is likely to lead to further chip loss, and at this stage, I should wait for a better opportunity in the final hand to maximize my stack.

I am on the button with a weak hand (2s 6c) in the final hand of the tournament. The opponent (Minni) has a significant chip lead. There is no strategic value in playing a weak hand out of position post-flop when I am so far behind in the chip count at the end of the session. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my remaining stack and conclude the tournament.
